11. 11. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore
Classification table8
SS vs NMS comparison12
Encephalopathy types10
Peripheral neuropathy table10
Seizure threshold drugs6
Viva answers12
Total58

Target: ≥46/58 (80%)

12. 12. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Question (10 marks): "Differentiate Serotonin Syndrome from Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome. Outline management of each."

  1. Comparison Table (4): Mechanism, Onset, Mental status, Motor, Autonomic, Reflexes, CK, Key drugs, Antidotes, Supportive
  2. Serotonin Syndrome Management (3): Stop all serotonergic drugs, Cyproheptadine 12mg → 2mg 2-hourly, Benzodiazepines, Cooling, IV fluids, ICU if severe
  3. NMS Management (3): Stop offending drug, Dantrolene 1–2.5mg/kg IV, Bromocriptine 2.5–7.5mg/day, Cooling, IV fluids, ICU, avoid depolarising muscle relaxants

2. Short Question (5 marks): "Hunter Criteria for Serotonin Syndrome"

  • Spontaneous clonus OR
  • Inducible clonus + agitation/sweating OR
  • Ocular clonus + agitation/sweating OR
  • Tremor + hyperreflexia OR
  • Hypertonia + Temp >38°C + ocular/inducible clonus

3. Viva (2 min): "Patient on sertraline 100mg, started linezolid for MRSA. 24h later: agitated, clonus, hyperreflexia, temp 39°C, tachycardia. Diagnosis? Management?"

  • Serotonin Syndrome (SSRI + Linezolid = MAO inhibition)
  • Stop sertraline AND linezolid immediately
  • Cyproheptadine 12mg stat PO/NG, then 2mg 2-hourly PRN
  • IV lorazepam 1–2mg for agitation
  • Cooling, IV fluids, cardiac monitoring
  • ICU if severe (hyperthermia >41°C, DIC, rhabdo)

4. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on valproate 1500mg/day, confused, drowsy. LFTs normal. Ammonia 180 (normal <50). Diagnosis? Treatment?"

  • Valproate hyperammonaemic encephalopathy
  • Stop valproate
  • L-carnitine 100mg/kg IV
  • Switch to alternative AED (levetiracetam, lamotrigine)
  • Monitor ammonia, consciousness

5. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • SS = 5-HT2A excess → Clonus, Hyperreflexia, Fever → Cyproheptadine
  • NMS = D2 blockade → Rigidity, Bradykinesia, CK↑↑ → Dantrolene, Bromocriptine
  • Valproate encephalopathy = Normal LFTs, ↑ Ammonia → L-carnitine 100mg/kg
  • PRES = Tacro/Ciclo/Siro → Headache, visual loss, seizures → Stop drug, BP control
  • INH neuropathy = Pyridoxine (B6) prophylaxis
  • Vincristine = Foot drop + Ileus (autonomic)
  • Lithium toxicity >1.5 = toxic, >2.5 = dialysis
  • Phenytoin cerebellar = Nystagmus, ataxia (level >20)
  • BZD withdrawal seizure = restart + slow taper
  • Metoclopramide dystonia = IV Procyclidine/Benztropine

13. 13. 📚 Summary Card

NEURO EMERGENCY TRIAD:
1. SEROTONIN SYNDROME → Clonus + Hyperreflexia + Fever → Cyproheptadine 12mg stat
2. NMS → Lead-pipe Rigidity + Bradykinesia + CK↑↑ → Dantrolene 1–2.5mg/kg IV
3. VALPROATE ENCEPHALOPATHY → Confusion + Normal LFTs + ↑ Ammonia → L-carnitine 100mg/kg IV
PRES = Tacro/Ciclo/Siro → Visual loss + Seizures → Stop drug, BP control
INH Neuropathy = Pyridoxine (B6) prophylaxis
Lithium Toxicity >2.5 = Dialysis

14. 14. ❓ MCQs (15)

  1. Serotonin Syndrome — key diagnostic feature per Hunter Criteria:

A. Lead-pipe rigidity

B. Clonus (spontaneous/inducible/ocular) ✓

C. CK >10,000

D. Bradykinesia

E. Hyporeflexia

  1. NMS — characteristic motor finding:

A. Clonus

B. Hyperreflexia

C. Lead-pipe rigidity with bradykinesia ✓

D. Myoclonus

E. Tremor

  1. Cyproheptadine dose for Serotonin Syndrome:

A. 4mg stat

B. 8mg stat

C. 12mg stat, then 2mg 2-hourly ✓

D. 16mg stat

E. 2mg stat

  1. Dantrolene mechanism in NMS:

A. 5-HT2A antagonism

B. D2 agonism

C. Ryanodine receptor antagonism → inhibits Ca²⁺ release from sarcoplasmic reticulum ✓

D. GABA agonism

E. Dopamine depletion

  1. Valproate hyperammonaemic encephalopathy — key lab finding:

A. ↑ ALT/AST

B. ↑ Ammonia with normal LFTs ✓

C. ↓ Sodium

D. ↑ CK

E. ↑ Lactate

  1. L-carnitine dose for valproate encephalopathy:

A. 10mg/kg

B. 50mg/kg

C. 100mg/kg IV ✓

D. 200mg/kg

E. 500mg/kg

  1. PRES — typical MRI finding:

A. Frontal lobe restricted diffusion

B. Parietal-occipital vasogenic oedema (T2/FLAIR hyperintensity) ✓

C. Basal ganglia haemorrhage

D. Cortical restricted diffusion

E. Cerebellar atrophy

  1. Drugs causing PRES:

A. Beta-blockers, ACEi

B. Tacrolimus, Ciclosporin, Sirolimus, VEGF inhibitors, EPO ✓

C. SSRIs, SNRIs

D. Statins, Fibrates

E. PPIs, H2 blockers

  1. Isoniazid neuropathy prevention:

A. Folic acid

B. Pyridoxine (B6) 10–50mg/day ✓

C. Vitamin B12

D. Thiamine

E. L-carnitine

  1. Vincristine neuropathy — characteristic autonomic feature:

A. Urinary retention

B. Ileus ✓

C. Orthostatic hypotension

D. Erectile dysfunction

E. Anhidrosis

  1. Lithium toxicity — dialysis threshold:

A. >1.0 mmol/L

B. >1.5 mmol/L

C. >2.5 mmol/L (or severe toxicity at any level) ✓

D. >3.0 mmol/L

E. >4.0 mmol/L

  1. Phenytoin cerebellar toxicity — features:

A. Tremor, rigidity

B. Nystagmus, ataxia, dysarthria ✓

C. Choreoathetosis

D. Myoclonus

E. Dystonia

  1. Metoclopramide acute dystonia — treatment:

A. IV Haloperidol

B. IV Procyclidine 5mg / Benztropine 1–2mg ✓

C. IV Lorazepam

D. IV Phenytoin

E. IV Levodopa

  1. Drug-induced seizure threshold lowering — highest risk antidepressant:

A. Sertraline

B. Bupropion ✓

C. Citalopram

D. Mirtazapine

E. Venlafaxine

  1. Ifosfamide encephalopathy — specific antidote:

A. L-carnitine

B. Methylene blue ✓

C. N-acetylcysteine

D. Pyridoxine

E. Flumazenil

15. 15. 🃏 Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
SS key featureClonus (spontaneous/inducible/ocular)
NMS key featureLead-pipe rigidity + bradykinesia
SS vs NMS onsetSS = rapid (hours); NMS = slow (days-weeks)
SS vs NMS reflexesSS = hyperreflexia/clonus; NMS = normal/reduced
SS vs NMS CKSS = normal/mild ↑; NMS = markedly ↑
Cyproheptadine dose12mg stat → 2mg 2-hourly PRN (5-HT2A antagonist)
Dantrolene dose1–2.5mg/kg IV (ryanodine receptor antagonist)
Bromocriptine dose2.5–7.5mg/day PO/NG (D2 agonist)
Valproate encephalopathyNormal LFTs + ↑ ammonia → L-carnitine 100mg/kg IV
PRES drugsTacrolimus, Ciclosporin, Sirolimus, VEGF inhibitors, EPO
PRES MRIParietal-occipital vasogenic oedema
INH neuropathy prophylaxisPyridoxine (B6) 10–50mg/day
Vincristine neuropathyFoot drop + Ileus (autonomic)
Lithium toxicity dialysis>2.5 mmol/L or severe
Phenytoin cerebellarNystagmus, ataxia, dysarthria (level >20)
Metoclopramide dystoniaIV Procyclidine 5mg / Benztropine 1–2mg
Bupropion seizure riskHighest among antidepressants
Theophylline seizureLevel >20 mg/L
Ifosfamide encephalopathyMethylene blue
Linezolid neuropathy>14 days use → sensory axonal + optic

16. 16. ✅ Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — Clonus is the hallmark Hunter criterion
  2. C — Lead-pipe rigidity with bradykinesia
  3. C — 12mg stat, then 2mg 2-hourly
  4. C — Ryanodine receptor antagonism
  5. B — ↑ Ammonia with normal LFTs
  6. C — 100mg/kg IV
  7. B — Parietal-occipital vasogenic oedema
  8. B — Tacrolimus, Ciclosporin, Sirolimus, VEGF inhibitors, EPO
  9. B — Pyridoxine (B6) 10–50mg/day
  10. B — Ileus (autonomic)
  11. C — >2.5 mmol/L or severe
  12. B — Nystagmus, ataxia, dysarthria
  13. B — IV Procyclidine/Benztropine (anticholinergic)
  14. B — Bupropion highest seizure risk among antidepressants
  15. B — Methylene blue for ifosfamide

File: /mnt/tb/Medicine/Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing/ADRs/Neurological ADRs.md | Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.1.13 Renal Adrs

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Adverse Drug Reactions → System-Specific Patterns | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH (AKI classification, crystal nephropathy, AIN, dialysis dosing)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify drug-induced kidney injury by mechanism (AKI, AIN, Crystal nephropathy, ATN, Tubular toxicity)
  • [ ] Apply KDIGO AKI staging
  • [ ] Identify high-risk drugs for each renal lesion
  • [ ] Execute management: drug cessation, renal replacement, rechallenge avoidance
  • [ ] Know dosing adjustments for renal impairment (covered in Special Populations)

2. 2. 📊 Classification of Renal ADRs

Lesion TypeMechanismTypical DrugsKey Features
Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN)Type IV hypersensitivity (T-cell mediated)NSAIDs, Antibiotics (β-lactams, sulfonamides, rifampicin), PPIs, Diuretics (thiazide, loop), Allopurinol, ICD drugsSubacute (days–weeks), fever, rash, eosinophilia (blood/urine), sterile pyuria, ↑ Cr
Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN)Direct tubular toxicity / ischaemiaAminoglycosides, Amphotericin B, Contrast, Cisplatin, Vancomycin (high trough), Tenofovir, Lithium↑ Cr, muddy brown casts, FENa >2%, non-oliguric often
Crystal NephropathyIntraluminal crystal precipitationAcyclovir, Indinavir, Atazanavir, Sulfonamides, Methotrexate (high-dose), Triamterene, CiprofloxacinFlank pain, haematuria, crystals on US/microscopy, ↑ Cr
Glomerular DiseaseImmune complex / podocyte injuryGold, Penicillamine, NSAIDs (MCD), Interferon-α, Heparin (HIT), Cancer immunotherapy (ICI)Proteinuria (nephrotic/nephritic), haematuria, ↑ Cr
Thrombotic Microangiopathy (TMA)Endothelial injuryCiclosporin, Tacrolimus, Sirolimus, Clopidogrel, Ticlopidine, Quinine, Gemcitabine, VEGF inhibitorsMAHA (schistocytes), thrombocytopenia, ↑ Cr, neurological
Electrolyte / TubularSpecific tubular dysfunctionLithium (NDI), Amphotericin B (RTA type 1), Ifosfamide (Fanconi), Tenofovir (Fanconi), Cisplatin (Mg²⁺/K⁺ wasting)Polyuria, electrolyte abnormalities, ↑ Cr

3. 3. 🔬 Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN) — Most Common Drug-Induced Renal Lesion

1. Clinical Presentation

FeatureTypical
OnsetDays to weeks (NSAIDs: weeks–months)
SymptomsFever (30–50%), rash (25–50%), arthralgia
Labs↑ Cr, eosinophilia (blood 30%, urine 80%), sterile pyuria, WBC casts
UrineLow-grade proteinuria (<1g/d), haematuria
BiopsyInterstitial oedema, inflammatory infiltrate (eosinophils, lymphocytes, plasma cells), tubulitis

2. High-Risk Drugs for AIN

Drug ClassSpecific AgentsNotes
NSAIDsAll (esp. fenoprofen, ibuprofen)Most common cause; delayed onset (weeks–months); often no fever/rash/eosinophilia
Antibioticsβ-lactams (penicillins, cephalosporins), Sulfonamides, Rifampicin, QuinolonesClassic triad (fever, rash, eosinophilia) more common
PPIsOmeprazole, Esomeprazole, LansoprazoleIncreasing recognition; often insidious
DiureticsThiazides, Loop, TriamtereneOften with other features
AllopurinolAllopurinolPart of DRESS/hypersensitivity
ICI (Immunotherapy)Pembrolizumab, Nivolumab, IpilimumabImmune checkpoint inhibitor nephritis — distinct entity, high-dose steroids

3. Management

  1. STOP offending drug immediately
  2. Supportive: hydration, avoid nephrotoxins, correct electrolytes
  3. Corticosteroids: Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day (0.5–1mg/kg) × 2–4 weeks → taper 4–8 weeks
  • Best if started early (<7–10 days from Cr rise)
  • NSAID-AIN: less responsive to steroids
  1. Renal replacement if indicated (KDIGO criteria)
  2. Follow-up: Monitor Cr for 6–12 months (risk of CKD)

4. 4. 💊 Aminoglycoside Nephrotoxicity (ATN)

FeatureDetails
MechanismProximal tubular uptake → lysosomal phospholipidosis → mitochondrial dysfunction
Risk FactorsExtended-interval dosing (↓ risk), age>60, pre-existing CKD, dehydration, loop diuretics, amphotericin, vancomycin, contrast
PresentationNon-oliguric ↑ Cr (day 5–10), mdrd casts, FENa >2%, tubular proteinuria (β2-microglobulin, NAG)
MonitoringExtended-interval (Hartford): single daily dose; trough <1 mg/L at 18h post-dose
ManagementStop/hold aminoglycoside; hydration; avoid other nephrotoxins; usually reversible
TDMTarget: Peak 14–20 mg/L (traditional); Trough <1 mg/L (extended-interval)

5. 5. 💎 Crystal Nephropathy

DrugCrystalsPresentationPrevention
AcyclovirNeedle-like (urine pH dependent)AKI, flank pain, crystalluriaHydration (IV 1.5–2L/day), adjust for renal, avoid rapid infusion
Indinavir / AtazanavirRectangular / star-shapedFlank pain, haematuria, obstructive uropathyHydration (2–3L/day); atazanavir less nephrotoxic
SulfonamidesSheaf-of-wheatCrystalluria, obstructionHydration, alkalinisation (historical)
Methotrexate (HD)MTX + 7-OH-MTXAKI, tubular precipitationLeucovorin rescue, hydration, urine alkalinisation (pH>7), monitor levels
CiprofloxacinNeedle-likeRare crystalluriaHydration

6. 6. 🩸 Thrombotic Microangiopathy (TMA)

DrugContextFeatures
Ciclosporin / Tacrolimus / SirolimusTransplantMAHA, thrombocytopenia, ↑ Cr, neurological; dose-dependent; ↓ dose/stop
Clopidogrel / TiclopidineAntiplateletRare; onset days–weeks; stop drug → plasma exchange
Quinine / QuinineMalaria/leg crampsImmune-mediated; stop immediately
Gemcitabine / VEGF inhibitorsChemotherapyDose-dependent; may need drug cessation
HIT (Heparin)Anticoagulation4Ts score; ↓ platelets >50%; stop heparin, argatroban/bivalirudin

7. 7. 📋 KDIGO AKI Staging (for Drug-Induced AKI)

StageSerum CreatinineUrine Output
11.5–1.9× baseline OR ≥26.5 μmol/L increase<0.5 mL/kg/h for 6–12h
22.0–2.9× baseline<0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥12h
3≥3.0× baseline OR ≥354 μmol/L OR RRT OR <0.3 mL/kg/h for ≥24h / anuria ≥12h

8. 8. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Clinical PictureMost Likely Renal ADRKey DrugAction
65yo on ibuprofen 2mo, ↑ Cr, eosinophilia, sterile pyuria, no fever/rashNSAID-induced AINNSAIDs (most common)Stop NSAID; steroids if no recovery 1wk
30yo on piperacillin-tazobactam 2wk, fever, rash, eosinophilia, ↑ CrAntibiotic-induced AINβ-lactamsStop abx; steroids
70yo on gentamicin 7d, ↑ Cr, muddy brown casts, FENa >2%Aminoglycoside ATNGentamicinHold gentamicin; hydration; TDM
40yo on acyclovir IV 3d, flank pain, crystalluria, ↑ CrAcyclovir crystal nephropathyAcyclovirHydration; alkalinise; stop if severe
Transplant on tacrolimus, Hb↓, platelets↓, schistocytes, ↑ Cr, confusionTacrolimus TMATacrolimus/Ciclosporin↓ dose/stop; plasma exchange if severe
50yo on lithium 10y, polyuria, hypernatremia, ↑ CrLithium NDILithiumWater access; consider switch; amiloride

9. 9. ❓ Viva Questions (12)

QAnswer
1. Classify drug-induced renal lesions.AIN (Type IV hypersensitivity), ATN (direct tubular toxicity), Crystal nephropathy, Glomerular (immune complex), TMA (endothelial injury), Tubular/electrolyte (specific transporters)
2. Most common cause of drug-induced AIN?NSAIDs (but often insidious, no classic triad)
3. Classic AIN triad vs NSAID-AIN?Classic: fever, rash, eosinophilia (β-lactams, sulfonamides); NSAID-AIN: often no fever/rash/eosinophilia, delayed onset, older patients
4. PPI-induced AIN — features?Increasingly recognised; often insidious, subacute; biopsy shows eosinophilic infiltrate; steroids effective
5. Aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity — risk factors, monitoring?Risk: age>60, CKD, dehydration, loop diuretics, vancomycin, amphotericin; Monitoring: extended-interval dosing, trough <1 mg/L at 18h
6. Acyclovir nephrotoxicity — mechanism, prevention?Crystal precipitation in tubules (pH dependent); Prevention: IV hydration 1.5–2L/day, adjust dose for renal, infuse over 1h
7. Indinavir/Atazanavir crystal nephropathy — presentation, prevention?Flank pain, haematuria, obstruction; Hydration 2–3L/day
8. TMA — drug causes, management?Ciclosporin, tacrolimus, sirolimus (dose-dependent); clopidogrel/ticlopidine (immune); quinine; gemcitabine; Management: stop/reduce drug, plasma exchange if immune
9. Lithium nephrotoxicity — types, management?NDI (polyuria, hypernatremia) — most common; Chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis (↑ Cr); Management: water access, amiloride 5–10mg BD, consider switch
10. Contrast-induced nephropathy — definition, prevention?↑ Cr ≥26.5 μmol/L or 1.5× baseline within 48h of contrast; Prevention: hydration (IV 0.9% NaCl), NAC (controversial), iso-osmolar contrast, ↓ volume, hold nephrotoxins
11. Vancomycin nephrotoxicity — risk factors, TDM target?Risk: trough >15–20 mg/L, concomitant nephrotoxins (aminoglycosides, piperacillin-tazobactam), age>65, CKD; Target AUC 400–600 mg·h/L (trough 10–20 old)
12. KDIGO AKI Stage 2 criteria?Cr 2.0–2.9× baseline OR UO <0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥12h

10. 10. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
AIN vs ATNAIN = immune (eosinophilia, WBC casts, sterile pyuria, fever/rash); ATN = toxic/ischaemic (muddy brown casts, FENa>2%, no eosinophilia)
NSAID-AIN vs Antibiotic-AINNSAID = insidious, no triad, older; Antibiotic = classic triad, younger
Crystal nephropathy vs AINCrystal = flank pain, crystalluria, obstructive; AIN = allergic features, eosinophilia
TMA vs HUS/TTPDrug-induced TMA = stop drug first; TTP = ADAMTS13 deficiency → plasma exchange; HUS = STEC/shiga toxin
Lithium NDI vs DINDI = renal resistance to ADH (lithium, amphotericin); DI = central ADH deficiency

Mnemonics:

  • "AIN DRUGS" = Antibiotics (β-lactams, sulfa, rifampicin), Ibuprofen/NSAIDs, Nexium/PPIs, Diuretics, Rifampicin, Uric acid (allopurinol), Gold, Sulfonamides
  • "ATN TOXINS" = Aminoglycosides, Tenofovir, Nephrotoxins (Ampho B, Cisplatin, Contrast, Vancomycin)
  • "CRYSTALS" = Ciprofloxacin, Rifampicin (rare), Y (Acyclovir), Sulfonamides, Triamterene, Atazanavir/Indinavir, Lithium (not crystal), S (Methotrexate HD)
  • "TMA DRUGS" = Tacrolimus/Ciclosporin/Sirolimus, Mitomycin/Gemcitabine/VEGF, Antiplatelet (Clopidogrel/Ticlopidine), Drug (Quinine), Rare, Uncommon, Gemcitabine, Stop drug
  • "LITHIUM" = Lithium → Nephrogenic DI (NDI) → Polyuria, Hypernatremia → Amiloride

11. 11. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

Renal ADRs
AIN (Type IV)
NSAIDs (most common, insidious)
Antibiotics (β-lactams, sulfa, rifampicin - classic triad)
PPIs (increasing)
Diuretics
Allopurinol
ICI (checkpoint inhibitors)
Rx: STOP drug, Steroids 1mg/kg 2-4wk taper
ATN
Aminoglycosides (proximal tubular phospholipidosis)
Amphotericin B
Contrast
Cisplatin
Vancomycin (high trough)
Tenofovir
Lithium (chronic interstitial)
Rx: Stop, Hydration, TDM
Crystal Nephropathy
Acyclovir (hydration, alkalinisation)
Indinavir/Atazanavir (hydration 2-3L)
Sulfonamides
Methotrexate HD (leucovorin, alkalinisation)
Ciprofloxacin
Glomerular
NSAIDs (MCD)
Gold/Penicillamine (MN)
Interferon (FSGS)
Heparin (HIT)
ICI
TMA
CNIs (Tacro/Ciclo/Siro) - dose dependent
Clopidogrel/Ticlopidine (immune)
Quinine
Chemo (Gemcitabine, VEGF)
Rx: Stop/reduce, PLEX if immune
Tubular/Electrolyte
Lithium (NDI + chronic interstitial)
Amphotericin (RTA type 1)
Tenofovir (Fanconi)
Cisplatin (Mg/K wasting)
KDIGO AKI Staging
Stage 1: 1.5-1.9x Cr or +26.5
Stage 2: 2.0-2.9x Cr
Stage 3: 3.0x Cr or RRT

12. 12. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScoreNext
11d
23d
31w
42w
51m
63m

13. 13. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore
Classification table10
AIN details12
ATN/Aminoglycosides8
Crystal nephropathy8
TMA6
KDIGO staging6
Viva answers12
Total62

Target: ≥50/62 (80%)

14. 14. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Question (10 marks): "Classify drug-induced kidney injury. Discuss AIN in detail."

  1. Classification (2): 6 categories (AIN, ATN, Crystal, Glomerular, TMA, Tubular) with mechanisms
  2. AIN (4): Pathophysiology (Type IV), drugs (NSAIDs #1, antibiotics, PPIs), clinical features (classic triad vs NSAID-AIN), biopsy, management (stop drug, steroids 1mg/kg)
  3. Other key lesions (3): ATN (aminoglycosides, TDM), Crystal (acylovir hydration), TMA (CNIs, clopidogrel)
  4. AKI Staging (1): KDIGO 3 stages

2. Short Question (5 marks): "NSAID-induced AIN vs Antibiotic-induced AIN"

FeatureNSAID-AINAntibiotic-AIN
OnsetWeeks–monthsDays–weeks
Classic triadOften absentPresent (fever, rash, eosinophilia)
AgeOlderAny
EosinophiliaOften absentCommon
BiopsySimilarSimilar
Steroid responseLess responsiveMore responsive

3. Viva (2 min): "70yo on ibuprofen 3 months for OA, Cr 180 (baseline 90), no fever/rash, urine eosinophils+. Diagnosis? Management?"

  • NSAID-induced AIN (insidious, no classic triad)
  • STOP ibuprofen immediately
  • Hydration, avoid nephrotoxins
  • Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day × 2–4 weeks → taper 4–8 weeks
  • Monitor Cr weekly ×4, then monthly ×6–12mo
  • Switch to paracetamol/topical NSAID for OA

4. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on IV acyclovir for HSV encephalitis, day 3: flank pain, crystalluria, Cr rising. Action?"

  • Acyclovir crystal nephropathy
  • Aggressive IV hydration (2–3L/day), urine alkalinisation (target pH>7), slow infusion
  • Adjust dose for renal function
  • Stop if severe AKI

5. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • AIN = Type IV hypersensitivity; NSAIDs #1 cause (insidious); antibiotics = classic triad
  • AIN management: Stop drug → Steroids 1mg/kg × 2–4wk → taper
  • ATN: Aminoglycosides, Ampho B, Contrast, Cisplatin, Vancomycin (trough>15), Tenofovir
  • Crystal nephropathy: Acyclovir (hydration), Indinavir/Atazanavir (hydration), MTX HD (alkalinisation + leucovorin)
  • TMA: CNIs (dose-dependent), Clopidogrel (immune → PLEX)
  • Lithium = NDI (polyuria, hypernatremia) + chronic interstitial → amiloride
  • KDIGO: Stage 1 (1.5× Cr), Stage 2 (2×), Stage 3 (3×/RRT)

15. 15. 📚 Summary Card

RENAL ADR TRIAD:
1. AIN — NSAIDs #1 (insidious); Abx = classic triad; Steroids 1mg/kg
2. ATN — Aminoglycosides (trough<1), Ampho B, Contrast, Cisplatin, Vancomycin (AUC 400–600)
3. CRYSTAL — Acyclovir/Indinavir = HYDRATION; MTX HD = ALKALINISATION + LEUCOVORIN
LITHIUM = NDI → Amiloride
TMA = CNIs (↓ dose); Clopidogrel (PLEX)
KDIGO = 1.5× / 2× / 3× Cr

16. 16. ❓ MCQs (15)

  1. Most common cause of drug-induced AIN:

A. Penicillins

B. NSAIDs ✓

C. Sulfonamides

D. PPIs

E. Diuretics

  1. Classic AIN triad (fever, rash, eosinophilia) is MOST typical of:

A. NSAID-induced AIN

B. Antibiotic-induced AIN (β-lactams, sulfonamides) ✓

C. PPI-induced AIN

D. Diuretic-induced AIN

E. Allopurinol-induced AIN

  1. Aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity — preferred dosing to reduce risk:

A. Traditional multiple daily dosing

B. Extended-interval (once daily) dosing ✓

C. Continuous infusion

D. No difference

E. Avoid in all elderly

  1. Extended-interval aminoglycoside TDM target:

A. Peak 14–20 mg/L

B. Trough <1 mg/L at 18h post-dose ✓

C. Trough 5–10 mg/L

D. AUC 400–600

E. Random level 2–4 mg/L

  1. Acyclovir nephrotoxicity — mechanism:

A. Direct tubular toxicity

B. Crystal precipitation in tubules (pH dependent) ✓

C. Immune-mediated AIN

D. Crystal-induced ATN

E. Glomerulonephritis

  1. Acyclovir crystal nephropathy prevention:

A. Urine acidification

B. IV hydration 1.5–2L/day, adjust for renal, infuse over 1h ✓

C. Loop diuretic co-administration

D. Sodium bicarbonate only

E. No prevention possible

  1. Tacrolimus/ciclosporin associated TMA — mechanism:

A. Immune-mediated (ADAMTS13 deficiency)

B. Dose-dependent endothelial injury ✓

C. Crystal precipitation

D. Complement activation

E. Antiplatelet antibody

  1. Clopidogrel-induced TMA — management:

A. Reduce dose

B. Stop clopidogrel + Plasma exchange ✓

C. Continue with steroids

D. Switch to ticagrelor

E. IVIG only

  1. Lithium nephrotoxicity — most common manifestation:

A. Acute tubular necrosis

B. Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (NDI) ✓

C. Acute interstitial nephritis

D. Glomerulonephritis

E. Crystal nephropathy

  1. Lithium-induced NDI management:

A. Desmopressin

B. Amiloride 5–10mg BD ✓

C. Loop diuretic

D. Thiazide diuretic

E. Fluid restriction

  1. Vancomycin nephrotoxicity — current TDM target:

A. Trough 10–20 mg/L

B. AUC 400–600 mg·h/L (AUC/MIC) ✓

C. Peak 20–40 mg/L

D. Trough 5–10 mg/L

E. Random level

  1. Contrast-induced nephropathy definition (KDIGO):

A. Cr increase >50% within 24h

B. Cr ≥26.5 μmol/L or 1.5× baseline within 48h ✓

C. Cr doubling within 72h

D. Need for RRT within 1 week

E. Any Cr rise after contrast

  1. KDIGO AKI Stage 3 — urine output criterion:

A. <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6h

B. <0.5 mL/kg/h for 12h

C. <0.3 mL/kg/h for ≥24h OR anuria ≥12h ✓

D. <1 mL/kg/h for 24h

E. <0.1 mL/kg/h for 6h

  1. Methotrexate high-dose nephrotoxicity prevention:

A. Urine acidification

B. Hydration + Urine alkalinisation (pH>7) + Leucovorin rescue ✓

C. Loop diuretic

D. NSAID co-administration

E. Hydration only

  1. Drug-induced glomerular disease — NSAIDs classically cause:

A. Membranous nephropathy

B. Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis

C. Minimal change disease (MCD) ✓

D. IgA nephropathy

E. Crescentic GN

17. 17. 🃏 Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
AIN most common causeNSAIDs (insidious, no triad)
Classic AIN triadFever, rash, eosinophilia (β-lactams, sulfonamides)
NSAID-AIN featuresInsidious, older, no triad, delayed onset, less steroid responsive
AIN managementSTOP drug → Pred 1mg/kg 2-4wk → taper 4-8wk
Aminoglycoside dosingExtended-interval (once daily) preferred
Aminoglycoside TDMTrough <1 mg/L at 18h (Hartford)
ATN drugsAminoglycosides, Ampho B, Contrast, Cisplatin, Vancomycin (AUC), Tenofovir
Acyclovir nephrotoxicityCrystal precipitation (pH dependent)
Acyclovir preventionIV hydration 1.5-2L/day, renal adjust, infuse 1h
Indinavir/AtazanavirHydration 2-3L/day
MTX HD nephrotoxicityHydration + alkalinisation (pH>7) + leucovorin
TMA drugsCNIs (dose-dependent), Clopidogrel/Ticlopidine (immune → PLEX), Quinine
Lithium NDIPolyuria, hypernatremia → Amiloride 5-10mg BD
Lithium chronicTubulointerstitial nephritis → ↑ Cr
Vancomycin TDMAUC 400-600 mg·h/L (AUC/MIC)
Contrast nephropathyCr ≥26.5 or 1.5× baseline within 48h
KDIGO Stage 11.5-1.9× Cr or +26.5
KDIGO Stage 22.0-2.9× Cr
KDIGO Stage 33.0× Cr or RRT or UO <0.3 for 24h/anuria 12h
NSAID glomerularMinimal change disease (MCD)

18. 18. ✅ Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — NSAIDs most common AIN cause
  2. B — Classic triad with antibiotics
  3. B — Extended-interval (once daily) ↓ nephrotoxicity
  4. B — Trough <1 mg/L at 18h
  5. B — Crystal precipitation
  6. B — Hydration 1.5–2L/day
  7. B — CNI TMA = dose-dependent endothelial injury
  8. B — Stop clopidogrel + PLEX
  9. B — Lithium = NDI most common
  10. B — Amiloride for NDI
  11. B — AUC 400–600 (current standard)
  12. B — Cr ≥26.5 or 1.5× in 48h
  13. C — UO <0.3 for 24h or anuria 12h
  14. B — Hydration + alkalinisation + leucovorin
  15. C — NSAIDs → MCD

File: /mnt/tb/Medicine/Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing/ADRs/Renal ADRs.md | Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.1.14 Reporting And Pharmacovigilance
[!info]
Disease-Level Topic under ADRs → Reporting and Pharmacovigilance.
Davidson 24e Ch2 — "Adverse Drug Reactions" (Maxwell SRJ).

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Define pharmacovigilance (PV) and its purpose
  • [ ] Describe the Yellow Card Scheme (UK MHRA)
  • [ ] Identify drugs on the Black Triangle (▼) list
  • [ ] Differentiate spontaneous reports from clinical trial data
  • [ ] Explain Phase IV surveillance and post-marketing studies
  • [ ] List WHO Pharmacovigilance Programme structure
  • [ ] Discuss signal detection, PRR/ROR, PSUR/PBRER, RMP
  • [ ] Recognise the role of healthcare professionals in ADR reporting

2. 2. Definition & Purpose

TermDefinition
PharmacovigilanceScience and activities relating to detection, assessment, understanding, and prevention of adverse effects or any other drug-related problems (WHO)
Adverse Drug Reaction (ADR)Noxious, unintended response at normal doses (WHO 1972)
Serious ADRFatal, life-threatening, hospitalising, disabling, congenital anomaly, or other medically significant
Unexpected ADRReaction not consistent with SmPC (Summary of Product Characteristics)
SignalReported information on a possible causal relationship between an ADR and a drug, not previously known or incompletely documented

Purpose:

  • Identify previously unknown ADRs (especially rare, delayed)
  • Quantify risk (frequency, severity, populations)
  • Update prescribing information (SmPC, PIL)
  • Trigger regulatory action (warnings, restrictions, withdrawal)
  • Support risk-benefit re-assessment

3. 3. Mermaid Algorithm — Pharmacovigilance Workflow

⬇ Clinical Pathway
ADR occurs in patient
↓
Healthcare professional identifies
↓
YELLOW CARD to MHRA or national PV centre
↓
National PV database VigiBase - WHO Uppsala
↓
Signal detection PRR, ROR, EBCPV
↓
Confirmed signal?
↓
Continued monitoring
Regulatory action
↓
SmPC update
DHPC letter
Black box warning
Drug withdrawal / restriction
PSUR / PBRER
↓
Prescribers informed
↓
Updated guidelines / NICE

4. 4. Comparison Tables

1. 4.1 Yellow Card Scheme (UK MHRA)

FeatureDetail
Established1964 (UK); 1st globally
TriggerThalidomide disaster (1956-62)
Who reportsDoctors, pharmacists, nurses, patients, CARs (Coroners)
HowOnline, paper, app
What to reportSerious ADR; new/unexpected ADR; ADR to Black Triangle drug; ADR in child; congenital anomaly; ADR to herbal/OTC
ConfidentialityPatient identifier removed before MHRA
CausalityNot required — MHRA assesses
TimeWithin 15 days for serious; ASAP

2. 4.2 Black Triangle (▼) Scheme

FeatureDetail
Symbol▼ (inverted black triangle)
IndicationNewly licensed drug (typically <5 yr) OR new formulation/route OR new indication
PurposeIntensive post-marketing surveillance — ALL ADRs reportable (not just serious)
ListUpdated monthly on BNF/MHRA
Examples (recent)▼ sacubitril-valsartan, ▼ SGLT2 inhibitors (early), ▼ CAR-T therapies, ▼ mRNA COVID vaccines, ▼ bispecific antibodies
DurationUsually 5 yr, but may be extended if safety concerns

3. 4.3 Pharmacovigilance Tools & Methods

ToolDescription
Spontaneous reportsVoluntary (Yellow Card) — main source of signal
Prescription Event Monitoring (PEM)UK Drug Safety Research Unit — patients identified from Rx
Intensive MonitoringHospital-based active surveillance
RegistriesDisease-based or drug-based (e.g., TNF inhibitor registry)
Sentinel SitesDesignated hospitals for active reporting
PSUR / PBRERPeriodic Safety Update Report (now PBRER — Periodic Benefit-Risk Evaluation Report) — submitted by MAH
RMPRisk Management Plan — proactive risk minimisation (now part of licence)
PASSPost-Authorisation Safety Studies
PAESPost-Authorisation Efficacy Studies
DHPCDirect Healthcare Professional Communication — urgent safety info

4. 4.4 Signal Detection — Quantitative Methods

MeasureFormulaInterpretation
PRR (Proportional Reporting Ratio)(a/b) / (c/d) where a=reports drug+ADR, b=reports drug only, c=reports ADR only, d=reports neitherPRR ≥2 = signal; ≥3 = strong signal
ROR (Reporting Odds Ratio)(a × d) / (b × c)ROR ≥2 = signal
IC (Information Component, WHO-UMC)Bayesian shrinkage estimateIC >0 = signal (positive)
EBGM (Empirical Bayes Geometric Mean, FDA)BayesianEBGM ≥2 = signal

5. 4.5 Pharmacovigilance — National & International

LevelBodyRole
GlobalWHO Programme for International Drug Monitoring (Uppsala Monitoring Centre, UMC)Maintains VigiBase; IC analysis; signal sharing
EUEMA (European Medicines Agency)PRAC (Pharmacovigilance Risk Assessment Committee) — PSUR assessment, RMP
UKMHRA + CHM (Commission on Human Medicines)Yellow Card; regulatory action
USFDA (MedWatch) + FAERSFAERS database; Sentinel System
JapanPMDAJapan PV system

6. 4.6 Causality Assessment in PV

LevelDescription
Individual caseNaranjo or WHO-UMC
Aggregate (signal)PRR/ROR/IC in large database
ConfirmatoryRCT, observational study, registry, case-control

5. 5. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

PearlDetail
Yellow Card established1964 (1st globally)
Trigger for UK PVThalidomide (phocomelia, 1956-62)
Threshold for strong signal (PRR)≥2
Black Triangle duration~5 yr post-licensing
Reportable on Black TriangleALL ADRs (not just serious)
Yellow Card is forAll serious, new, or Black Triangle drug ADRs
Reporting ADR in childAlways report (rare events, off-label)
Pregnancy ADRAlways report (congenital anomalies)
PBRER (PSUR)Submitted by MAH at intervals; assesses benefit-risk
RMPRisk Management Plan; required for all new drugs
DHPCDirect Healthcare Professional Communication — urgent safety alert
VigiBaseWHO global ADR database (Uppsala)
FAERSFDA Adverse Event Reporting System (US)
Drug withdrawal example (signal)Rofecoxib (Vioxx) 2004 — CV events; Rosiglitazone 2010 — MI
Spontaneous reporting limitationUnder-reporting (estimated <10%); bias; no denominator
When to report ADRAny serious, new/unexpected, paediatric, congenital, or Black Triangle

6. 6. Viva Questions (10)

  1. Define pharmacovigilance.

The science and activities relating to the detection, assessment, understanding, and prevention of adverse effects or any other drug-related problems (WHO).

  1. What was the trigger for modern pharmacovigilance?

Thalidomide disaster (1956-62) — caused severe congenital anomalies (phocomelia) in >10,000 babies worldwide. Led to UK Yellow Card (1964) and Kefauver-Harris Amendment (US, 1962).

  1. What is the Yellow Card Scheme? Who reports?

UK MHRA's spontaneous ADR reporting system (1964). Healthcare professionals (doctors, pharmacists, nurses) and patients can report.

  1. What should be reported via Yellow Card?

All serious ADRs; new/unexpected ADRs; ADRs to Black Triangle drugs (▼); ADRs in children; congenital anomalies; ADR to herbal/OTC products.

  1. What is the Black Triangle (▼) symbol?

Inverted black triangle indicating a newly licensed drug (<5 yr) under intensive surveillance. ALL ADRs must be reported.

  1. What is a "signal" in pharmacovigilance?

Reported information on a possible causal relationship between an ADR and a drug, not previously known or incompletely documented. Detected via disproportionality analysis (PRR, ROR, IC).

  1. What is PRR? Threshold for a signal?

Proportional Reporting Ratio = (a/b)/(c/d) in spontaneous report database. PRR ≥2 = possible signal; ≥3 = strong signal.

  1. What is PBRER?

Periodic Benefit-Risk Evaluation Report (formerly PSUR) — submitted by Marketing Authorisation Holder at defined intervals, summarising global safety data and benefit-risk assessment.

  1. What is a Risk Management Plan (RMP)?

Document required for all new drug licences; describes identified risks, potential risks, missing information, and risk minimisation activities (e.g., Pregnancy Prevention Programme for thalidomide, isotretinoin, valproate).

  1. What are the limitations of spontaneous reporting?

Under-reporting (estimated <10% of serious ADRs); no denominator (cannot calculate incidence); reporter bias; duplicate reports; no comparator; confounded by polypharmacy. Must be complemented by active surveillance, registries, and analytical studies.

7. 7. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionResolution
Yellow Card vs Black TriangleYellow = report ADR; Black Triangle = newly licensed, ALL ADRs reportable
PRR vs RORPRR = proportional reporting; ROR = odds ratio equivalent. Both used in PV.
ADR vs ADEADR = causally related; ADE = any untoward event
Signal vs known ADRSignal = hypothesis; known ADR = confirmed in SmPC
PSUR vs PBRERPSUR = older term; PBRER = new (2012) — assesses benefit-risk, not just safety
RMP vs REMSRMP = EU; REMS = US Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy
PBRER vs DSURPBRER = post-marketing; DSUR = development (annual safety report during trials)
Passive vs active surveillancePassive = spontaneous (Yellow Card); Active = sentinel, registries, PEM
VigiBase vs FAERSVigiBase = WHO global; FAERS = US only
Under-reporting reasonTime, fear of blame, unsure of causality, "well-known" ADR
Phase IV vs PVPhase IV = post-marketing trials; PV = broader (spontaneous + studies)
Spontaneous vs stimulated reportingSpontaneous = voluntary (Yellow Card); Stimulated = e.g., PEM

Mnemonic — Yellow Card: "Yellow = Yellow card reports all You need (serious, new, paediatric, congenital, Black Triangle)"

Mnemonic — Black Triangle (▼): "Triangle = Trace Thoroughly" (all ADRs in newly licensed drugs)

Mnemonic — Signal threshold: "PRR ≥2, ROR ≥2, IC >0, EBGM ≥2" (proportional analysis)

Mnemonic — Reportable ADRs: "SSNPP" (Serious, Suspected new, Neonate, Pregnancy, Paediatric, Plus Black Triangle)

Mnemonic — PV hierarchy: "Global (WHO) > Regional (EMA) > National (MHRA) > Local (Trust)"

8. 8. Mermaid Mind Map

Pharmacovigilance

9. 9. Spaced Repetition Tracker

TopicDay 1Day 3Day 7Day 14Day 30

10. 10. Self-Test Scorecard

DomainScore (0-5)
Yellow Card/5
Black Triangle/5
Signal detection/5
PRR/ROR/5
PBRER/RMP/5
PV limitations/5
TOTAL/30

11. 11. MCQs (10)

  1. The Yellow Card Scheme was established in the UK in:

A. 1944

B. 1964 ✓

C. 1984

D. 2004

E. 2014

  1. The trigger for modern pharmacovigilance was:

A. Aspirin-induced GI bleed

B. Thalidomide disaster ✓

C. Acetaminophen hepatotoxicity

D. Rofecoxib CV events

E. Pandemrix narcolepsy

  1. The Black Triangle (▼) symbol indicates:

A. A dangerous drug

B. A newly licensed drug requiring intensive monitoring ✓

C. A CD

D. A withdrawn drug

E. An off-label drug

  1. A signal in pharmacovigilance refers to:

A. A confirmed ADR

B. A possible causal relationship not previously known ✓

C. An SmPC update

D. A drug withdrawal

E. A serious ADR

  1. PRR threshold for a signal is:

A. ≥1

B. ≥2 ✓

C. ≥5

D. ≥10

E. ≥20

  1. PBRER is submitted by:

A. Doctors

B. Pharmacists

C. Marketing Authorisation Holder (MAH) ✓

D. Patients

E. Nurses

  1. Which ADR should be reported via Yellow Card?

A. Well-known side effect

B. Serious, new, or Black Triangle drug ADR ✓

C. Non-serious

D. Common ADR in BNF

E. Old drug reactions

  1. VigiBase is maintained by:

A. FDA

B. EMA

C. WHO Uppsala Monitoring Centre ✓

D. MHRA

E. NHS

  1. Rofecoxib was withdrawn in 2004 due to:

A. Hepatotoxicity

B. Cardiovascular events ✓

C. Renal failure

D. SJS

E. Agranulocytosis

  1. Limitations of spontaneous reporting include:

A. Over-reporting

B. Has denominator

C. Under-reporting, no denominator, bias ✓

D. Strict causality required

E. Population-based

12. 12. SBAs (5)

  1. A GP prescribes a newly licensed diabetes drug and the patient develops DKA within 2 weeks. The BEST action is:
  • A) Stop and switch, no report
  • B) Report via Yellow Card — Black Triangle drug, ALL ADRs ✓
  • C) Wait for second case
  • D) Report to manufacturer only
  • E) Continue and observe
  1. A hospital pharmacist identifies an unusual cluster of pancreatitis cases with a gliptin. The next step is:
  • A) Yellow Card for each
  • B) Submit aggregated signal to MHRA / CHM ✓
  • C) Wait for EMA
  • D) Publish only
  • E) Local review only
  1. An MAH submits an annual PBRER for a 10-year-old drug. The most important component is:
  • A) Sales figures
  • B) Benefit-risk re-evaluation with cumulative safety data ✓
  • C) Marketing plan
  • D) Manufacturing data
  • E) Patent status
  1. A patient on ▼ drug develops mild headache. Should it be reported?
  • A) No — too mild
  • B) Yes — all ADRs in Black Triangle drugs must be reported ✓
  • C) Only if serious
  • D) Only if hospitalised
  • E) Only if new
  1. Thalidomide is now used for multiple myeloma. The risk-minimisation tool is:
  • A) Black Triangle
  • B) Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP) with iPLEDGE-style REMS ✓
  • C) Yellow Card
  • D) PBRER
  • E) DHPC

13. 13. Answer Key

1. MCQ Answers

  1. B (1964 — first global PV system)
  2. B (Thalidomide)
  3. B (Newly licensed = intensive monitoring)
  4. B (Possible causal relationship, new)
  5. B (PRR ≥2)
  6. C (MAH submits PBRER)
  7. B (Serious, new, Black Triangle)
  8. C (WHO Uppsala)
  9. B (Vioxx — rofecoxib CV events)
  10. C (Under-reporting, no denominator, bias)

2. SBA Answers

  1. B — Black Triangle drug: report ALL ADRs (mild to serious).
  2. B — Aggregated signal to MHRA/CHM (cluster suggests possible new ADR).
  3. B — PBRER = Periodic Benefit-Risk Evaluation Report.
  4. B — Black Triangle: all ADRs reportable, even mild.
  5. B — Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP) — strict contraception, monthly pregnancy tests, iPLEDGE-style programme.

14. 14. Summary Box

Pharmacovigilance = detection, assessment, understanding, prevention of ADRs. Yellow Card (UK, 1964) is the global template; thalidomide triggered it. Black Triangle (▼) = newly licensed, ALL ADRs reportable. Signal detection uses PRR ≥2 / ROR ≥2 / IC >0. PBRER (formerly PSUR) and RMP are regulatory tools. Spontaneous reporting has limitations: under-reporting (<10%), no denominator, bias. Examples: rofecoxib (CV), rosiglitazone (MI), valproate (PPP).

15. 15. Cross-Links

  • Heading Hub: [[ADRs]]

Last Updated: 2026-06-15

Status: FULLY COMPLETE with Exam Suite (Viva 10, MCQ 10, SBA 5, Answer Key, Confusions, Mind Map, Spaced Repetition, Self-Test, Exam Modes)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.1.15 Type A Augmented

Status: draft | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Adverse Drug Reactions | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH (Mechanism-based, dose-predictable, common in exams)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Define Type A ADR: dose-dependent, predictable from pharmacology
  • [ ] Distinguish Type A vs Type B (idiosyncratic) — classic exam differentiation
  • [ ] List major mechanisms: excessive therapeutic effect, unwanted pharmacological effect, secondary pharmacological effect
  • [ ] Identify high-risk drugs with narrow therapeutic index (NTI)
  • [ ] Apply monitoring strategies to prevent Type A ADRs

2. 2. 📖 Definition & Classification

FeatureType A (Augmented)Type B (Bizarre)
MechanismExaggerated pharmacological effectIdiosyncratic, non-pharmacological
Dose-dependenceYes — ↑ dose = ↑ riskNo — unpredictable
PredictabilityPredictable from known pharmacologyUnpredictable
IncidenceCommon (high)Rare
MortalityLow (usually reversible)High (unpredictable severity)
ExamplesWarfarin bleed, insulin hypoglycaemiaAnaphylaxis, SJS/TEN, DRESS
ManagementDose reduction, monitoring, antagonistDrug withdrawal, avoid rechallenge

3. 3. 🧬 Mechanisms of Type A ADRs

1. 1. Excessive Therapeutic Effect (On-target, excessive)

DrugTherapeutic EffectType A ADR (Excessive)
WarfarinAnticoagulationHaemorrhage (INR >4)
Insulin / Sulfonylureas↓ Blood glucoseHypoglycaemia
Antihypertensives↓ BPSymptomatic hypotension, falls
DiureticsNatriuresisHyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, dehydration
Beta-blockers↓ HR, ↓ contractilityBradycardia, heart block, HF exacerbation
OpioidsAnalgesiaRespiratory depression, sedation
Digoxin↑ ContractilityToxicity: nausea, arrhythmias, visual changes

2. 2. Unwanted Pharmacological Effect (Off-target, predictable)

DrugPrimary TargetUnwanted Effect (Off-target)
NSAIDsCOX-2 (inflammation)COX-1 → Gastric ulcer, renal vasoconstriction, platelet inhibition
Tricyclics (Amitriptyline)NA/5-HT reuptakeAnticholinergic: dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, confusion
First-gen antihistaminesH1 receptorCNS H1 → Sedation; Anticholinergic; Alpha-1 → Hypotension
Beta-agonists (Salbutamol)Beta-2 (bronchodilation)Beta-1 → Tachycardia; Beta-2 → Tremor, Hypokalaemia
ACE inhibitorsACE → ↓ Ang IIBradykinin accumulation → Dry cough, Angioedema
Calcium channel blockersVascular smooth muscleReflex tachycardia (dihydropyridines); Constipation; Ankle oedema

3. 3. Secondary Pharmacological Effect (Downstream consequence)

DrugPrimary EffectSecondary Effect → ADR
CorticosteroidsAnti-inflammatoryHyperglycaemia, osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, Cushing's
Diuretics (Loop/Thiazide)NatriuresisHypokalaemia → Arrhythmias, Digoxin toxicity; Hyponatraemia; Gout
Antibiotics (Broad-spectrum)BactericidalC. difficile colitis; Resistance; Superinfection (Candida)
BisphosphonatesOsteoclast inhibitionOesophagitis; Atypical femoral fracture; ONJ

4. 4. ⚠️ High-Risk Drugs for Type A ADRs (Narrow Therapeutic Index)

DrugMonitoring ParameterTarget RangeADR if Exceeded
WarfarinINR2.0–3.0 (most) / 2.5–3.5 (mechanical mitral)Major haemorrhage
DigoxinSerum level0.5–0.9 ng/mL (HF) / 0.8–2.0 (AF)Nausea, arrhythmias, visual (yellow halos)
LithiumSerum level0.6–1.0 mmol/L (maintenance)Tremor, NDI, renal, neurotoxicity (>1.5)
PhenytoinTotal level10–20 µg/mL (free 1–2)Nystagmus, ataxia, gingival hyperplasia
GentamicinTrough (extended interval)<1 mg/L (Hartford)Nephrotoxicity, Ototoxicity
VancomycinAUC₀₋₂₄/MIC400–600Nephrotoxicity, Ototoxicity
TheophyllineSerum level10–20 mg/LSeizures, arrhythmias, nausea
Ciclosporin / TacrolimusTrough (C0)Drug/indication specificNephrotoxicity, HTN, neurotox, hyperK
Methotrexate (high-dose)Serum level (24h, 48h)<1 µmol/L (24h)Myelosuppression, mucositis, hepatotoxicity

5. 5. 🛡️ Prevention Strategies (Exam Essentials)

StrategyApplication
Start low, go slowElderly, renal/hepatic impairment, polypharmacy
Therapeutic Drug Monitoring (TDM)NTI drugs: warfarin, digoxin, lithium, phenytoin, aminoglycosides, vancomycin, immunosuppressants
Renal dose adjustmentCockcroft-Gault CrCl for: DOACs, metformin, aminoglycosides, gabapentin, allopurinol, antibiotics
Hepatic dose adjustmentChild-Pugh for: warfarin, phenytoin, theophylline, morphine, statins
Drug interaction screeningCYP450, P-gp, renal transporters — esp. initiators/inhibitors
Patient educationHypoglycaemia symptoms, INR monitoring, fall risk, adrenal insufficiency (steroids)
DeprescribingReview indication, time-to-benefit, ACB, DBI — STOPP/START

6. 6. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

ScenarioType A ADRMechanism
Warfarin + antibiotics → INR 8, haematuriaHaemorrhageExcessive therapeutic effect (↑ anticoagulation)
Elderly on furosemide 80mg → falls, confusion, Na⁺ 125HyponatraemiaUnwanted effect (impaired water excretion)
Amitriptyline 50mg → dry mouth, constipation, urinary retentionAnticholinergic syndromeOff-target (muscarinic blockade)
Salbutamol neb → tremor, tachycardia, K⁺ 2.8Beta-2 stimulationOff-target (beta-1/2)
ACEi → persistent dry coughBradykinin accumulationSecondary pharmacological effect
Prednisolone 30mg long-term → hyperglycaemia, osteoporosisMetabolic, boneSecondary pharmacological effect

7. 7. ❓ Viva Questions (8)

QAnswer
1. Define Type A ADR. Give 3 examples.Dose-dependent, predictable from pharmacology. Examples: warfarin bleed, insulin hypoglycaemia, NSAID gastric ulcer
2. How does Type A differ from Type B?Type A: dose-dependent, predictable, common, low mortality. Type B: idiosyncratic, unpredictable, rare, high mortality
3. Patient on warfarin, INR 6.5, no bleeding. Management?Omit 1–2 doses; vitamin K 1–2mg PO if INR >8 or risk factors; restart at lower dose when INR therapeutic
4. Elderly patient on amitriptyline develops confusion, dry mouth, constipation. ADR type? Mechanism?Type A — anticholinergic (off-target muscarinic blockade)
5. Why are NSAIDs high-risk for Type A ADRs in elderly?COX-1 inhibition → ↓ gastric PG → ulcer; ↓ renal PG → AKI; antiplatelet → bleed; ↑ CV risk
6. What monitoring prevents Type A ADRs with digoxin?Serum level (0.5–0.9 ng/mL), K⁺, Mg²⁺, renal function, ECG — hypokalaemia ↑ toxicity
7. Patient on ACEi develops angioedema. Mechanism? Management?Bradykinin accumulation (ACE degrades bradykinin). Stop ACEi; avoid ARB (30% cross-reactivity); use alternative
8. How does "Start low, go slow" prevent Type A ADRs?Allows homeostatic adaptation; identifies susceptibility; reduces peak concentrations; crucial in elderly/renal/hepatic impairment

8. 8. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Type A vs B dose-dependenceType A = dose-dependent; Type B = dose-independent (but may need minimum dose to trigger)
Type A = always mild?No — warfarin bleed, digoxin toxicity, insulin hypoglycaemia can be fatal
On-target vs off-targetOn-target = excessive therapeutic (warfarin bleed); Off-target = unwanted pharmacology (amitriptyline anticholinergic)
Secondary effect = Type C?No — secondary pharmacological effect is still Type A (predictable cascade). Type C = chronic (time-dependent, e.g., steroid osteoporosis)

Mnemonics:

  • "A = Augmented = Dose-Dependent = Predictable" — A for Augmented, Anticipated, A lot (common)
  • "Dose makes the poison" — Type A
  • "B = Bizarre = Idiosyncratic" — Type B

9. 9. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

Type A ADR
Characteristics

Predictable
Common

Mechanisms
Excessive therapeutic effect
– Warfarin bleed
– Insulin hypo
– Digoxin toxicity

– TCA anticholinergic

Digoxin (level, K, Mg)
Lithium (level, renal)
Phenytoin (level, free)
Aminoglycosides (trough)
Vancomycin (AUC)
Immunosuppressants (trough)
Prevention
Start low, go slow
TDM
Renal/hepatic adjust

10. 10. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScoreNext

11. 11. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore
Definition & comparison Type A vs B6
Mechanisms (3 categories + examples)12
NTI drugs & monitoring10
Prevention strategies6
Viva answers8
Total42

Target: ≥34/42 (80%)

12. 12. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Question (10 marks): "Classify ADRs with examples. Discuss Type A ADRs in detail."

  1. Classification (2): Rawlins/Thompson → Type A (Augmented), Type B (Bizarre), Type C (Chronic), D (Delayed), E (End of use), F (Failure) — or DoTS
  2. Type A Definition (2): Dose-dependent, predictable, common, low mortality
  3. Three Mechanisms (3): Excessive therapeutic, Unwanted pharmacological, Secondary pharmacological — with 2 examples each
  4. High-risk NTI drugs (2): Table of drug, monitoring, target, ADR
  5. Prevention (1): Start low/go slow, TDM, dose adjust, interaction screen, deprescribing

2. Short Question (5 marks): "Type A vs Type B ADR"

  • Table: Mechanism, Dose-dependence, Predictability, Incidence, Mortality, Examples, Management

3. Viva (2 min): "80yo on warfarin, INR 5.8, no bleed. Why Type A? Manage."

  • Type A: Excessive anticoagulation (dose-dependent, predictable)
  • Manage: Omit 1–2 doses; Vit K 1mg PO if high bleed risk/elderly; Restart lower dose; Review interacting drugs

4. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on furosemide 80mg, K⁺ 2.9. Type A or B?"

  • Type A — Unwanted pharmacological effect (renal K⁺ wasting). Action: K⁺ replacement, ↓ dose, add K⁺-sparing, review indication.

5. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Type A = Augmented = Dose-dependent = Predictable = Common
  • Type B = Bizarre = Idiosyncratic = Unpredictable = Rare = High mortality
  • NTI drugs: Warfarin, Digoxin, Lithium, Phenytoin, Gentamicin, Vancomycin, Tacrolimus, Theophylline
  • Mechanisms: 1° Excessive therapeutic 2° Off-target 3° Secondary cascade

13. 13. 📚 Summary Card

TYPE A ADR TRIAD:
1. Excessive therapeutic — Warfarin bleed, Insulin hypo, Digoxin toxicity
2. Unwanted pharmacological — NSAID GI/renal, TCA anticholinergic, ACEi cough, Beta-agonist tremor
3. Secondary pharmacological — Steroid metabolic/bone, Diuretic electrolytes, Abx C.diff
PREVENTION: Start low, go slow + TDM (NTI) + Renal/hepatic adjust + Interaction screen + Deprescribe

14. 14. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Which characteristic defines a Type A (Augmented) ADR?

A. Idiosyncratic, unpredictable

B. Dose-dependent, predictable from pharmacology ✓

C. Occurs after long-term use only

D. Immunologically mediated

E. Genetic predisposition required

  1. Warfarin-induced haemorrhage with INR >4 is an example of which Type A mechanism?

A. Unwanted pharmacological effect

B. Excessive therapeutic effect ✓

C. Secondary pharmacological effect

D. Idiosyncratic reaction

E. Drug interaction effect

  1. Amitriptyline causing dry mouth, constipation, and urinary retention represents:

A. Type B ADR

B. Type A: Unwanted pharmacological effect (anticholinergic) ✓

C. Excessive therapeutic effect

D. Secondary pharmacological effect

E. Drug interaction

  1. ACE inhibitor-induced dry cough is mediated by:

A. Excessive therapeutic effect (oversuppression of Ang II)

B. Bradykinin accumulation (secondary pharmacological effect) ✓

C. Unwanted pharmacological effect (off-target receptor)

D. Direct bronchial irritation

E. Type B idiosyncratic reaction

  1. Which drug does NOT have a narrow therapeutic index requiring TDM?

A. Digoxin

B. Lithium

C. Phenytoin

D. Amoxicillin ✓

E. Warfarin

  1. An elderly patient on furosemide 80 mg daily develops hyponatraemia (Na⁺ 125 mmol/L). This is a Type A ADR via which mechanism?

A. Excessive therapeutic effect

B. Unwanted pharmacological effect (impaired water excretion) ✓

C. Secondary pharmacological effect

D. Type B idiosyncratic

E. Drug interaction

  1. Salbutamol-induced tremor and hypokalaemia are due to:

A. Excessive beta-2 bronchodilation

B. Unwanted pharmacological effect (beta-2 stimulation in muscle, beta-1 in heart) ✓

C. Secondary effect of hypoxia correction

D. Type B hypersensitivity

E. Drug interaction with steroids

  1. Corticosteroid-induced osteoporosis is classified as:

A. Type A: Excessive therapeutic effect

B. Type A: Unwanted pharmacological effect

C. Type A: Secondary pharmacological effect (metabolic/bone) ✓

D. Type C: Chronic ADR only

E. Type B: Idiosyncratic

  1. Key prevention strategy for Type A ADRs in elderly polypharmacy patients:

A. Avoid all medications

B. Start low, go slow; regular review; deprescribing ✓

C. Use only Type B drugs

D. Increase doses gradually to tolerance

E. Routine genetic testing

  1. Patient on digoxin 125 mcg develops nausea, yellow vision, and bradycardia. Serum K⁺ 3.1. Most likely explanation:

A. Type B ADR

B. Type A ADR: Digoxin toxicity precipitated by hypokalaemia ✓

C. Drug interaction with ACEi

D. Secondary pharmacological effect

E. Idiopathic intolerance

15. 15. 🃏 Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
Type A ADR definitionDose-dependent, predictable from pharmacology, common, usually reversible
Type B ADR definitionIdiosyncratic, non-dose-dependent, unpredictable, rare, high mortality
Type A mechanism 1Excessive therapeutic effect — Warfarin bleed, Insulin hypo, Digoxin toxicity
Type A mechanism 2Unwanted pharmacological (off-target) — NSAID COX-1 GI/renal, TCA anticholinergic, ACEi cough, Beta-agonist tremor
Type A mechanism 3Secondary pharmacological — Steroid metabolic/bone, Diuretic electrolytes, Abx C.diff
NTI drugs requiring TDMWarfarin (INR), Digoxin (level/K/Mg), Lithium (level/renal), Phenytoin (level), Gentamicin (trough), Vancomycin (AUC), Tacrolimus/Ciclosporin (trough), Theophylline (level)
ACEi cough mechanismBradykinin accumulation (ACE degrades bradykinin)
NSAID GI/renal mechanismCOX-1 inhibition → ↓ prostaglandins → ↓ gastric mucus/HCO₃⁻, ↓ renal vasodilation
TCA anticholinergic SEsDry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, confusion, blurred vision, tachycardia

16. 16. ✅ Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — Type A = dose-dependent, predictable
  2. B — Warfarin bleed = excessive anticoagulation (therapeutic effect)
  3. B — TCA anticholinergic = unwanted off-target muscarinic blockade
  4. B — Bradykinin accumulation = secondary pharmacological effect
  5. D — Amoxicillin wide TI, no TDM
  6. B — Furosemide hyponatraemia = unwanted effect (impaired dilution)
  7. B — Salbutamol tremor/hypoK = beta-2 off-target (muscle, K⁺ shift)
  8. C — Steroid osteoporosis = secondary metabolic effect (chronological but pharmacologically mediated)
  9. B — Start low/go slow + review + deprescribe
  10. B — Digoxin toxicity + hypokalaemia = Type A (predictable, dose/exposure dependent)

File: /mnt/tb/Medicine/Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing/ADRs/Type A (Augmented).md | Status: draft → upgrade to full-fcps-mrcp-note after review

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.1.16 Type B Bizarre

Status: draft | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Adverse Drug Reactions | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH (Idiosyncratic, high mortality, VIVA favourites — SJS/TEN, DRESS, AHS, Torsades)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Define Type B ADR: idiosyncratic, non-dose-dependent, unpredictable
  • [ ] Classify Type B by mechanism: immunological (hypersensitivity I–IV), non-immunological idiosyncrasy
  • [ ] Recognise high-mortality syndromes: SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP, AHS, Torsades, Anaphylaxis
  • [ ] Apply causality assessment (Naranjo, WHO-UMC, RUCAM) to Type B reactions
  • [ ] Know absolute contraindication to rechallenge

2. 2. 📖 Definition & Core Features

FeatureType B (Bizarre)
SynonymIdiosyncratic
Dose-dependenceNo (may need threshold but not linear)
PredictabilityUnpredictable from pharmacology
IncidenceRare (1:1,000 – 1:100,000)
MortalityHigh (can be fatal)
MechanismImmunological (hypersensitivity) or non-immunological idiosyncrasy
RechallengeAbsolutely contraindicated
Genetic predispositionOften (HLA alleles, enzyme polymorphisms)

3. 3. 🧬 Classification by Mechanism

1. A. Immunological (Hypersensitivity Reactions — Gell & Coombs)

TypeMechanismMediatorsOnsetClassic Drug ExamplesClinical Syndrome
Type I (Immediate)IgE-mediated mast cell/basophil degranulationHistamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, PGD₂Minutes–hoursPenicillins, cephalosporins, NSAIDs, muscle relaxants, insulin, latexAnaphylaxis, urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, anaphylactoid
Type II (Cytotoxic)IgG/IgM + complement → cell lysisComplement, ADCCDays–weeksPenicillins, cephalosporins (haemolytic anaemia); Methyldopa (AIHA); Quinidine (thrombocytopenia); Heparin (HIT)Immune haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia
Type III (Immune Complex)IgG/IgM-antigen complexes → vasculitisComplement, neutrophils1–3 weeksPenicillins, sulfonamides, allopurinol, biologics (infliximab), vaccinesSerum sickness, vasculitis, glomerulonephritis, drug fever
Type IV (Delayed T-cell)T-cell mediated (CD4+/CD8+)Cytokines (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-5)Days–weeksAllopurinol, sulfonamides, antiepileptics, abacavir, dapsone, NSAIDsMorbilliform, SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP, AHS, maculopapular exanthema (MPE)
Subtypes of Type IV (PHIL):
- IVa (CD4+ Th1): Maculopapular, DRESS
- IVb (CD4+ Th2): Eosinophilic (DRESS, AHS)
- IVc (CD8+ cytotoxic): SJS/TEN, AGEP, MPE — granulysin, perforin, Fas-FasL
- IVd (T-cell → neutrophil): AGEP — IL-8, CXCL8

2. B. Non-Immunological Idiosyncrasy

MechanismExampleSyndrome
Enzyme deficiency (pharmacogenetic)G6PD deficiency + oxidative drugs (primaquine, dapsone, sulfonamides)Haemolytic anaemia
CYP2D6 poor metaboliser + codeineNo analgesia
CYP2D6 ultra-rapid + codeineToxic morphine levels → apnoea
NAT2 slow acetylator + isoniazidPeripheral neuropathy, hepatotoxicity
TPMT deficiency + azathioprine/6-MPMyelosuppression
Mitochondrial toxicityValproate, linezolid, NRTIs (zidovudine)Hepatic steatosis, lactic acidosis, neuropathy
ChannelopathyQT-prolonging drugs in congenital LQTSTorsades de Pointes
Idiosyncratic hepatotoxicityIsoniazid, valproate, diclofenac, amoxicillin-clavulanateDILI (Hy's Law)

4. 4. ⚠️ High-Mortality Type B Syndromes (Must Know for Exams)

1. 1. Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (SJS/TEN)

FeatureSJSTENOverlap (SJS/TEN)
BSA detachment<10%>30%10–30%
Mucosal sites≥2≥2≥2
Typical drugsAllopurinol, sulfonamides, AEDs (CZP, PHT, LMG, OXC), NSAIDs, nevirapineSameSame
HLA associationsB15:02 (CZP/PHT/OXC), B58:01 (Allopurinol), A*31:01 (CZP DRESS)
Mortality~5–10%~30–50%~15–25%
SCORTEN7 factors: age>40, HR>120, cancer, BSA>10%, BUN>28, Glc>14, HCO₃<20

SCORTEN Mortality Prediction:

ScoreMortality
0–13%
212%
335%
458%
590%+

Management: Stop culprit immediately → ICU/burn unit → supportive (fluids, electrolytes, wound care, infection prevention) → IVIG (controversial), cyclosporine, etanercept (emerging) → NO systemic steroids (controversial, ↑ sepsis risk)

2. 2. DRESS (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms)

FeatureDetails
TriadFever + Rash + Organ involvement
Typical onset2–8 weeks after drug start (delayed)
Key drugsAllopurinol, AEDs (CZP, PHT, PB, LMG, VPA), sulfonamides, dapsone, abacavir, minocycline, allopurinol
HLAB58:01 (Allopurinol), B15:02 (CZP), A31:01 (CZP), B13:01 (Dapsone)
RegiSCAR criteriaFever ≥38°C, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia >1.5×10⁹/L or atypical lymphs, organ involvement (liver, kidney, lung, heart), rash, reaction >2wks
Mortality~10% (liver failure, myocarditis)
ManagementStop drug → systemic steroids (pred 1mg/kg) → slow taper 6–12wks → monitor LFTs, renal, cardiac

3. 3. AGEP (Acute Generalised Exanthematous Pustulosis)

FeatureDetails
PresentationAcute febrile + non-follicular pustules on oedematous erythema
Onset1–5 days (rapid)
Key drugsBeta-lactams (esp. aminopenicillins), quinolones, hydroxychloroquine, diltiazem, sulfonamides
HistologySubcorneal pustules, neutrophilic infiltration
BiomarkerNeutrophilia (not eosinophilia)
CourseSelf-limiting <2 weeks after withdrawal
MortalityLow (<5%)

4. 4. AHS (Anticonvulsant Hypersensitivity Syndrome) / DRESS by AEDs

FeatureDetails
DrugsPhenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital, lamotrigine, valproate
TriadFever, rash, systemic involvement (hepatitis, nephritis, pneumonitis)
Onset1–8 weeks
HLAHLA-B15:02 (CZP), HLA-A31:01 (CZP DRESS), HLA-B*38:01 (dapsone/CZP)
Cross-reactivityHigh among aromatic AEDs (CZP, PHT, PB, OXC) — avoid all if reaction to one
ManagementStop all aromatic AEDs → switch to non-aromatic (levetiracetam, topiramate, gabapentin, lacosamide, zonisamide)

5. 5. Torsades de Pointes (Drug-Induced)

FeatureDetails
MechanismhERG (IKr) blockade → prolonged repolarisation → afterdepolarisations → R-on-T
Risk factorsFemale, elderly, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, bradycardia, congenital LQTS, polypharmacy (multiple QT drugs)
High-risk drugsAntiarrhythmics (Class Ia, III), antipsychotics (haloperidol, thioridazine, ziprasidone), antidepressants (citalopram, escitalopram, TCAs), antibiotics (macrolides, fluoroquinolones), antiemetics (ondansetron, domperidone), antifungals (azole), methadone
MonitoringBaseline ECG, repeat if risk factors; QTc >500ms or Δ>60ms = high risk
Acute managementIV Magnesium sulfate 2g bolus (even if normal Mg²⁺) → correct K⁺/Mg²⁺ → pacing/isoprenaline if paused → stop offending drug

6. 6. Anaphylaxis (Type I)

FeatureDetails
CriteriaAcute onset + skin/mucosal + respiratory compromise OR hypotension OR GI symptoms
Common drugsPenicillins, cephalosporins, NSAIDs, neuromuscular blockers, radiocontrast, insulin, biologics
ManagementIM Adrenaline 1:1000 (0.5mg = 0.5mL) anterolateral thigh → supine + legs raised → O₂ → IV fluids → chlorphenamine + hydrocortisone (adjuncts) → observe 6–12h
TryptasePeak 1–2h, half-life 2h — sample at 1–2h and 24h (baseline)

7. 7. DILI (Drug-Induced Liver Injury) — Idiosyncratic (Type B)

PatternDrugsFeatures
Hepatocellular (ALT>ALP)Isoniazid, valproate, paracetamol (overdose = Type A), nevirapine, flucloxacillinHy's Law: ALT>3×ULN + Bilirubin>2×ULN → 10–50% mortality
Cholestatic (ALP>ALT)Flucloxacillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, azathioprine, chlorpromazine, anabolic steroidsPruritus, jaundice, prolonged
MixedDiclofenac, sulfonamides, allopurinolIntermediate
HLADRB107:01/DQA102:01 (Flucloxacillin), B*57:01 (Flucloxacillin in some)

5. 5. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

SyndromeKey Drug(s)OnsetKey FeatureMortalityManagement
AnaphylaxisPenicillins, NSAIDs, NM blockersMinutes–hrsHypotension, angioedema, wheezeHigh if delayedIM Adrenaline 0.5mg
SJS/TENAllopurinol, Sulfa, AEDs, NSAIDs1–4 weeksBSA detachment, mucosal erosions5–50%Stop drug, ICU, supportive
DRESSAllopurinol, AEDs, Dapsone, Abacavir2–8 weeksFever, eosinophilia, organ involvement~10%Stop drug, steroids 1mg/kg, slow taper
AGEPBeta-lactams, quinolones1–5 daysNon-follicular pustules, neutrophiliaLowStop drug, supportive
TorsadesQT-prolonging drugs + risk factorsVariablePolymorphic VT, R-on-THighIV MgSO₄ 2g, correct K/Mg, pacing
DILI (Hy's Law)INH, Valproate, Flucloxacillin, AugmentinVariableALT>3×ULN + Bil>2×ULN10–50%Stop drug, monitor, transplant eval

6. 6. ❓ Viva Questions (12)

QExpected Answer
1. Define Type B ADR. How does it differ from Type A?Idiosyncratic, non-dose-dependent, unpredictable, rare, high mortality. Type A: dose-dependent, predictable, common, lower mortality.
2. Classify Type B by mechanism.Immunological (Gell-Coombs I–IV) + Non-immunological (pharmacogenetic, mitochondrial, channelopathy)
3. SJS vs TEN vs Overlap — BSA criteria?SJS <10%, Overlap 10–30%, TEN >30% BSA detachment; all ≥2 mucosal sites
4. SCORTEN components? Mortality for score 3?Age>40, HR>120, malignancy, BSA>10%, BUN>28, Glc>14, HCO₃<20. Score 3 = 35% mortality
5. DRESS: typical onset, key labs, management?2–8 weeks; eosinophilia >1.5, atypical lymphs, LFTs, renal; stop drug, pred 1mg/kg, taper 6–12wks
6. AGEP vs SJS/TEN: distinguishing features?AGEP: pustules (not bullae), neutrophilia (not eosinophilia), onset 1–5d, self-limiting <2wks; SJS/TEN: bullae, necrosis, eosinophilia, high mortality
7. Aromatic AED cross-reactivity? Non-aromatic alternatives?High cross-reactivity (CZP, PHT, PB, OXC, LMG). Non-aromatic: Levetiracetam, Topiramate, Gabapentin, Lacosamide, Zonisamide, Pregabalin
8. Torsades: mechanism, highest-risk drug classes, acute treatment?hERG (IKr) blockade; Class Ia/III antiarrhythmics, antipsychotics, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, ondansetron, methadone; IV MgSO₄ 2g bolus
9. Hy's Law for DILI? Significance?ALT>3×ULN + Bilirubin>2×ULN (no obstruction) → predicts 10–50% mortality / transplant; stop drug immediately
10. Flucloxacillin cholestatic DILI: HLA association?HLA-DRB107:01 / DQA102:01 (Class II); onset 2–4 weeks; prolonged jaundice
11. Abacavir hypersensitivity: HLA? PPV/NPV? Management if occurs?HLA-B*57:01; NPV 98–100%, PPV ~50%; Stop permanently, never rechallenge, document allergy
12. Allopurinol SJS/TEN: HLA? Screening in whom? Alternative?HLA-B*58:01; Screen Han Chinese, Korean, Thai, African, CKD≥3; Alternative: Febuxostat (CARE: CV risk)

7. 7. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Type B vs Type C/D/E/FType B = idiosyncratic (unpredictable); Type C = chronic (time-dependent); D = delayed (carcinogenesis); E = end of use (withdrawal); F = failure (inefficacy)
SJS/TEN vs DRESS vs AGEPSJS/TEN: necrosis/bullae, mucosal, high mortality; DRESS: eosinophilia, organ involvement, delayed onset; AGEP: pustules, neutrophilia, rapid onset, benign
AHS vs DRESSAHS = DRESS caused specifically by aromatic AEDs — same spectrum; cross-reactivity among aromatic AEDs
Torsades = Type A or B?Type B — idiosyncratic (not all get it at same dose); requires susceptibility (congenital LQTS, electrolytes, polypharmacy)
Anaphylaxis vs AnaphylactoidAnaphylaxis = IgE-mediated (Type I); Anaphylactoid = non-IgE, direct mast cell activation (e.g., radiocontrast, opioids, vancomycin "red man") — treatment identical

Mnemonics:

  • "B = Bizarre = Bad (high mortality) = idiosyncratic"
  • "SCORTEN" = Skin >10%, Cancer, Old >40, Renal (BUN>28), Tachy >120, Electrolytes (HCO₃<20, Glc>14), Neoplasia
  • "REGISCAR for DRESS" = Rash, Eosinophilia, Globular (atypical lymphs), Intermittent fever, Systemic organ, Cytology, Allopurinol/AEDs, Reaction >2wks
  • "Hy's Law" = Hepatocellular (ALT>3×) + Yellow (Bil>2×) = High mortality
  • "IV Mg for Torsades" = Magnesium Mg for Mechanism (hERG block)

8. 8. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

Type B ADR
Immunological (Gell-Coombs)
Type I (IgE) --> Anaphylaxis, Urticaria
Type II (Cytotoxic) --> AIHA, ITP, Neutropenia, HIT
Type III (Immune Complex) --> Serum Sickness, Vasculitis
Type IV (T-cell)
– IVa (Th1) --> Maculopapular, DRESS
– IVb (Th2) --> Eosinophilic DRESS, AHS
– IVc (CD8 Cytotoxic) --> SJS/TEN, AGEP, MPE
– IVd (Neutrophil) --> AGEP
Non-Immunological
Pharmacogenetic
– G6PD deficiency --> Oxidative drugs haemolysis
– CYP2D6 UM --> Codeine toxicity
– TPMT deficiency --> Azathioprine myelosuppression
– NAT2 slow --> INH neuropathy/hepatotox
Mitochondrial toxicity
– Valproate, NRTIs, Linezolid
Channelopathy
– Congenital LQTS + QT drugs --> Torsades
Idiosyncratic DILI
– INH, Valproate, Flucloxacillin, Augmentin
High-Mortality Syndromes
Anaphylaxis
SJS/TEN (SCORTEN)
DRESS (RegiSCAR)
AGEP
Torsades (hERG)
DILI (Hy's Law)
HLA Associations
B*15:02 --> CZP/PHT/OXC SJS/TEN
B*58:01 --> Allopurinol SCAR
B*57:01 --> Abacavir HSS
A*31:01 --> CZP DRESS
DRB1*07:01 --> Flucloxacillin DILI
B*13:01 --> Dapsone HSS

Total70

Target: ≥56/70 (80%)

11. 11. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Question (10 marks): "Discuss the classification, clinical features, and management of severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SCARs)."

  1. Classification (2): Type B (idiosyncratic), Type IV T-cell mediated; SCARs = SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP, AHS
  2. SJS/TEN (3): Definition (BSA), SCORTEN, drugs (allopurinol, sulfa, AEDs), HLA (B15:02, B58:01), management (stop drug, ICU, supportive, IVIG/cyclosporine/etanercept, NO steroids)
  3. DRESS (2): RegiSCAR criteria, 2–8wk onset, eosinophilia/organ involvement, drugs, HLA, steroids 1mg/kg taper 6–12wks
  4. AGEP (2): Pustules, neutrophilia, 1–5d onset, beta-lactams, self-limiting
  5. Cross-reactivity (1): Aromatic AEDs — avoid all if one causes SCAR

2. Short Question (5 marks): "SJS vs TEN vs DRESS — distinguish."

FeatureSJSTENDRESS
BSA detachment<10%>30%Variable (usually <10%)
Mucosal≥2 sites≥2 sitesOften
Onset1–4 wks1–4 wks2–8 wks
Key labs——Eosinophilia, atypical lymphs
Mortality5–10%30–50%~10%
HLAB*15:02B*15:02B58:01, A31:01

3. Viva (2 min): "Patient on allopurinol 2 weeks, fever, rash, eosinophilia 3×10⁹, ALT 200. Diagnosis? Management?"

  • DRESS (RegiSCAR criteria met)
  • Stop allopurinol immediately
  • Prednisolone 1mg/kg, slow taper 6–12 weeks
  • Monitor LFTs, renal, cardiac
  • Screen HLA-B*58:01 (retrospective)
  • Alternative: Febuxostat (caution CV risk)

4. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on clarithromycin + citalopram develops syncope. ECG shows polymorphic VT, twisting points. Immediate management?"

  • Torsades de Pointes — IV Magnesium sulfate 2g bolus (even if Mg²⁺ normal)
  • Stop clarithromycin + citalopram (both QT prolonging)
  • Correct K⁺ >4.5, Mg²⁺ >1.0
  • Temporary pacing / isoprenaline if recurrent

5. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Type B = Bizarre = Idiosyncratic = Unpredictable = High mortality
  • SJS <10%, TEN >30%, Overlap 10–30% BSA
  • SCORTEN: 7 factors, score 3 = 35% mortality
  • DRESS: 2–8 wks, eosinophilia, organ involvement, steroids taper 6–12wks
  • AGEP: pustules, neutrophilia, 1–5d, beta-lactams, benign
  • Torsades: hERG block → IV Mg 2g
  • Hy's Law: ALT>3× + Bil>2× = 10–50% mortality
  • HLA: B15:02 (CZP), B58:01 (Allo), B57:01 (ABC), A31:01 (CZP DRESS), DRB1*07:01 (Fluclox)
  • Aromatic AEDs cross-react: CZP, PHT, PB, OXC, LMG → switch to LEV, TPM, GBP, LCM

12. 12. 📚 Summary Card

SCAR TRIAD FOR EXAMS:
1. SJS/TEN — Necrosis/bullae, SCORTEN, HLA-B15:02/B58:01, ICU, stop drug, no steroids
2. DRESS — Eosinophilia + organ, 2–8wks, HLA-B58:01/A31:01, Steroids 1mg/kg taper 6–12wks
3. AGEP — Pustules + neutrophilia, 1–5d, beta-lactams, benign, self-limiting
Torsades = hERG block → IV MgSO₄ 2g
Anaphylaxis = IM Adrenaline 0.5mg
Hy's Law = ALT>3× + Bil>2× = transplant/mortality risk

13. 13. ❓ MCQs (15)

  1. Which statement BEST describes a Type B (Bizarre) ADR?

A. Dose-dependent and predictable

B. Idiosyncratic, non-dose-dependent, unpredictable, high mortality ✓

C. Occurs only after chronic use

D. Mediated by IgG antibodies only

E. Preventable by therapeutic drug monitoring

  1. Gell-Coombs Type IV hypersensitivity is mediated by:

A. IgE

B. IgG/IgM + complement

C. Immune complexes

D. T-lymphocytes (CD4+ and CD8+) ✓

E. Neutrophils

  1. SJS/TEN overlap syndrome is defined by BSA detachment of:

A. <10%

B. 10–30% ✓

C. >30%

D. 30–50%

E. >50%

  1. A patient on allopurinol for 3 weeks develops fever, diffuse maculopapular rash, facial oedema, eosinophilia 2.5×10⁹/L, and ALT 300 U/L. Most likely diagnosis?

A. SJS

B. TEN

C. DRESS ✓

D. AGEP

E. Serum sickness

  1. SCORTEN includes all EXCEPT:

A. Age >40

B. Heart rate >120

C. Serum creatinine >200 ✓

D. BUN >28 mg/dL

E. Bicarbonate <20 mmol/L

F. Glucose >14 mmol/L

G. BSA detached >10%

  1. AGEP is characterised by:

A. Full-thickness epidermal necrosis

B. Non-follicular pustules on oedematous erythema + neutrophilia ✓

C. Eosinophilia >1.5×10⁹/L

D. Onset 4–8 weeks after drug initiation

E. Mucosal involvement in >90%

  1. Which drug class has the HIGHEST cross-reactivity in aromatic antiepileptic hypersensitivity?

A. Non-aromatic AEDs (levetiracetam, topiramate)

B. Aromatic AEDs (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine) ✓

C. Valproate only

D. Benzodiazepines

E. Gabapentinoids

  1. Torsades de Pointes is caused by blockade of which potassium channel?

A. IKs

B. IK1

C. hERG (IKr) ✓

D. Ito

E. IKATP

  1. Acute management of Torsades de Pointes (regardless of magnesium level):

A. IV Amiodarone 300mg

B. IV Magnesium sulfate 2g bolus ✓

C. IV Lidocaine 1mg/kg

D. Synchronised cardioversion 100J

E. IV Potassium chloride 20mmol

  1. Hy's Law for DILI predicts:

A. Mild self-limiting hepatitis

B. 10–50% mortality or need for transplant ✓

C. Cholestatic pattern only

D. Resolution within 2 weeks

E. No long-term sequelae

  1. Flucloxacillin-induced cholestatic DILI is associated with:

A. HLA-B*15:02

B. HLA-B*58:01

C. HLA-B*57:01

D. HLA-DRB107:01 / DQA102:01 ✓

E. HLA-A*31:01

  1. Abacavir hypersensitivity syndrome: HLA allele and management if reaction occurs?

A. HLA-B*15:02; reduce dose

B. HLA-B*57:01; stop permanently, never rechallenge ✓

C. HLA-B*58:01; switch to alternative NRTI

D. HLA-A*31:01; continue with steroids

E. HLA-DRB1*07:01; monitor only

  1. Valproate-induced hepatotoxicity is primarily due to:

A. IgE-mediated hypersensitivity

B. Mitochondrial toxicity (beta-oxidation inhibition) ✓

C. CYP2C9 inhibition

D. Immune complex deposition

E. Direct dose-dependent toxicity (Type A)

  1. A patient on carbamazepine develops SJS. Which HLA allele is most strongly associated?

A. HLA-A*31:01

B. HLA-B*15:02 ✓

C. HLA-B*58:01

D. HLA-B*57:01

E. HLA-DRB1*07:01

  1. DRESS vs AGEP — key distinguishing lab finding:

A. Eosinophilia (DRESS) vs Neutrophilia (AGEP) ✓

B. Lymphopenia (DRESS) vs Leukocytosis (AGEP)

C. Thrombocytopenia (DRESS) vs Thrombocytosis (AGEP)

D. Both have eosinophilia

E. Both have neutrophilia

14. 14. 🃏 Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
Type B ADR definitionIdiosyncratic, non-dose-dependent, unpredictable, rare, high mortality
Gell-Coombs Type IIgE-mediated, minutes-hours: Anaphylaxis, urticaria
Gell-Coombs Type IIIgG/IgM cytotoxic: AIHA, ITP, HIT
Gell-Coombs Type IIIImmune complexes: Serum sickness, vasculitis
Gell-Coombs Type IVT-cell mediated: SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP, MPE, AHS
Type IVc (CD8 cytotoxic)SJS/TEN, AGEP, MPE — granulysin, perforin, Fas-FasL
Type IVd (neutrophil)AGEP — IL-8, CXCL8
SJS BSA<10%
TEN BSA>30%
Overlap BSA10–30%
SCORTEN 7 factorsAge>40, HR>120, Cancer, BSA>10%, BUN>28, Glc>14, HCO3<20
SCORTEN score 3 mortality35%
DRESS onset2–8 weeks
DRESS key labsEosinophilia >1.5, atypical lymphs, LFTs, renal
DRESS managementStop drug, Pred 1mg/kg, taper 6–12 weeks
AGEP featuresPustules, neutrophilia, 1–5 days, beta-lactams, benign
AHS = DRESS byAromatic AEDs (CZP, PHT, PB, OXC, LMG) — cross-reactivity HIGH
Non-aromatic AEDsLevetiracetam, Topiramate, Gabapentin, Lacosamide, Zonisamide, Pregabalin
Torsades mechanismhERG (IKr) blockade
Torsades acute RxIV MgSO4 2g bolus
Hy's LawALT>3xULN + Bil>2xULN = 10–50% mortality
HLA-B*15:02Carbamazepine/phenytoin/oxcarbazepine SJS/TEN (Asians)
HLA-B*58:01Allopurinol SCAR (Asians, CKD)
HLA-B*57:01Abacavir HSS (universal screen)
HLA-A*31:01Carbamazepine DRESS (Europeans/Japanese)
HLA-DRB1*07:01Flucloxacillin DILI (Class II)
HLA-B*13:01Dapsone HSS

15. 15. ✅ Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — Type B = idiosyncratic, unpredictable, high mortality
  2. D — Type IV = T-cell mediated
  3. B — SJS/TEN overlap = 10–30% BSA
  4. C — Allopurinol 3wks + fever + rash + eosinophilia + hepatitis = DRESS
  5. C — SCORTEN uses BUN not creatinine
  6. B — AGEP = pustules + neutrophilia
  7. B — Aromatic AEDs high cross-reactivity
  8. C — hERG (IKr) blockade
  9. B — IV MgSO₄ 2g bolus (first-line)
  10. B — Hy's Law = high mortality/transplant
  11. D — Flucloxacillin = Class II HLA-DRB107:01/DQA102:01
  12. B — Abacavir = B*57:01; stop permanently, never rechallenge
  13. B — Valproate = mitochondrial toxicity (beta-oxidation inhibition)
  14. B — CZP SJS/TEN = B*15:02
  15. A — DRESS = eosinophilia; AGEP = neutrophilia
