Algorithms per class
Communication
Goals of care
Time-to-benefit
Shared decision

14. 14. Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScore (0–5)Next
Day 11d
Day 33d
Day 71w
Day 142w
Day 301m
Day 903m

15. 15. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore%
STOPP/START criteria15
ACB/DBI/FORTA calculation10
Renal/hepatic dosing8
Prescribing cascades10
Safer alternatives table12
Viva answers10
Total65

Target: ≥52/65 (80%)

16. 16. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Case (10 marks): "80-year-old woman, frail, 14 medications, recent falls, confusion. Conduct medication review."

  1. Framework (2): STEPS / CEASE — list all meds with indication
  2. Identify PIMs (3): STOPP v3 — amitriptyline (ACB 3), lorazepam (BZD >4w), omeprazole (>8w no indication), 3 antihypertensives (SBP 105), digoxin 250mcg
  3. Prioritise (2): PILL — Preventable harm (amitriptyline, lorazepam) → Indication lost (PPI) → Limited benefit (statin primary prev) → Low benefit (antihypertensives to low SBP)
  4. Deprescribe plan (2): Amitriptyline→duloxetine 4wk; Lorazepam→Ashton 8wk; Stop PPI step-down 4wk; Stop 1 antihypertensive 2wk; Review statin (life exp)
  5. Monitor & Communicate (1): Follow-up 2wk, EMPOWER, restart criteria

2. Short Question (5 marks): "STOPP v3 criterion for antipsychotics in dementia"

  • Antipsychotic prescribed for BPSD >12 weeks without clinical review (STOPP C3)
  • Or: Antipsychotic + cognitive impairment/dementia without documented discussion of risks (mortality ×1.6, stroke ×3)

3. Viva (2 min): "82yo, AF, CrCl 28, on warfarin INR erratic. Plan?"

  • Switch to apixaban 2.5mg BD (CrCl 15–29 + age ≥80 OR weight ≤60kg → 2.5mg BD)
  • If CrCl <15 or dialysis → apixaban 2.5mg BD (off-label but evidence) or warfarin with close INR

4. Ward Round (30 sec): "75yo on glibenclamide. Problem?"

  • Prolonged hypoglycaemia risk (renal excretion, active metabolites). Switch to gliclazide MR or SGLT2i/DPP-4i.

5. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • CG for dosing, eGFR for staging
  • ACB target <3; DBI ≥1 bad
  • FORTA D = avoid (diphenhydramine, amitriptyline, glibenclamide)
  • CCB oedema → don't add diuretic, reduce CCB
  • Statin primary prev: stop if life exp <2y
  • BPSD antipsychotic: review q3mo, stop by 12wk
  • Apixaban renal: 2.5mg BD if 2 of (age≥80, wt≤60, CrCl≤30)

17. 17. Summary Card

ELDERLY PRESCRIBING TRIAD:
1. STOPP/START v3 — explicit criteria
2. ACB <3 / DBI <1 / FORTA A/B — burden scores
3. CrCl (CG) for dosing — not eGFR
CASCADE REVERSAL: Stop the precipitating drug, not the new one
DEPRESCRIBE ORDER (PILL): Preventable harm → Indication lost → Limited life expectancy → Low benefit

18. 18. MCQs (15)

  1. Which equation should be used for drug dosing in an 85-year-old frail woman with sarcopenia?

A. CKD-EPI eGFR

B. Cockcroft-Gault CrCl ✓

C. MDRD

D. Schwartz formula

E. Cystatin C eGFR

  1. FORTA classification: Drug that is "Absolutely indicated, clear benefit" = Class:

A. B

B. C

C. D

D. A ✓

E. None

  1. STOPP v3: Anticholinergic burden score ≥3 is criterion under which section?

A. Cardiovascular

B. Central Nervous System ✓

C. GI

D. Respiratory

E. Musculoskeletal

  1. An 80-year-old man with AF (CHA₂DS₂-VASc 4) has CrCl 25 mL/min. Appropriate apixaban dose?

A. 5 mg BD

B. 2.5 mg BD ✓

C. 5 mg OD

D. 2.5 mg OD

E. Avoid DOAC; use warfarin

  1. Which drug is FORTA Class D (Avoid) for heart failure?

A. Bisoprolol

B. Diclofenac ✓

C. Ramipril

D. Spironolactone

E. Furosemide

  1. Prescribing cascade: Amlodipine → ankle oedema → furosemide. Correct intervention:

A. Increase furosemide

B. Reduce/stop amlodipine ✓

C. Add spironolactone

D. Switch to nifedipine

E. Continue both

  1. Target ACB score in older adults:

A. 0

B. <1

C. <3 ✓

D. <5

E. <10

  1. Drug with ACB score 3:

A. Citalopram

B. Nortriptyline

C. Amitriptyline ✓

D. Quetiapine

E. Solifenacin

  1. Time-to-benefit for statin primary prevention:

A. 6 months

B. 1 year

C. 2–3 years ✓

D. 5 years

E. Immediate

  1. Bisphosphonate drug holiday considered after:

A. 1 year oral

B. 2 years oral

C. 3–5 years oral / 3 years IV ✓

D. 5 years oral / 5 years IV

E. 10 years oral

  1. STOPP v3: PPI inappropriate if:

A. Used for 4 weeks

B. Used >8 weeks for uncomplicated GERD without review ✓

C. Used with aspirin

D. Used with warfarin

E. Used in Barrett's oesophagus

  1. Which sulfonylurea is SAFEST in elderly?

A. Glibenclamide

B. Chlorpropamide

C. Gliclazide MR ✓

D. Glimepiride

E. Tolbutamide

  1. Patient on donepezil develops urinary incontinence. Doctor starts oxybutynin. This is a prescribing cascade. Correct action:

A. Continue both

B. Increase oxybutynin

C. Stop oxybutynin; review donepezil ✓

D. Switch to tolterodine

E. Add mirabegron

  1. Digoxin dose in elderly with AF — maximum maintenance dose:

A. 62.5 mcg daily

B. 125 mcg daily ✓

C. 250 mcg daily

D. 0.5 mg daily

E. 1 mg daily

  1. DBI (Drug Burden Index) incorporates which two domains?

A. Anticholinergic + Cardiovascular

B. Sedative + Anticholinergic ✓

C. Renal + Hepatic

D. Falls + Cognition

E. Bleeding + Thrombosis

19. 19. SBAs (8)

  1. 78-year-old woman with recurrent falls. Medications: amitriptyline 25mg nocte, amlodipine 10mg, furosemide 40mg, paracetamol 1g QDS. Most appropriate intervention?

A. Increase furosemide to 80mg

B. Add bisoprolol 2.5mg for rate control

C. Stop amitriptyline; switch to duloxetine 30mg ✓

D. Stop amlodipine; add indapamide 1.5mg

E. Add vitamin D 800 IU daily

Explanation: Amitriptyline ACB 3 (STOPP C1) — major fall risk. First-line neuropathic: duloxetine/gabapentin/pregabalin (ACB 0–1). Amlodipine oedema managed by reducing amlodipine, not adding diuretic.

  1. 84-year-old man with AF (CHA₂DS₂-VASc 5), CrCl 22 mL/min, weight 58kg. Current warfarin INR labile (1.8–3.5). Best anticoagulation strategy?

A. Continue warfarin; increase INR monitoring

B. Switch to apixaban 2.5mg BD ✓

C. Switch to rivaroxaban 15mg OD

D. Switch to dabigatran 110mg BD

E. Aspirin 75mg + clopidogrel 75mg

Explanation: Apixaban 2.5mg BD if ≥2 of: age ≥80, weight ≤60kg, CrCl ≤30. This patient meets all three. Rivaroxaban 15mg OD if CrCl 15–49; dabigatran avoid if CrCl <30.

  1. 80-year-old woman with T2DM, eGFR 38 mL/min, on glibenclamide 5mg BD. HbA1c 58 mmol/mol. Frequent hypoglycaemia (2–3/week). Best switch?

A. Increase glibenclamide to 10mg BD

B. Switch to gliclazide MR 60mg BD

C. Switch to dapagliflozin 10mg OD ✓

D. Switch to sitagliptin 50mg OD

E. Stop all; insulin sliding scale

Explanation: Glibenclamide Beers/FORTA-D in elderly (prolonged hypoglycaemia). SGLT2i (dapa/empa) safe if eGFR >20, cardio-renal benefit, no hypoglycaemia. Gliclazide MR safer than glibenclamide but still hypoglycaemia risk. DPP-4i safe but no cardio-renal benefit.

  1. 75-year-old man on omeprazole 20mg OD for 3 years for "dyspepsia". Currently asymptomatic. Upper GI endoscopy 2 years ago: mild gastritis, H. pylori negative. STOPP v3 recommendation?

A. Continue omeprazole indefinitely

B. Increase to omeprazole 40mg OD

C. Step down over 4 weeks then stop; lifestyle advice ✓

D. Switch to ranitidine 150mg BD

E. Refer for repeat endoscopy

Explanation: STOPP D1 — PPI >8 weeks for uncomplicated GERD/peptic ulcer healed, no ongoing NSAID/anticoagulant, no Barrett's → step down and stop.

  1. 82-year-old woman with moderate Alzheimer's (MMSE 18/30), on donepezil 10mg OD. Develops new urinary incontinence. GP starts oxybutynin 5mg BD. After 2 weeks, increased confusion and falls. Most likely cause?

A. Donepezil toxicity

B. Antimuscarinic (oxybutynin) worsening cholinergic excess → delirium ✓

C. Urinary tract infection

D. Alzheimer's progression

E. Oxybutynin underdose

Explanation: Cholinesterase inhibitor → ↑ ACh → detrusor overactivity → antimuscarinic prescribed → worsens cognition. Prescribing cascade #3. Correct: stop oxybutynin; review donepezil; consider mirabegron if OAB persists.

  1. 88-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer (on androgen deprivation), atrial fibrillation (CHA₂DS₂-VASc 4), osteoporosis. Medications: apixaban 5mg OD, alendronate 70mg weekly, atorvastatin 40mg, calcium/vitamin D, denosumab 6-monthly. Life expectancy ~8 months. Which medication to STOP?

A. Apixaban

B. Alendronate

C. Atorvastatin ✓

D. Calcium/vitamin D

E. Denosumab

Explanation: Statin primary prevention time-to-benefit 2–3 years. Life expectancy 8 months → no benefit, only harm (myalgia, interactions). Anticoagulant: stroke prevention immediate benefit. Bisphosphonate/denosumab: fracture prevention relevant in ADT. Calcium/vitamin D: low harm.

  1. 70-year-old woman with COPD (FEV1 45% predicted), on tiotropium 18mcg OD, salmeterol/fluticasone 50/250 BD. GP adds theophylline 200mg BD for "extra bronchodilation". After 1 week, nausea, tachycardia, insomnia. STOPP v3 criterion violated?

A. Theophylline narrow therapeutic index (STOPP E1)

B. LABA + LAMA + theophylline = triple therapy not indicated

C. Theophylline + beta-agonist → additive tachycardia

D. A + B + C ✓

E. Theophylline contraindicated in COPD

Explanation: STOPP E1 — theophylline narrow TI, multiple interactions, prefers LABA/LAMA/ICS per GOLD. Not first-line add-on.

  1. 76-year-old man with Parkinson's disease, on levodopa/carbidopa 100/25 TID, ropinirole 2mg TID. Develops visual hallucinations. Neurologist starts quetiapine 25mg nocte. After 4 weeks, worsened motor symptoms. Why?

A. Quetiapine dopamine agonist effect

B. Quetiapine D2 antagonism → worsens parkinsonism ✓

C. Levodopa dose too high

D. Ropinirole dose too high

E. Disease progression

Explanation: Antipsychotics (even atypical) block D2 → worsen Parkinson's. If essential: quetiapine/clozapine lowest D2 affinity. First: reduce/stop dopamine agonist (ropinirole), then reduce levodopa.

20. 20. Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
Cockcroft-Gault formula(140-age)×wt×0.85♀ / (72×SCr) ×1.23 for mL/min
eGFR vs CrCl dosingCrCl (CG) for dosing; eGFR for CKD staging
STOPP v3 PPI criterion>8 weeks uncomplicated GERD/healed ulcer → review/stop
START anticoagulant AFCHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2♂/≥3♀ → anticoagulate
FORTA classesA=Absolutely, B=Beneficial, C=Careful, D=Don't
ACB 3 drugsAmitriptyline, oxybutynin, diphenhydramine, chlorpromazine, olanzapine, scopolamine
ACB target<3
Glibenclamide avoidProlonged hypoglycaemia (renal excretion, active metabolites)
CCB oedema cascadeReduce CCB, not add diuretic
Apixaban renal dosing5mg BD standard; 2.5mg BD if ≥2 of (age≥80, wt≤60, CrCl≤30)
Statin primary prev T2B2–3 years
Bisphosphonate holiday3–5y oral / 3y IV if low fracture risk
BPSD antipsychotic limit12 weeks without review (STOPP)
Cholinesterase inhibitor cascadeDonepezil → incontinence → oxybutynin → STOP oxybutynin
PILL deprescribing priorityPreventable harm → Indication lost → Limited life exp → Low benefit

21. 21. Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — CG uses actual weight & age; eGFR overestimates in sarcopenia
  2. D — FORTA A = absolutely indicated
  3. B — STOPP C1: ACB ≥3
  4. B — Apixaban 2.5mg BD if 2/3 criteria met (age≥80 + CrCl≤30)
  5. B — NSAIDs FORTA D in HF
  6. B — Reverse the cascade: stop precipitant (amlodipine)
  7. C — ACB <3 target
  8. C — Amitriptyline ACB 3
  9. C — Statin primary prevention 2–3y
  10. C — 3–5y oral / 3y IV
  11. B — STOPP D1
  12. C — Gliclazide MR shortest half-life, no active metabolites
  13. C — Antimuscarinic worsens cholinergic excess; stop it
  14. B — Digoxin max 125mcg (0.125mg) in elderly
  15. B — Sedative + Anticholinergic

2. SBAs

  1. C — Amitriptyline ACB 3; switch to duloxetine/gabapentin
  2. B — Apixaban 2.5mg BD (meets all 3 renal dose criteria)
  3. C — Dapagliflozin (SGLT2i) safe eGFR>20, no hypoglycaemia, cardio-renal benefit
  4. C — STOPP D1: step down and stop
  5. B — Prescribing cascade: cholinergic → antimuscarinic → delirium
  6. C — Statin primary prevention no benefit <2y life expectancy
  7. D — Theophylline STOPP E1 (narrow TI) + not indicated + additive toxicity
  8. B — Antipsychotic D2 blockade worsens parkinsonism

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.4.02 Hepatic Impairment

Status: draft | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Prescribing in Special Populations → Hepatic Impairment | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH (Daily clinical use, dosing adjustments, FCPS/MRCP staple)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Calculate Child-Pugh Score (A/B/C)
  • [ ] Apply drug dosing adjustments by Child-Pugh class
  • [ ] Identify contraindicated drugs in hepatic impairment
  • [ ] Recognise drugs requiring monitoring (LFTs, INR, albumin)

2. 2. 📊 Child-Pugh Classification

Parameter1 Point2 Points3 Points
Total Bilirubin (μmol/L)<3434–50>50
Serum Albumin (g/L)>3528–35<28
INR<1.71.7–2.3>2.3
AscitesNoneMild / ControlledModerate–Severe / Refractory
EncephalopathyNoneGrade 1–2Grade 3–4
ClassScoreSurvival (1yr)Survival (2yr)Clinical
A (Mild)5–6100%85%Compensated
B (Moderate)7–980%60%Decompensated
C (Severe)10–1545%35%Decompensated
MELD Score now preferred for transplant listing; Child-Pugh still used for drug dosing decisions

3. 3. ⚖️ Drug Dosing Principles in Hepatic Impairment

PrincipleApplication
↓ First-pass metabolism → ↑ Bioavailability (oral)Propranolol, Morphine, Nitrates, Labetalol, Verapamil, TCAs — ↓ Oral dose
↓ CYP metabolism → ↓ ClearanceCYP3A4/2C9/2C19/1A2 substrates — ↓ Dose / ↑ Interval
↓ Albumin → ↑ Free drug (acidic)Warfarin, Phenytoin, Valproate, NSAIDs — Use FREE levels, ↓ dose
↓ Clotting factors → ↑ Bleeding riskAnticoagulants, Antiplatelets, NSAIDs — Avoid/Monitor closely
↓ Ammonia clearanceValproate — CONTRAINDICATED in active liver disease
Portosystemic shunting↑ Bioavailability of high-extraction drugs

1. Dosing Adjustment by Child-Pugh

Drug ClassChild-Pugh AChild-Pugh BChild-Pugh C
CYP3A4 substrates (Most statins, CCBs, Immunosuppressants, Benzodiazepines)Standard dose; monitor↓ 25–50%↓ 50–75% or AVOID
CYP2C9 substrates (Warfarin, Phenytoin, NSAIDs, Sulfonylureas)Standard; monitor INR/level↓ 25–50%; monitor freeAVOID if possible
CYP1A2 substrates (Theophylline, Clozapine, Duloxetine)Standard↓ 25–50%AVOID
Low extraction drugs (Valproate, Phenytoin, Warfarin)Monitor FREE levelsMonitor FREE levelsAVOID
High extraction drugs (Propranolol, Morphine, Verapamil, Lidocaine)↓ Oral dose (↑ bioavailability)↓ 50%↓ 75% or IV preferred
Renal + Hepatic (Metformin, Metformin contraindicated if both)CautionCONTRAINDICATEDCONTRAINDICATED

4. 4. 🚫 Contraindicated / Avoid in Hepatic Impairment

DrugReason
ValproateInhibits urea cycle → hyperammonaemia; hepatotoxic — CONTRAINDICATED in active liver disease
MethotrexateHepatotoxicity (fibrosis/cirrhosis) — AVOID
IsoniazidHepatotoxicity — AVOID if active liver disease
KetoconazoleHepatotoxicity — AVOID
HalothaneHepatitis — historical
Niacin (high dose)Hepatotoxicity
AmiodaroneHepatotoxicity (steatohepatitis) — Use with extreme caution
Statins (Simvastatin, Atorvastatin high dose)↑ Risk myopathy/rhabdomyolysis — Use low dose, monitor CK
Oral Contraceptives (Estrogen)↑ Thrombosis, cholestasis — AVOID
DanazolPeliosis hepatis, adenomas
MethyldopaHepatitis — rare but serious
CarbamazepineHepatitis, enzyme induction — caution
SulfonamidesHypersensitivity hepatitis

5. 5. 📋 Drugs Requiring Enhanced Monitoring

DrugMonitoringAdjustment
WarfarinINR q1–3d (↓ synthesis of factors, ↓ albumin)↓ Dose; use lowest effective
PhenytoinFREE level (↓ albumin, ↑ free fraction)Use FREE level; ↓ dose
ValproateAmmonia, LFTs (hepatotoxicity)AVOID in active liver disease
CarbamazepineLFTs, CBC (hepatitis, aplastic anaemia)Caution; monitor
StatinsLFTs baseline, 12wks, then q6mo; CK if symptomaticLow dose; avoid simvastatin 80mg
Anticoagulants (DOACs)Renal + Hepatic functionApixaban safest (↓ hepatic metab); avoid rivaroxaban if severe
BenzodiazepinesSedation riskLorazepam/Oxazepam preferred (no CYP, glucuronidation)
OpioidsMorphine, Codeine, Tramadol — ↑ Bioavailability, ↓ clearance↓ Dose 50%, avoid codeine/tramadol (active metabolites)
ParacetamolStandard dose safe (therapeutic); Overdose = hepatotoxicMax 2–3g/day in severe impairment

6. 6. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

PearlDetails
Child-Pugh AScore 5–6; Compensated; Standard dosing with monitoring
Child-Pugh BScore 7–9; Decompensated; ↓ Dose 25–50% for CYP substrates
Child-Pugh CScore 10–15; Severe; AVOID most CYP-metabolised drugs
ValproateCONTRAINDICATED in active liver disease (hyperammonaemia, hepatotoxicity)
WarfarinINR monitoring q1–3d; ↓ dose; ↓ albumin → ↑ free
PhenytoinUse FREE level (↓ albumin → ↑ free fraction)
BenzodiazepinesLorazepam/Oxazepam preferred (glucuronidation, no CYP)
OpioidsMorphine/Codeine/Tramadol — ↓ dose 50%; ↑ bioavailability
StatinsLFTs baseline, 12wks, q6mo; Low dose; Avoid simva 80mg
ParacetamolSafe at therapeutic dose; Max 2–3g/day in severe

7. 7. ❓ Viva Questions (8)

QAnswer
1. Child-Pugh scoring — 5 parameters?Bilirubin, Albumin, INR, Ascites, Encephalopathy (each 1–3 points)
2. Class A/B/C score ranges?A: 5–6, B: 7–9, C: 10–15
3. Valproate in hepatic impairment — why contraindicated?Inhibits carbamoyl phosphate synthetase → hyperammonaemia; Direct hepatotoxicity
4. Warfarin dosing in cirrhosis?↓ Dose; INR monitoring q1–3d (↓ synthesis of factors II, VII, IX, X; ↓ albumin → ↑ free warfarin)
5. Phenytoin monitoring in hypoalbuminaemia?Use FREE phenytoin level (total unreliable; ↓ albumin → ↑ free fraction)
6. Benzodiazepine choice in liver disease?Lorazepam or Oxazepam (glucuronidation only, no CYP metabolism, no active metabolites)
7. Opioid adjustment in liver disease?↓ Dose 50% (morphine, codeine, tramadol — ↑ bioavailability, ↓ clearance); Avoid codeine/tramadol (unpredictable active metabolite formation)
8. Paracetamol in severe hepatic impairment?Safe at therapeutic dose; Max 2–3g/day (avoid >4g); Antidote for overdose = N-acetylcysteine

8. 8. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Child-Pugh vs MELDChild-Pugh for drug dosing; MELD for transplant listing
Valproate = contraindicatedActive liver disease — not just elevated LFTs; valproate ↑ ammonia + direct hepatotoxicity
Total vs Free phenytoinHypoalbuminaemia → use FREE level (total underestimates active drug)
Lorazepam/Oxazepam safeGlucuronidation (preserved in liver disease); No CYP; No active metabolites
Paracetamol safeTherapeutic dose = safe; Overdose = hepatotoxic (GSH depletion)

Mnemonics:

  • "CHILD-PUGH ABC" = Bilirubin, Albumin, INR, Liver (Ascites), Dementia (Encephalopathy); Score: A=5-6, B=7-9, C=10-15
  • "VALPROATE = NO IN LIVER" = Hyperammonaemia + Hepatotoxicity
  • "WARFARIN LIVER" = INR q1-3d, ↓ dose, monitor free
  • "PHENYTOIN = FREE LEVEL" = Hypoalbuminaemia → free fraction ↑
  • "BENZO LIVER = LORAZEPAM/OXAZEPAM" = Glucuronidation, no CYP
  • "OPIOID LIVER = HALF DOSE" = Morphine/Codeine/Tramadol ↓ 50%
  • "PARACETAMOL = SAFE THERAPEUTIC, TOXIC OVERDOSE" = Max 2-3g/day in severe

9. 9. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

Hepatic Impairment
Child-Pugh
Bilirubin, Albumin, INR, Ascites, Encephalopathy
A: 5-6 (Compensated)
B: 7-9 (Decompensated)
C: 10-15 (Severe)
Dosing Principles
↓ First-pass → ↑ Bioavailability (oral high-extraction)
↓ CYP → ↓ Clearance
↓ Albumin → ↑ Free drug (acidic)
↓ Clotting factors → ↑ Bleed risk
Contraindicated
Valproate (ammonia + hepatotox)
Methotrexate, INH, Ketoconazole, High-dose Niacin
Danazol, OCP (estrogen)
Monitoring
Warfarin: INR q1-3d
Phenytoin: FREE level
Valproate: Ammonia, LFTs
Statins: LFTs baseline, 12wk, q6mo
Preferred Choices
Benzo: Lorazepam, Oxazepam (glucuronidation)
Opioid: ↓ 50% dose, avoid codeine/tramadol
Anticoag: Apixaban (safest DOAC)
Paracetamol: Safe therapeutic (max 2-3g/day severe)

10. 10. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScoreNext
11d
23d
31w
42w
51m
63m

11. 11. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore
Child-Pugh scoring8
Dosing principles6
Contraindicated drugs6
Monitoring6
Viva answers8
Total34

Target: ≥27/34 (80%)

12. 12. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Short Question (5 marks): "Child-Pugh classification and drug dosing principles in hepatic impairment."

  • Parameters: Bilirubin, Albumin, INR, Ascites, Encephalopathy (each 1–3 pts)
  • Classes: A=5–6 (Compensated), B=7–9 (Decompensated), C=10–15 (Severe)
  • Principles: ↓ First-pass → ↑ bioavailability (oral high-extraction); ↓ CYP → ↓ clearance; ↓ Albumin → ↑ free acid drugs; ↓ Clotting factors → ↑ bleed risk
  • Adjustments: Child-Pugh B: ↓ CYP substrates 25–50%; Child-Pugh C: Avoid most

2. Viva (1 min): "Patient with Child-Pugh B cirrhosis needs anticoagulation for AF. Options?"

  • DOAC preferred over warfarin (no INR monitoring, ↓ bleed risk)
  • Apixaban safest (↓ hepatic metabolism ~25%, renal ~25%); Dose 2.5mg BD if ≥2 of: age≥80, weight≤60kg, Cr≥133
  • Avoid rivaroxaban (significant hepatic metabolism)
  • If warfarin: INR q1–3d, ↓ dose, monitor free warfarin

3. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient with cirrhosis on valproate for epilepsy. LFTs rising. Action?"

  • Valproate CONTRAINDICATED in active liver disease (hepatotoxicity + hyperammonaemia)
  • STOP valproate immediately
  • Switch to levetiracetam or lamotrigine (no hepatic metabolism)
  • Monitor ammonia, LFTs

4. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Child-Pugh: Bilirubin, Albumin, INR, Ascites, Encephalopathy; A=5-6, B=7-9, C=10-15
  • Valproate = CONTRAINDICATED in active liver disease
  • Warfarin = INR q1-3d, ↓ dose
  • Phenytoin = FREE level in hypoalbuminaemia
  • Benzo choice = Lorazepam/Oxazepam (glucuronidation)
  • Opioids = ↓ 50% dose, avoid codeine/tramadol
  • Paracetamol = Safe therapeutic, max 2-3g/day severe

13. 13. 📚 Summary Card

HEPATIC IMPAIRMENT:
CHILD-PUGH: Bilirubin, Albumin, INR, Ascites, Encephalopathy
A=5-6, B=7-9, C=10-15
VALPROATE = CONTRAINDICATED (ammonia + hepatotox)
WARFARIN: INR q1-3d, ↓ dose
PHENYTOIN: FREE level
BENZO: Lorazepam/Oxazepam (glucuronidation)
OPIOIDS: ↓ 50%, avoid codeine/tramadol
PARACETAMOL: Safe therapeutic (max 2-3g/day severe)
STATINS: LFTs baseline, 12wk, q6mo; low dose
DOAC: Apixaban safest (25% hepatic metab)

14. 14. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Child-Pugh Class B score range:

A. 5–6

B. 7–9 ✓

C. 10–15

D. 1–4

E. 16–18

  1. Valproate in active liver disease:

A. Safe with monitoring

B. Contraindicated ✓

C. Reduce dose 50%

C. Monitor ammonia only

E. Safe if albumin normal

  1. Warfarin monitoring in cirrhosis:

A. INR weekly

B. INR q1–3d ✓

C. INR monthly

D. No INR needed

E. Use anti-Xa level

  1. Phenytoin in hypoalbuminaemia — which level to use?

A. Total phenytoin

B. Free phenytoin ✓

C. Both total and free

D. Corrected total only

E. Saliva level

  1. Benzodiazepine of choice in hepatic impairment:

A. Diazepam

B. Lorazepam ✓

C. Midazolam

D. Alprazolam

E. Clonazepam

  1. Opioid dose adjustment in cirrhosis:

A. No change

B. ↓ 50% ✓

C. ↑ 50%

D. ↑ 100%

E. Avoid all opioids

  1. Paracetamol in severe hepatic impairment — max daily dose:

A. 4g

B. 2–3g ✓

C. 1g

D. 500mg

E. Contraindicated

  1. DOAC safest in hepatic impairment:

A. Dabigatran

B. Rivaroxaban

C. Apixaban ✓

D. Edoxaban

E. All equal

  1. Statins monitoring in liver disease:

A. No monitoring needed

B. LFTs baseline, 12 weeks, then q6mo ✓

C. LFTs monthly

D. CK only

E. LFTs only if symptomatic

  1. Valproate hepatotoxicity mechanism:

A. Immune-mediated

B. Mitochondrial toxicity (β-oxidation inhibition) + Hyperammonaemia (CPS1 inhibition) ✓

C. Bile duct injury

D. Hypersensitivity

E. Steatosis only

Child-Pugh B7–9 (Decompensated)

1. MCQs

  1. B — 7–9
  2. B — Contraindicated
  3. B — INR q1–3d
  4. B — Free phenytoin
  5. B — Lorazepam

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.4.03 Hepatic Prescribing

Parent Topic: Prescribing in Special Populations → Clinical Therapeutics Overview

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (FCPS/MRCP — Child-Pugh dosing, hepatotoxic drugs, PK changes in cirrhosis)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 2; BNF Hepatic Impairment; FDA/EMA Hepatic Impairment Guidance; AASLD/EASL Guidelines; Liverpool Drug Interaction (hep-druginteractions.org)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify hepatic impairment: Child-Pugh A/B/C and MELD — interpretation and prescribing relevance
  • [ ] Understand PK changes in cirrhosis: ↓ first-pass, ↓ clearance, ↓ protein binding, ↑ Vd, portosystemic shunting
  • [ ] Apply dose adjustment principles: avoid, reduce dose, extend interval, monitor TDM
  • [ ] Identify hepatotoxic drugs (intrinsic vs idiosyncratic) and drug-induced liver injury (DILI) patterns
  • [ ] Know contraindicated/avoid drugs in cirrhosis: NSAIDs, sedatives, metformin (lactic acidosis), certain antibiotics
  • [ ] Apply specific dosing for common drugs: antibiotics, anticoagulants, analgesics, antidiabetics, cardiovascular
  • [ ] Answer viva: "How does cirrhosis change drug metabolism?" and "Dose adjustment for [drug] in Child-Pugh B/C"

2. 2. 🧠 Core Concept: PK Changes in Liver Disease

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Cirrhosis / Liver Disease
↓
Architectural Distortion ↓ Hepatocyte mass Portosystemic shunting
↓
↓ FIRST-PASS METABOLISM ↑ Bioavailability (oral) High extraction drugs: propranolol, morphine, nitrates, verapamil
↓ HEPATIC CLEARANCE ↓ CYP450 (1A2, 2C9, 2C19, 3A4) ↓ Phase II (UGT, SULT, GST) Low extraction drugs: warfarin, phenytoin, theophylline
↓ PROTEIN SYNTHESIS ↓ Albumin → ↑ Free fraction (acidic drugs: warfarin, phenytoin, valproate) ↓ α1-AGP → ↑ Free fraction (basic drugs: lidocaine, propranolol)
↑ VOLUME OF DISTRIBUTION Ascites, oedema → ↑ Vd (hydrophilic drugs: aminoglycosides, digoxin) ↓ Albumin → ↑ Vd (protein-bound drugs)
RENAL IMPAIRMENT (HRS) ↓ GFR → ↓ Renal clearance Combined hepatorenal dosing needed
↓
CLINICAL EFFECT: ↑ Drug levels, ↑ toxicity risk

1. Child-Pugh Classification (Prescribing Relevance)

ParameterPoints 1Points 2Points 3
Bilirubin (μmol/L)<3434–50>50
Albumin (g/L)>3528–35<28
INR<1.71.7–2.3>2.3
AscitesNoneMild/Med (diuretic responsive)Severe (refractory)
EncephalopathyNoneGrade 1–2Grade 3–4
ClassScore1-y SurvivalPrescribing Implication
A5–6~100%Mild — Minor dose adjustments; most drugs OK with monitoring
B7–9~80%Moderate — Significant dose reductions; avoid hepatotoxic drugs; TDM essential
C10–15~45%Severe — Avoid many drugs; major dose reductions; prefer non-hepatic clearance; TDM mandatory
Viva Key: Child-Pugh B = moderate impairment = reduce dose ~50% for hepatically cleared drugs. Child-Pugh C = severe = avoid if possible, use alternatives with renal clearance.

3. 3. ️⃣ Drug Dosing Principles in Hepatic Impairment

1. General Rules

PrincipleApplication
Avoid hepatotoxic drugsIntrinsic (dose-dependent) + Idiosyncratic (unpredictable) — see Section 2
Reduce dose for high extraction drugs↓ First-pass → ↑ Bioavailability → ↓ Dose (e.g., propranolol, morphine, verapamil, nitrates)
Reduce dose for low extraction drugs with hepatic clearance↓ CYP/UGT → ↓ Clearance → ↓ Dose or ↑ Interval (e.g., warfarin, phenytoin, theophylline, diazepam)
Prefer renal clearance drugsIf alternative exists with renal elimination (e.g., atenolol > propranolol; gabapentin > pregabalin? both renal)
Monitor free drug levels↓ Albumin → ↑ Free fraction → Measure FREE level (phenytoin, valproate, warfarin)
TDM mandatoryNarrow therapeutic index drugs: warfarin, phenytoin, digoxin, lithium, immunosuppressants
Start low, go slowLower initial dose; titrate to effect; monitor for toxicity

2. Dose Adjustment Categories (FDA/EMA)

CategoryActionExamples
No adjustment neededRenal clearance; minimal hepatic metabolismAtenolol, Gabapentin, Pregabalin, Digoxin (mostly renal), Lithium, Aminoglycosides, Vancomycin, Most antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems)
Reduce dose / Extend intervalHepatic clearance; moderate impairmentChild-Pugh B: ↓ 50% dose or ↑ interval 2x; Child-Pugh C: ↓ 75% or avoid
Avoid / ContraindicatedHepatotoxic; high extraction + narrow TI; lactic acidosis riskNSAIDs, Metformin (Child-Pugh C), High-dose paracetamol >2g/day, Sedatives (benzos, Z-drugs), Certain antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampicin, flucloxacillin in susceptible)

4. 4. ️⃣ Hepatotoxic Drugs: Avoid or Use with Extreme Caution in Liver Disease

1. Intrinsic (Dose-Dependent) Hepatotoxins

DrugMechanismRisk in Cirrhosis
Paracetamol >4g/day (or >2g/day in malnutrition/alcohol)NAPQI accumulation → hepatocellular necrosis↑ Risk — ↓ Glutathione stores; max 2–3g/day in cirrhosis
MethotrexateHepatocyte apoptosis → fibrosisContraindicated in significant liver disease
IsoniazidHydrazine metabolites → hepatocellular injuryMonitor LFTs monthly; avoid if Child-Pugh C
RifampicinCYP induction + direct toxicityCholestatic injury; monitor LFTs
PyrazinamideHepatocellular injuryMonitor LFTs; avoid if Child-Pugh C
ValproateMitochondrial toxicity → microvesicular steatosisAvoid in liver disease; ↑ ammonia (hyperammonaemic encephalopathy)
AmiodaronePhospholipidosis → steatohepatitisMonitor LFTs; long half-life = prolonged risk
MethyldopaAutoimmune hepatitis-likeRare but avoid if alternative exists

2. Idiosyncratic (Unpredictable) Hepatotoxins — High Risk in Cirrhosis

DrugPatternNotes
FlucloxacillinCholestatic (delayed 2–4 weeks)HLA-B*57:01 not linked; risk ↑ with age >55, duration >2 weeks
Co-amoxiclavCholestatic (1–6 weeks)Most common cause of DILI in UK; avoid if prior DILI
Carbamazepine / Phenytoin / PhenobarbitalHepatocellular / Mixed / Hypersensitivity (DRESS/SJS)HLA-B*15:02 for CBZ/PHT; monitor LFTs first 3 months
AllopurinolMixed / DRESS / SJSHLA-B*58:01 screen; start low (50mg), titrate slowly
Sulfonamides (TMP-SMX, sulfasalazine)Hypersensitivity / CholestaticAvoid in cirrhosis if possible
Statins (all)Hepatocellular (asymptomatic ALT rise)Not contraindicated in stable chronic liver disease; monitor LFTs; avoid acute liver failure
Azoles (fluconazole, itraconazole, voriconazole)Hepatocellular / CholestaticMonitor LFTs; avoid in Child-Pugh C
Antiretrovirals (NNRTIs > PIs > INSTIs)HepatocellularNVP > EFV > DTG; monitor LFTs monthly initially
Viva Key: Flucloxacillin/co-amoxiclav = cholestatic DILI (delayed). INH/rifampicin/pyrazinamide = dose-dependent + idiosyncratic. Allopurinol = HLA-B58:01 screen. Valproate = avoid in liver disease (↑ ammonia). Statins = generally SAFE in stable CLD.*

5. 5. ️⃣ Drug-Specific Dosing in Hepatic Impairment (High-Yield)

1. Cardiovascular

DrugChild-Pugh AChild-Pugh BChild-Pugh CNotes
Beta-blockers
Propranolol (high extraction)↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%Avoid↓ First-pass → ↑ bioavailability; use atenolol (renal) instead
MetoprololStandard↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%CYP2D6 substrate
BisoprololStandardStandard↓ Dose 50%Partially renal
AtenololPreferredPreferredPreferredRenal clearance — no hepatic adjustment
Calcium Channel Blockers
AmlodipineStandardStandard?↓ Dose 50%Long half-life; CYP3A4
Verapamil / Diltiazem (high extraction)↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%Avoid↓ First-pass; ↑ bioavailability
ACEi / ARBStandardStandardStandard (caution: AKI risk)Monitor renal/K+; no hepatic dose adjustment
Diuretics
FurosemideStandardStandardStandard (caution: ototoxicity)No hepatic adjustment; monitor renal
SpironolactoneStandardStandardStandardMonitor K+/renal; no hepatic adjustment
Statins
Atorvastatin / SimvastatinStandardStandard?↓ Dose 50% / Avoid simvaCYP3A4; monitor LFTs; not contraindicated in stable CLD
Rosuvastatin / PravastatinPreferredPreferredPreferredMinimal CYP; renal clearance component
Anticoagulants
WarfarinMonitor INR closelyMonitor INR closelyMonitor INR closely↓ Albumin → ↑ free warfarin; ↓ CYP2C9 → ↓ clearance; INR unpredictable
DOACs (Apixaban, Rivaroxaban)Standard↓ Dose (apixaban 2.5mg)Avoid (apixaban 2.5mg if essential)Apixaban preferred; Rivaroxaban avoid Child-Pugh B/C
AntiplateletsStandardStandardStandardNo hepatic adjustment

2. Analgesics

DrugChild-Pugh AChild-Pugh BChild-Pugh CNotes
ParacetamolMax 3g/dayMax 2g/dayMax 2g/day↓ Glutathione → ↑ NAPQI risk; avoid chronic high dose
NSAIDsAvoidAvoidAvoid↑ GI bleed (varices), renal impairment (HRS), fluid retention
Opioids
Morphine (high extraction)↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%Avoid↓ First-pass → ↑ bioavailability; active metabolite M6G accumulates in renal impairment
Oxycodone↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%AvoidCYP3A4/2D6; active metabolites
Fentanyl / BuprenorphinePreferredPreferredPreferred (transdermal)Transdermal bypasses first-pass; less accumulation
Tramadol↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%AvoidCYP2D6/3A4; seizure risk
CodeineAvoidAvoidAvoidProdrug → morphine (CYP2D6); unpredictable in liver disease
Gabapentin / PregabalinPreferredPreferredPreferred (renal adjust)Renal clearance — no hepatic adjustment

3. Antibiotics / Antifungals

DrugChild-Pugh AChild-Pugh BChild-Pugh CNotes
Penicillins / Cephalosporins / CarbapenemsStandardStandardStandard (renal adjust)Renal clearance — safe in liver disease
Fluoroquinolones (Cipro/Levo/Oflox)StandardStandardStandard (renal adjust)Minimal hepatic; monitor QT
MetronidazoleStandard↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 50%Hepatic metabolism; neurotoxicity risk
Macrolides (Clarithro/Erythro)Standard↓ Dose 50%AvoidCYP3A4 inh; QT prolongation; hepatotoxicity risk
AzithromycinStandardStandardStandardPreferred macrolide
RifampicinStandardStandard?AvoidHepatotoxicity; enzyme induction worsens other drugs
IsoniazidStandardStandard (monitor LFTs)AvoidHepatotoxicity; pyridoxine co-prescribe
FluconazoleStandardStandard↓ Dose 50%Renal + hepatic; monitor LFTs
Voriconazole / Posaconazole / IsavuconazoleStandard↓ Dose / MonitorAvoidHepatotoxicity; CYP3A4; TDM essential
TMP-SMXStandardStandardStandard (renal adjust)HyperK risk in renal impairment

4. Antidiabetics

DrugChild-Pugh AChild-Pugh BChild-Pugh CNotes
MetforminStandardContraindicatedContraindicatedLactic acidosis risk ↑ in liver disease (↓ lactate clearance)
Gliclazide / GlimepirideStandard↓ Dose 50%AvoidHypoglycaemia risk ↑ (↓ gluconeogenesis, ↓ clearance)
GlipizideStandard↓ Dose 50%AvoidSimilar
PioglitazoneAvoidAvoidAvoidFluid retention → worsens ascites; hepatotoxicity
DPP-4 inhibitors (Sitagliptin/Linagliptin)Standard (renal adjust)Standard (renal adjust)Standard (renal adjust)Preferred — minimal hepatic, renal adjust
SGLT2i (Dapa/Empa)Standard (renal adjust)Standard (renal adjust)Standard (renal adjust)Preferred — cardio/renal benefit; monitor eGFR
GLP-1 RA (Liraglutide/Semaglutide)StandardStandardStandardPreferred — weight loss, cardio benefit; no hepatic adjustment
InsulinStandardStandardStandardPreferred — no hepatic clearance; monitor hypoglycaemia (↓ gluconeogenesis)

5. Neurological / Psychiatric

DrugChild-Pugh AChild-Pugh BChild-Pugh CNotes
Benzodiazepines (Diazepam, Lorazepam, Midazolam)↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%Avoid↑ Bioavailability, ↑ Vd, ↓ clearance; ↑ encephalopathy risk
Z-drugs (Zopiclone/Zolpidem)↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%AvoidSimilar to benzos
Antidepressants
SSRIs (Sertraline, Citalopram, Escitalopram)Standard↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 50%CYP2C19/3A4/2D6; citalopram QT risk
MirtazapineStandard↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 50%Sedation ↑ encephalopathy risk
Antipsychotics
Quetiapine↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%AvoidCYP3A4; sedation; metabolic
Olanzapine↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%AvoidCYP1A2; metabolic
Haloperidol↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%AvoidCYP3A4/2D6; QT; EPS
Anticonvulsants
PhenytoinMonitor FREE levelMonitor FREE levelMonitor FREE level↓ Albumin → ↑ free fraction; ↓ CYP2C9/19; FREE level essential
Carbamazepine↓ Dose 50%↓ Dose 75%AvoidAuto-induction + ↓ clearance; DRESS risk
ValproateAvoidAvoidAvoid↑ Ammonia → encephalopathy; hepatotoxicity
LevetiracetamPreferredPreferredPreferred (renal adjust)Renal clearance — safe
Lacosamide / BrivaracetamStandardStandardStandard (renal adjust)Minimal hepatic
LithiumStandardStandardStandard (renal adjust)Renal clearance — monitor renal function

6. Immunosuppressants (Transplant Context)

DrugChild-Pugh AChild-Pugh BChild-Pugh CNotes
TacrolimusTDM ↓ doseTDM ↓ doseTDM ↓ doseCYP3A4/P-gp; TDM essential; ↑ nephrotoxicity
CiclosporinTDM ↓ doseTDM ↓ doseTDM ↓ doseCYP3A4/P-gp; TDM essential
Sirolimus / EverolimusTDM ↓ doseTDM ↓ doseTDM ↓ doseCYP3A4/P-gp; TDM essential
MycophenolateStandardStandardStandardGlucuronidation (UGT); minor hepatic
Prednisolone → PrednisoneUse prednisoloneUse prednisoloneUse prednisolonePrednisone requires hepatic activation → use prednisolone directly

6. 6. ️⃣ Drug-Induced Liver Injury (DILI): Patterns & Management

1. DILI Patterns

PatternBiochemistryExamplesManagement
HepatocellularALT/AST ↑↑↑ (×10–100 ULN), Bilirubin ↑, ALP normal/mild ↑Paracetamol, Isoniazid, Valproate, Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, Statins (rare), AntiretroviralsStop drug immediately; monitor LFTs; N-acetylcysteine for paracetamol
CholestaticALP ↑↑ (×2–5 ULN), ALT/AST mild ↑, Bilirubin ↑, GGT ↑↑Flucloxacillin, Co-amoxiclav, Macrolides, Rifampicin, Sulfonamides, Chlorpromazine, AzathioprineStop drug; ursodeoxycholic acid for pruritus; monitor bilirubin (Hy's Law)
MixedBoth ALT & ALP ↑↑ (ratio 2–5)Amoxicillin-clavulanate, Flucloxacillin, TMP-SMX, Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, AllopurinolStop drug; monitor for Hy's Law
VascularSOS (sinusoidal obstruction syndrome), Budd-Chiari, Nodular regenerative hyperplasiaCyclophosphamide, Azathioprine, Oxaliplatin, Oral contraceptivesStop drug; specialist management

2. Hy's Law (Predictor of Fatal DILI)

ALT/AST >3× ULN + Total Bilirubin >2× ULN + No ALP elevation (or <2× ULN) = ~10–50% mortality / transplant
  • Action: Stop drug immediately; urgent hepatology referral; monitor for liver failure

3. RUCAM (Roussel Uclaf Causality Assessment Method) — See [[ADRs/Causality assessment/RUCAM]]

7. 7. ️⃣ Special Situations

1. Paracetamol in Cirrhosis

  • Max dose: 2–3g/day (not 4g)
  • Avoid chronic use
  • Malnutrition/alcohol → ↓ glutathione → ↑ toxicity at lower doses
  • IV paracetamol — same limit; weight-based dosing (<50kg: 15mg/kg/dose)

2. Alcohol + Drugs in Liver Disease

  • Acute alcohol → inhibits CYP2E1, CYP3A4 → ↑ drug levels (metronidazole, warfarin, phenytoin)
  • Chronic alcohol → induces CYP2E1, CYP3A4, CYP1A2 → ↓ drug levels (paracetamol toxicity at lower doses, theophylline)
  • Disulfiram-like reaction: Metronidazole, TMP-SMX, Cephalosporins (cefotetan), Griseofulvin, Chloramphenicol + alcohol → flushing, tachycardia, hypotension

3. Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC) / Transplant List

  • Child-Pugh C / MELD >15 — transplant assessment
  • Drugs to avoid pre-transplant: NSAIDs, nephrotoxins, hepatotoxins
  • Post-transplant: Immunosuppressant interactions (see [[Drug Interactions/High-risk drug combinations/Immunosuppressant interactions]])

8. 8. ⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

ConceptKey Points
Child-Pugh ClassificationA (5–6) = mild, B (7–9) = moderate, C (10–15) = severe; guides dosing
PK Changes in Cirrhosis↓ First-pass (↑ bioavailability high extraction), ↓ CYP/UGT clearance, ↓ albumin (↑ free drug), ↑ Vd (ascites), portosystemic shunting
Dosing PrinciplesAvoid hepatotoxins; reduce dose high extraction drugs; reduce dose/extend interval low extraction hepatic drugs; prefer renal clearance; TDM; free levels for protein-bound
Intrinsic HepatotoxinsParacetamol (>2g/day cirrhosis), Methotrexate, INH, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, Valproate (↑ ammonia), Amiodarone
Idiosyncratic HepatotoxinsFlucloxacillin (cholestatic), Co-amoxiclav (cholestatic), CBZ/PHT/PB (hypersensitivity/DRESS), Allopurinol (HLA-B*58:01), Sulfonamides, Azoles
Statins in CLDSAFE in stable disease; monitor LFTs; avoid acute liver failure; rosuvastatin/pravastatin preferred
AnticoagulationWarfarin: unpredictable INR (↓ albumin, ↓ CYP2C9); DOACs: apixaban preferred (↓ dose Child-Pugh B), avoid rivaroxaban B/C
AnalgesicsParacetamol max 2–3g/day; NSAIDs AVOID; opioids: fentanyl/buprenorphine transdermal preferred; avoid morphine/codeine
AntidiabeticsMetformin CONTRAINDICATED Child-Pugh B/C (lactic acidosis); SU avoid; DPP-4/SGLT2i/GLP-1/Insulin preferred
SedativesBenzos/Z-drugs AVOID (↑ encephalopathy); if essential: lorazepam/oxazepam (no active metabolites, glucuronidation)
AnticonvulsantsPhenytoin: FREE level; Valproate: AVOID; CBZ: avoid; Levetiracetam/lacosamide/brivaracetam preferred
AntibioticsPenicillins/cephalosporins/carbapenems/fluoroquinolones = safe (renal); macrolides/rifampicin/INH = caution; azoles = monitor/TDM

9. 9. 🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1How does cirrhosis affect drug pharmacokinetics?1) ↓ First-pass metabolism → ↑ bioavailability of high extraction drugs (propranolol, morphine). 2) ↓ Hepatic clearance (CYP450, UGT) → ↓ clearance of low extraction drugs. 3) ↓ Albumin synthesis → ↑ free fraction of protein-bound drugs (warfarin, phenytoin). 4) ↑ Vd (ascites, oedema) → ↑ Vd of hydrophilic drugs. 5) Portosystemic shunting → bypass liver.
2Child-Pugh B cirrhosis: general dosing approach?Reduce dose ~50% or double interval for hepatically cleared drugs. Avoid hepatotoxins. Prefer renal clearance alternatives. TDM for NTI drugs. Monitor free levels for highly protein-bound.
3Which beta-blocker is preferred in cirrhosis?Atenolol (renal clearance, no first-pass). Avoid propranolol (high extraction → ↑ bioavailability).
4Paracetamol max dose in cirrhosis?2–3g/day (not 4g). ↓ Glutathione stores → ↑ NAPQI toxicity risk.
5Why are NSAIDs contraindicated in cirrhosis?1) GI bleed risk (varices, gastropathy). 2) Renal impairment → precipitates HRS (↓ prostaglandin-mediated renal perfusion). 3) Fluid retention → worsens ascites.
6Warfarin in cirrhosis: why is INR unpredictable?↓ Albumin → ↑ free warfarin; ↓ CYP2C9 → ↓ clearance; ↓ Vitamin K-dependent factor synthesis (liver synthetic dysfunction) → baseline INR elevated. Result: INR does not reflect anticoagulation intensity.
7DOAC choice in Child-Pugh B cirrhosis with AF?Apixaban 2.5mg BD (licensed for Child-Pugh B). Avoid rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban in Child-Pugh B/C.
8Metformin in cirrhosis: contraindicated in which Child-Pugh?Child-Pugh B and C (lactic acidosis risk — ↓ lactate clearance by liver). Child-Pugh A: use with caution, monitor lactate.
9Phenytoin monitoring in cirrhosis — what level?FREE phenytoin level (therapeutic 1–2 mg/L). Total level misleading due to ↓ albumin. Corrected formula unreliable.
10Valproate in liver disease — why avoid?1) Hepatotoxicity (microvesicular steatosis, mitochondrial toxicity). 2) ↑ Ammonia → hyperammonaemic encephalopathy (↓ urea cycle). 3) ↓ Albumin → ↑ free fraction.
11Flucloxacillin DILI pattern and timing?Cholestatic, delayed onset 2–4 weeks (sometimes up to 8 weeks). Risk ↑ age >55, duration >2 weeks.
12Co-amoxiclav DILI — most common cause in UK?Cholestatic, 1–6 weeks. Most common cause of DILI in UK. Avoid if prior DILI.
13Preferred sedative in cirrhosis if essential?Lorazepam or Oxazepam — no active metabolites, glucuronidation (Phase II preserved longer), short half-life. Avoid diazepam, chlordiazepoxide, Z-drugs.
14Hy's Law criteria?ALT/AST >3× ULN + Total Bilirubin >2× ULN + ALP <2× ULN = high risk of fatal DILI/transplant (~10–50%).

10. 10. 🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"All statins contraindicated in liver disease"FALSE. Statins are safe in stable chronic liver disease (NAFLD, compensated cirrhosis). Monitor LFTs. Avoid in acute liver failure / decompensated. Rosuvastatin/pravastatin preferred (less CYP).
"Metformin safe in Child-Pugh A"Caution. Child-Pugh A: may use with close lactate monitoring. Child-Pugh B/C: contraindicated. Lactic acidosis risk from ↓ hepatic lactate clearance.
"Total phenytoin level OK in cirrhosis"FALSE. ↓ Albumin → ↑ free fraction. Must measure FREE level. Total level underestimates free drug.
"All benzodiazepines same in liver disease"Lorazepam/oxazepam/temazepam = glucuronidation (Phase II) → relatively preserved. Diazepam/chlordiazepoxide/clonazepam = CYP3A4/2C19 (Phase I) → markedly impaired.
"Child-Pugh and MELD interchangeable for dosing"Child-Pugh used for drug dosing (A/B/C categories). MELD used for transplant prioritisation (continuous score). Both reflect severity but Child-Pugh is standard in dosing guidelines.
"Rifampicin safe in liver disease if monitor LFTs"Rifampicin = hepatotoxic + potent enzyme inducer → worsens other drug levels. Avoid if possible in Child-Pugh C; use with extreme caution in B.
"DOACs all contraindicated in cirrhosis"Apixaban licensed for Child-Pugh B (2.5mg BD). Rivaroxaban/dabigatran/edoxaban not recommended B/C. Warfarin = unpredictable but used (INR monitoring).
Mnemonic: LIVER PRESCRIBING
Liver disease PK: ↓ First-pass, ↓ Clearance, ↓ Albumin, ↑ Vd, Shunting
Intrinsic hepatotoxins: Paracetamol (>2g), Methotrexate, INH, Rifampicin, PZA, Valproate, Amiodarone
Valproate avoid: ↑ Ammonia, hepatotoxicity
Extraction ratio matters: High extraction (propranolol, morphine) → ↑ bioavailability → ↓ dose
Renal alternatives preferred: Atenolol, Gabapentin, Levetiracetam, DOACs (apixaban), Insulin, SGLT2i
Protein binding: ↓ Albumin → ↑ free warfarin, phenytoin, valproate → measure FREE level
Rifampicin/INH/PZA: Hepatotoxic + enzyme induction — avoid/reduce
Enzyme induction in chronic alcohol: CYP2E1, 3A4, 1A2 ↑ → ↓ drug levels
Statins SAFE in stable CLD: Monitor LFTs; rosuvastatin/pravastatin preferred
Child-Pugh: A=mild (minor adj), B=moderate (↓50%), C=severe (avoid)
Renal dose adjust needed too (HRS common)
Idosyncratic hepatotoxins: Flucloxacillin (cholestatic 2-4w), Co-amoxiclav (cholestatic 1-6w, #1 UK), CBZ/PHT/PB (DRESS/SJS, HLA-B15:02), Allopurinol (HLA-B58:01), Sulfonamides, Azoles
Benzos/Z-drugs AVOID: ↑ encephalopathy; if essential → lorazepam/oxazepam (glucuronidation)
INSULIN/DPP-4/SGLT2i/GLP-1 preferred for DM; Metformin CI B/C; SU avoid
NSAIDs CONTRAINDICATED: GI bleed (varices), HRS, fluid retention
Glucuronidation preserved: Lorazepam, oxazepam, morphine? (M6G renal), paracetamol? (but glutathione ↓)

11. 11. 🗺️ Mind Map

root((Hepatic
Prescribing))
PK Changes
↓ First-pass (high extraction ↑ bioavailability)
↓ CYP/UGT clearance (low extraction ↓ clearance)
↓ Albumin (↑ free fraction)
↑ Vd (ascites, oedema)
Portosystemic shunting
Child-Pugh
A (5-6): Mild, minor adj
B (7-9): Moderate, ↓50% dose
C (10-15): Severe, avoid
Hepatotoxins (Avoid)
Intrinsic: Paracetamol>2g, MTX, INH, Rifampicin, PZA, VPA, Amiodarone
Idiosyncratic: Flucloxacillin (cholestatic 2-4w), Co-amoxiclav (cholestatic #1 UK), AEDs (DRESS/SJS), Allopurinol (B*58:01), Sulfonamides, Azoles
Drug-Specific Dosing
CV: Atenolol>Propranolol; Apixaban>Warfarin>Rivaroxaban; Rosuva/Prava>Simva/Atorva
Analgesia: Paracetamol≤2-3g; NSAIDs AVOID; Fentanyl/Buprenorphine TD>Morphine
Antibiotics: Penicillins/Cephalosporins/Carbapenems/FQs safe; Macrolides/Rifampicin/INH/Azoles caution
DM: Metformin CI B/C; SU avoid; DPP-4/SGLT2i/GLP-1/Insulin preferred
Neuro: Benzo/Z-drug AVOID; Lorazepam/Oxazepam if essential; VPA AVOID; PHT FREE level; LEV/LCM/BRV preferred
Immunosupp: Tacro/Ciclo/Siro TDM essential; Prednisolone not prednisone
DILI Patterns
Hepatocellular: ALT↑↑ (Paracetamol, INH, VPA)
Cholestatic: ALP↑↑ (Flucloxacillin, Co-amoxiclav, Macrolides)
Mixed: Both (TMP-SMX, Phenytoin)
Vascular: SOS, BCS (Cyclophos, OCPs)
Hy's Law: ALT>3x + Bil>2x + ALP<2x = fatal risk
