14. 14. 💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Discuss prescribing in a patient with Child-Pugh B cirrhosis."PK changes, dosing principles, specific drug adjustments (CV, analgesia, antibiotics, DM, neuro), avoid hepatotoxins, TDM, free levels
Short Note"Drug-induced liver injury patterns."Hepatocellular, cholestatic, mixed, vascular; Hy's Law; examples; management
Viva"Patient with cirrhosis needs anticoagulation for AF. Options?"Warfarin: unpredictable INR (↓ albumin, ↓ CYP2C9, ↓ factor synthesis). DOAC: Apixaban 2.5mg BD licensed Child-Pugh B. Avoid rivaroxaban/dabigatran. Monitor renal.
Ward Round"Patient with decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C) prescribed ibuprofen for back pain."STOP NSAID — contraindicated: GI bleed (varices), precipitates HRS, worsens ascites. Alternative: paracetamol max 2g/day, or transdermal buprenorphine.
Last-Night"Hepatic prescribing: 3 PK changes, 3 intrinsic hepatotoxins, 3 idiosyncratic, 3 drug adjustments, 1 DILI law."PK: ↓1st pass, ↓clearance, ↓albumin, ↑Vd, shunt. Intrinsic: paracetamol>2g, MTX, INH, VPA. Idiosyncratic: fluclox, co-amoxiclav, allopurinol. Adjustments: atenolol, apixaban, fentanyl. Hy's Law.

15. 15. 📌 Summary

  • PK in cirrhosis: ↓ first-pass (↑ bioavailability high extraction), ↓ CYP/UGT clearance, ↓ albumin (↑ free drug), ↑ Vd (ascites), shunting
  • Child-Pugh: A=mild (minor adj), B=moderate (↓50% dose/extend interval), C=severe (avoid hepatically cleared drugs)
  • Intrinsic hepatotoxins: Paracetamol >2g/day, methotrexate, INH, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, valproate, amiodarone
  • Idiosyncratic hepatotoxins: Flucloxacillin (cholestatic 2–4w), co-amoxiclav (cholestatic 1–6w, #1 UK), AEDs (DRESS/SJS/HLA), allopurinol (HLA-B*58:01), sulfonamides, azoles
  • Dosing: Prefer renal clearance (atenolol, levetiracetam, apixaban, insulin, gabapentin, penicillins); avoid NSAIDs, metformin (Child B/C), sedatives, valproate, high-dose paracetamol; free phenytoin level; TDM for NTI
  • Statins: Safe in stable CLD; monitor LFTs; rosuvastatin/pravastatin preferred
  • DILI: Hy's Law (ALT>3×ULN + Bil>2×ULN + ALP<2×ULN) = high mortality risk

16. 16. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. High extraction ratio drug — effect of cirrhosis on oral bioavailability?

A. Decreased B. Increased C. Unchanged D. Unpredictable

Answer: B. ↓ First-pass metabolism → ↑ bioavailability (e.g., propranolol, morphine, verapamil, nitrates).

  1. Preferred beta-blocker in cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B)?

A. Propranolol B. Atenolol C. Metoprolol D. Carvedilol

Answer: B. Atenolol = renal clearance, no first-pass. Propranolol high extraction → ↑ bioavailability.

  1. Maximum paracetamol dose in cirrhosis?

A. 4g/day B. 2–3g/day C. 1g/day D. Contraindicated

Answer: B. ↓ Glutathione → ↑ NAPQI toxicity risk at lower doses.

  1. Which DOAC is licensed for use in Child-Pugh B cirrhosis?

A. Rivaroxaban B. Dabigatran C. Apixaban 2.5mg BD D. Edoxaban

Answer: C. Apixaban 2.5mg BD is licensed for Child-Pugh B. Others not recommended.

  1. Metformin contraindicated in which Child-Pugh class?

A. A only B. B and C C. C only D. All classes

Answer: B. Lactic acidosis risk from ↓ hepatic lactate clearance. Child-Pugh A: caution with monitoring.

  1. Phenytoin monitoring in cirrhosis — which level?

A. Total B. Free C. Both D. Neither

Answer: B. ↓ Albumin → ↑ free fraction. Total level misleading. Free therapeutic range 1–2 mg/L.

  1. Valproate avoided in liver disease because:

A. Only hepatotoxicity B. Only ↑ ammonia C. Both hepatotoxicity AND ↑ ammonia (hyperammonaemic encephalopathy) + ↑ free fraction D. Renal toxicity

Answer: C. Triple risk: mitochondrial hepatotoxicity, ↑ ammonia (↓ urea cycle), ↑ free fraction (↓ albumin).

  1. Hy's Law criteria for fatal DILI risk:

A. ALT>3x + Bil>2x + ALP>2x B. ALT>3x + Bil>2x + ALP<2x C. ALT>5x + Bil>3x D. ALP>3x + Bil>2x

Answer: B. Hy's Law: ALT/AST >3× ULN + Total Bilirubin >2× ULN + ALP <2× ULN (no cholestasis).

  1. Flucloxacillin DILI pattern and typical onset:

A. Hepatocellular, 1–2 weeks B. Cholestatic, 2–4 weeks C. Mixed, 4–8 weeks D. Vascular, immediate

Answer: B. Cholestatic pattern, delayed onset 2–4 weeks (delayed up to 8 weeks).

  1. Preferred sedative in cirrhosis if absolutely necessary?

A. Diazepam B. Zopiclone C. Lorazepam D. Clonazepam

Answer: C. Lorazepam (or oxazepam) — glucuronidation (Phase II preserved), no active metabolites, short half-life.

17. 17. 📋 SBAs (10)

  1. 65M with Child-Pugh B cirrhosis (alcohol) needs rate control for AF. Current: propranolol 40mg TDS. Best switch?

A. Increase propranolol B. Switch to atenolol 25mg OD C. Add digoxin D. Switch to verapamil

Answer: B. Propranolol high extraction → ↑ bioavailability in cirrhosis. Atenolol renal = preferred.

  1. 70F with cirrhosis on warfarin for mechanical mitral valve. INR 4.5 (target 2.5–3.5) on stable dose 5mg. No bleeding. Likely cause?

A. Drug interaction B. Cirrhosis: ↓ albumin (↑ free warfarin) + ↓ CYP2C9 + ↓ factor synthesis C. Dietary vitamin K D. Non-adherence

Answer: B. Cirrhosis alters warfarin PK/PD unpredictably. ↓ albumin → ↑ free warfarin; ↓ CYP2C9 → ↓ clearance; ↓ factor synthesis → baseline INR elevated.

  1. Patient with Child-Pugh C cirrhosis needs analgesia for femoral fracture. Best opioid?

A. Morphine 10mg IV B. Transdermal buprenorphine 5μg/h C. Oral oxycodone 10mg D. Codeine 30mg

Answer: B. Transdermal buprenorphine bypasses first-pass, less accumulation. Morphine/oxycodone high extraction → ↑ bioavailability. Codeine prodrug unpredictable.

  1. 60M with cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B) newly diagnosed T2DM. HbA1c 58. First-line?

A. Metformin 500mg BD B. Gliclazide 80mg BD C. Linagliptin 5mg OD (or SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA) D. Pioglitazone 15mg OD

Answer: C. Metformin CI Child-Pugh B. SU hypoglycaemia risk. Pioglitazone fluid retention. DPP-4/SGLT2i/GLP-1 preferred.

  1. Patient on phenytoin 300mg OD for epilepsy develops cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B). Total phenytoin 12 mg/L (therapeutic 10–20). Albumin 22 g/L. Action?

A. Increase dose B. No dose change; measure FREE phenytoin level C. Decrease dose D. Switch to levetiracetam

Answer: B. Hypoalbuminaemia → ↑ free fraction. Corrected total = 12/(0.2×2.2+0.1) = 22 mg/L (toxic). MUST measure FREE level.

  1. Antibiotic for intra-abdominal infection in Child-Pugh C cirrhosis. Best choice?

A. Co-amoxiclav B. Piperacillin-tazobactam (renal adjust) C. Clarithromycin D. Rifampicin

Answer: B. Penicillins/cephalosporins/carbapenems = renal clearance, safe in liver disease. Co-amoxiclav/DILI risk. Macrolides/rifampicin hepatotoxic.

  1. Statins in compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A) — correct statement?

A. Contraindicated B. Safe; monitor LFTs; rosuvastatin/pravastatin preferred C. Only simvastatin allowed D. Must stop if ALT >ULN

Answer: B. Statins safe in stable CLD. Monitor LFTs. Rosuvastatin/pravastatin minimal CYP. Not contraindicated.

  1. Hy's Law indicates:

A. Drug-induced cholestasis B. High risk of fatal DILI / need for transplant C. Hepatocellular recovery D. Safe to continue drug

Answer: B. ALT>3x + Bil>2x + ALP<2x = 10–50% mortality/transplant risk.

  1. Patient with cirrhosis on spironolactone 100mg develops hepatic encephalopathy. Precipitant?

A. Spironolactone B. Hypokalaemia from diuretic → ↑ renal ammonia production C. Spironolactone hepatotoxicity D. Hyperkalaemia

Answer: B. Diuretics → hypokalaemia → ↑ renal ammoniagenesis → encephalopathy. Spironolactone itself not direct cause.

  1. Rifampicin in Child-Pugh B cirrhosis with TB. Dosing?

A. Standard dose B. Standard dose but MONITOR LFTs closely; avoid if Child-Pugh C C. Halve dose D. Contraindicated

Answer: B. Rifampicin hepatotoxic + enzyme inducer. Use standard dose with LFT monitoring in Child-Pugh B. Avoid Child-Pugh C.

18. 18. 🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-C, 5-B, 6-B, 7-C, 8-B, 9-B, 10-C1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-C, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B

19. 19. 🔗 Cross-Links

  • [[Drug Interactions/Pharmacokinetic interactions/Metabolism interactions]] — CYP changes in liver disease
  • [[Drug Interactions/Pharmacokinetic interactions/Excretion interactions]] — Renal dosing (HRS)
  • [[Drug Interactions/High-risk drug combinations/QT prolonging combinations]] — QT drugs in cirrhosis
  • [[Therapeutic Drug Monitoring]] — TDM in hepatic impairment (phenytoin FREE, valproate, immunosuppressants)
  • [[ADRs/Type B (Bizarre)]] — DILI hypersensitivity (HLA, DRESS, SJS)
  • [[ADRs/Causality assessment/RUCAM]] — DILI causality
  • [[Special Populations/Elderly Prescribing]] — Overlap frailty + liver disease
  • [[Clinical Context/Perioperative Prescribing]] — Surgery in cirrhosis
  • [[Clinical Context/Palliative Care]] — End-stage liver disease prescribing
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.4.04 Paediatric Prescribing
[!tip] FCPS/MRCP Priority: HIGH
Daily paediatric practice + exams. Weight-based dosing (mg/kg) is non-negotiable. Four "NEVER" contraindications: Aspirin <16, Tetracycline <12, Fluoroquinolone <18, Codeine <12. Tenfold error prevention (lead zero, no trail zero) is examinable. Off-label prescribing framework (GMC) appears in ethics vivas.
Viva classic: "15kg child needs ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IV. Vial 1g reconstituted to 100mg/mL. What volume?"

1. 1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Calculate weight-based doses (mg/kg) and BSA-based doses (mg/m²) accurately
  • [ ] Apply age-related physiological changes (neonate → adolescent) to drug selection & dosing
  • [ ] Identify contraindicated/avoided drugs in children (aspirin, tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones, codeine)
  • [ ] Use age-appropriate formulations (oral liquid, dispersible, rectal, IV)
  • [ ] Recognise paediatric-specific ADRs (Grey Baby Syndrome, Reye's, Kernicterus, growth suppression, enamel dysplasia)
  • [ ] Perform paediatric medication safety checks (tenfold errors, decimal points, unlicensed/off-label)
  • [ ] Counsel parents/carers on administration, storage, adherence
  • [ ] Answer viva: "Weight-based calculation" and "Codeine contraindication mechanism" and "Ceftriaxone in jaundiced neonate"

2. 2. Core Concept: Paediatric Dosing Principles

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Child Presenting for Prescribing
↓
Obtain ACCURATE Weight (kg) & Height (cm)
↓
Calculate Dose
↓
Weight-Based (mg/kg)\nMost drugs
BSA-Based (mg/m²)\nChemo, high TI drugs
Neonatal: PMA/Corrected Age\nCaffeine, gentamicin, phenobarbital
↓
Select Formulation\nAge-appropriate
↓
Safety Check\nTenfold errors, lead zero, trail zero
↓
Document: mg/kg, weight, BSA, calculation, formulation, max dose
↓
Counsel Parent/Carer\nAdministration, storage, adverse effects
Golden Rule: Every paediatric prescription MUST have: weight in kg, dose in mg (or mcg), calculation shown, formulation specified, maximum daily dose stated.

3. 3. Dose Calculation Methods

1. 2.1 Weight-Based (mg/kg) — Most Common

$$Dose (mg) = Weight (kg) \times Dose (mg/kg)$$

  • Standard: Use actual body weight (not ideal)
  • Obese (BMI >95th percentile): Consider ideal body weight for lipophilic drugs (e.g., gentamicin, digoxin, midazolam)
  • Neonates: Use birth weight until term corrected age; then current weight

2. 2.2 Body Surface Area (BSA) — Chemo, Some Antibiotics, High TI Drugs

$$BSA (m^2) = \sqrt{\frac{Height (cm) \times Weight (kg)}{3600}} \quad \text{(Mosteller)}$$

$$Dose (mg) = BSA (m^2) \times Dose (mg/m^2)$$

AgeWeight (kg)BSA (m²)
Preterm 28wk1.00.08
Term neonate3.50.20
6 months7.50.35
1 year100.46
5 years180.68
10 years301.00
Adult701.73

3. 2.3 Age-Based (Clark's Rule / Fried's Rule) — Historical/Estimation Only

  • Fried's Rule (infants <1yr): $$Dose = \frac{Age (months)}{150} \times Adult Dose$$
  • Clark's Rule (children 1–12yr): $$Dose = \frac{Weight (lb)}{150} \times Adult Dose$$
⚠️ Not for precision prescribing — use weight/BSA

4. 2.4 Neonatal Dosing: Postmenstrual Age (PMA) & Postnatal Age

ConceptDefinition
Gestational Age (GA)Weeks from LMP to birth
Postnatal AgeDays/weeks since birth
Postmenstrual Age (PMA)GA + Postnatal age
Corrected AgePostnatal age - (40 - GA)

Neonatal dosing often uses PMA (e.g., caffeine, gentamicin, phenobarbital)

4. 4. Age-Related Pharmacokinetic Changes

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Developmental PK Changes
↓
Neonate/Preterm
Infant 1-12m
Child 1-12y
Adolescent
↓
↑ TBW 80-90%\n↓ Fat 1%\n↓ Albumin\n↓ CYP450 (Phase I)\n↓↓ UGT (Phase II)\n↓↓ GFR 10-20\n↑ BBB permeability
TBW 75%\nFat 15%\nCYP OVERSHOOT 2-5x adult\nUGT maturing\nGFR 100+\nTubular secretion maturing
Adult-like PK\nTBW 60%\nFat 15-20%\nAdult CYP/UGT\nAdult GFR
Adult PK\nPuberty hormonal effects\nBody composition changes
ParameterPreterm NeonateTerm NeonateInfant (1–12m)Child (1–12y)Adolescent
Gastric pHHighHigh → ↓ by 2wkLow (adult-like)AdultAdult
Gastric emptyingSlowSlow → normal 6mNormalNormalNormal
Protein binding↓ Albumin (↑ free drug)↓ Albumin↑ to adultAdultAdult
Body water (TBW)↑ 80–90%↑ 75%65%60%60%
Body fat↓ 1%↓ 15%25%15–20%15–20%
CYP450 (Phase I)↓↓↓ Immature↓↓ Immature↑↑ Overshoot (2–5× adult)AdultAdult
UGT (Phase II)↓↓↓ Very low↓↓ Low↑ to adultAdultAdult
Renal: GFR↓↓ 10–20 mL/min↓ 20–40↑↑ 100+Adult 120Adult
Renal: Tubular secretionAbsentImmatureMaturingAdultAdult
BBB permeabilityPermeablePermeableLess permeableAdultAdult

5. 5. Key Drug-Specific Implications by Age

Drug ClassNeonatal/Infant Consideration
Aminoglycosides (Gentamicin)↑ Vd (↑ TBW) → higher mg/kg dose; ↓ clearance (↓ GFR) → extended interval (q24–48h); TDM essential
Vancomycin↑ Vd; ↓ clearance → AUC-guided dosing (target 400–600 mg·h/L)
Phenytoin / Phenobarbital↓ Protein binding → ↑ free fraction; ↓ metabolism → saturation kinetics; TDM total + free
Morphine / Opioids↑ BBB permeability → ↑ respiratory depression risk; ↓ UGT2B7 (morphine-6-glucuronide)
Paracetamol↓ Glucuronidation (UGT1A6) → ↑ oxidative pathway (NAPQI) BUT ↑ sulphation compensates; standard 15mg/kg safe
Chloramphenicol↓ UGT → Grey Baby Syndrome (cardiovascular collapse, ashen grey) — AVOID neonates
SulfonamidesDisplace bilirubin → Kernicterus — avoid <2 months (and G6PD deficiency)
Cephalosporins (3rd gen)↑ Vd, ↓ clearance → standard mg/kg but extended interval in preterm
Proton Pump InhibitorsCYP2C19 polymorphism matters; omeprazole ↓ clearance in infants
Corticosteroids (inhaled/nasal)Growth velocity monitoring; adrenal suppression risk

6. 6. Contraindicated / Avoided Drugs in Children — THE BIG FOUR + MORE

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Contraindicated Drugs in Children
↓
NEVER: Aspirin <16
NEVER: Tetracycline <12
NEVER: Fluoroquinolone <18
NEVER: Codeine <12
Avoid: Chloramphenicol neonate
Avoid: Sulfonamide <2mo
Caution: Benzodiazepines <6m
Caution: 1st gen antihistamines <2-6y
DrugAge AvoidanceReasonAlternative
Aspirin<16 years (UK) / <19 (US)Reye's Syndrome (viral illness + aspirin → hepatic encephalopathy)Paracetamol, ibuprofen
Tetracyclines (doxycycline, minocycline)<12 years (some <8)Enamel dysplasia, bone deposition, growth inhibitionMacrolides, amoxicillin
Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin)<18 years (avoid <18 unless no alternative)Arthropathy, tendonitis, cartilage damage3rd gen cephalosporin, TMP-SMX
Codeine<12 years (UK) / <18 post-tonsillectomyCYP2D6 ultra-rapid metabolizers → morphine toxicity (apnoea, death)Morphine, oxycodone, paracetamol+ibuprofen
Tramadol<12 yearsSimilar CYP2D6 risk; seizuresAs above
ChloramphenicolNeonates <1 monthGrey Baby Syndrome (↓ UGT)Ceftriaxone, meropenem
Sulfonamides (TMP-SMX)<2 months (and G6PD)Kernicterus (bilirubin displacement)Alternative abx
Benzodiazepines (oral midazolam excepted)<6 months / cautious <2yRespiratory depression, paradoxical agitationAge-appropriate sedation
Antihistamines 1st gen (promethazine, diphenhydramine)<2 years (FDA) / <6y (UK)Respiratory depression, sedation, paradoxical excitation2nd gen (cetirizine, loratadine)
Hyoscine / Antimuscarinics<2 yearsHyperthermia, ileus, CNS effectsDomperidone (caution), ondansetron
Droperidol / Metoclopramide (high dose/prolonged)<1 year / <20kgExtrapyramidal, NMS riskOndansetron, cyclizine (caution)
Topical corticosteroids (high potency)<1 year face/flexuresSkin atrophy, adrenal suppressionLow potency, short course

7. 7. Formulation Selection by Age

AgePreferred FormulationsAvoid
Preterm/Term NeonateIV, oral liquid (preservative-free), rectal, buccalTablets, capsules, alcohol-containing liquids
Infant (1–12m)Oral liquid, dispersible tablet, rectal, IVTablets, capsules, chewables (choking)
Child 1–5yOral liquid, dispersible/chewable, sprinkle capsulesLarge tablets, sublingual
Child 6–12yTablets (small), chewable, dispersible, liquidLarge unscored tablets
AdolescentAdult formulations usually OK—

Key excipients to avoid in neonates/infants:

  • Propylene glycol (lorazepam IV, phenytoin IV) — metabolic acidosis, seizures
  • Benzyl alcohol (preservative) — gasping syndrome (preterm)
  • Ethanol (oral liquids) — CNS depression
  • Sorbitol / Mannitol — osmotic diarrhoea

8. 8. Paediatric Medication Safety — TENFOLD ERROR PREVENTION

1. 7.1 Common Error Traps

Error TypeExamplePrevention
Decimal point0.5 mg written as .5 mg → read as 5 mgAlways write 0.5 mg (leading zero); never .5 mg
Trailing zero5.0 mg → read as 50 mgNever write trailing zeros (5 mg not 5.0 mg)
Unit confusionmg vs mcg (µg)Write microgram in full or use mcg; avoid µg
Weight errorlb vs kgDocument weight in kg ONLY; convert at admission
BSA calculationWrong formula / height errorUse Mosteller; verify height
Concentration error100 mg/5mL vs 100 mg/mLState concentration on prescription; double-check
Frequency error"daily" vs "BD" vs "TDS"Write in full: ONCE DAILY, TWICE DAILY
Age-inappropriate formulationTablet to 2-year-oldSpecify formulation on Rx

2. 7.2 Safety Checklist (Every Prescription)

☐ Patient name, DOB, weight (kg), height (cm), BSA (m²), gestational age (if <2y)
☐ Drug name (generic), dose in mg (or mcg), NOT "1 tablet"
☐ Dose calculation shown: mg/kg or mg/m² + weight/BSA used
☐ Route, frequency (in words), duration
☐ Formulation specified (liquid: concentration mg/mL)
☐ Maximum daily dose stated
☐ Renal/hepatic adjustment noted if applicable
☐ Allergy status documented
☐ Unlicensed/off-label status flagged + justification
☐ Prescriber signature, GMC, date

9. 9. Common Paediatric Dosing Quick Reference

1. 8.1 Analgesia / Antipyretics

DrugDoseMaxNotes
Paracetamol15 mg/kg/dose PO/PR q4–6h60 mg/kg/day (max 4g/day >50kg)120 mg/5mL (24 mg/mL) or 250 mg/5mL
Ibuprofen10 mg/kg/dose PO q6–8h30–40 mg/kg/day>3 months, >5kg; with food; avoid dehydration
Morphine0.1–0.2 mg/kg/dose IV q2–4h—Neonate: 0.05–0.1 mg/kg; PCA >6y; monitor resp
Fentanyl1–2 mcg/kg/dose IV—Intranasal 1.5–2 mcg/kg (procedural)

2. 8.2 Antibiotics (Common)

DrugDose (mg/kg)FrequencyKey Notes
Amoxicillin25–50TDS125 mg/5mL, 250 mg/5mL
Co-amoxiclav25/6.25–50/12.5TDSAugmentin ES: 45/3.2 TDS
Cefalexin25–50QDS125 mg/5mL
Cefuroxime10–15BD125 mg/5mL
Ceftriaxone50–100OD IV/IMAvoid <28 days corrected if hyperbilirubinaemia (bilirubin displacement)
Azithromycin10OD x3–5d200 mg/5mL
Clarithromycin7.5BD125 mg/5mL
Trimethoprim4–8BDProphylaxis 2mg/kg nocte
Nitrofurantoin3–5QDSAvoid <3 months (haemolysis risk); renal impairment
Gentamicin4–5 (extended interval)OD (traditional) / q24–48h (Hartford)TDM: peak 14–20, trough <1; adjust interval
Vancomycin15q6–8h (neonate q8–12h)AUC-guided 400–600; trough 10–20 (old)

3. 8.3 Anticonvulsants

DrugLoadMaintenanceTDM
Phenytoin15–20 mg/kg IV4–8 mg/kg/day BDTotal 10–20 µg/mL; free 1–2
Phenobarbital15–20 mg/kg IV/IM3–5 mg/kg/day OD15–40 µg/mL
Levetiracetam—10–60 mg/kg/day BDNot routine
Sodium Valproate—10–30 mg/kg/day BD50–100 µg/mL
Carbamazepine—10–20 mg/kg/day BD4–12 µg/mL

4. 8.4 Respiratory

DrugDoseNotes
Salbutamol (nebuliser)2.5 mg (<5y) / 5 mg (>5y)q20min x3 then q1–4h
Ipratropium250 mcg nebq20min x3 with salbutamol
Prednisolone1–2 mg/kg/day OD3–5 days acute asthma; max 60mg
Dexamethasone0.15–0.6 mg/kg ODCroup: 0.15 mg/kg (max 10mg)

5. 8.5 Neonatal Specific

DrugDoseIndication
Caffeine citrateLoad 20 mg/kg → 5–10 mg/kg/day ODApnoea of prematurity
Surfactant100 mg/kg (poractant)RDS
Vitamin K1 mg IM (term) / 0.5 mg IM (preterm)Haemorrhagic disease prevention
Hepatitis B vaccine10 mcg (0.5 mL) IMBirth, 1m, 6m
BCG0.05 mL ID (<1yr) / 0.1 mL (>1yr)TB endemic areas

10. 10. Paediatric-Specific ADRs (Exam Favorites)

ADRDrugPopulationMechanism
Grey Baby SyndromeChloramphenicolNeonates <1 month↓ UGT → ↑ free drug → mitochondrial toxicity → CV collapse, ashen skin, hypothermia
Reye's SyndromeAspirin + viral illness<16 yearsMitochondrial injury → hepatic encephalopathy, cerebral oedema
KernicterusSulfonamides (TMP-SMX)Neonates <2 months, G6PDBilirubin displacement → bilirubin encephalopathy
Enamel dysplasia / Bone growth inhibitionTetracyclines<12 yearsCalcium chelation in developing teeth/bones
Arthropathy / Cartilage damageFluoroquinolones<18 yearsWeight-bearing joint cartilage toxicity (animal data)
Fatally toxic morphine levelsCodeine<12 years, CYP2D6 UMUltra-rapid metabolism → morphine overdose → respiratory arrest
Growth suppressionInhaled corticosteroids (high dose/long-term)ChildrenSystemic absorption → ↓ growth velocity (reversible catch-up)
Adrenal suppressionTopical/inhaled/nasal steroidsInfants/childrenHPA axis suppression
Hypertrophic pyloric stenosisMacrolides (erythromycin/azithromycin)Neonates <2 weeksGastric outlet obstruction (↑ risk 7–10×)
Cataracts / GlaucomaLong-term corticosteroidsChildrenPosterior subcapsular cataract, ↑ IOP

11. 11. Unlicensed / Off-Label Prescribing

TermDefinition
Licensed (On-label)Marketing authorisation for that age, dose, route, indication
Off-labelLicensed drug used outside licence (age, dose, indication, route)
UnlicensedNo UK marketing authorisation (imported, specials, clinical trial)

Prescriber Responsibilities (GMC):

  1. No suitable licensed alternative exists
  2. Evidence base supports use (guidelines, literature)
  3. Informed consent from parent/carer (and child if Gillick competent)
  4. Document justification in notes
  5. Monitor for efficacy and ADRs

Common Off-Label in Paeds:

  • Most antibiotics in neonates (dose/route)
  • Proton pump inhibitors <1 year
  • Antidepressants <18 years
  • Antipsychotics <18 years
  • Melatonin (unlicensed special in UK; licensed in EU)

12. 12. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Paediatric Prescribing

ScenarioKey Points
4kg neonate, gentamicin dosingExtended interval (Hartford): 4–5 mg/kg q24h; TDM pre-dose (trough <1) at 18h; adjust interval
3yo, 15kg, amoxicillin for AOM40 mg/kg/day TDS = 600 mg/day → 200 mg/dose → 4 mL of 250 mg/5mL TDS
6yo, tonsillectomy, codeine prescribedSTOP — codeine contraindicated <12y (CYP2D6 UM risk death); use morphine/paracetamol/ibuprofen
2mo, UTI, ceftriaxone?Avoid if jaundiced (bilirubin displacement); use cefotaxime instead
10yo, asthma exacerbation, prednisolone1–2 mg/kg OD (max 60mg) x 3–5 days; no taper if <14 days
Neonate, apnoea, caffeine citrateLoad 20 mg/kg IV → 5–10 mg/kg/day OD; TDM not routine
Child on long-term inhaled steroidMonitor growth velocity 6-monthly; use lowest effective dose; spacer + rinse
Adolescent, acne, doxycyclineAvoid <12y; if >12y, counsel on photosensitivity, oesophageal ulcer (take with water, upright)
Preterm, PDA, ibuprofen IV10 mg/kg dose 1 → 5 mg/kg at 24h & 48h; monitor renal, platelets, GI bleed

13. 13. Viva Questions (10)

QAnswer
1. 2-year-old, 12kg, needs paracetamol. What dose? Formulation?15 mg/kg = 180 mg/dose; 120 mg/5mL → 7.5 mL q4–6h (max 4 doses/24h)
2. Why is aspirin contraindicated <16 years?Reye's Syndrome — mitochondrial dysfunction → hepatic encephalopathy + cerebral oedema after viral illness
3. 3-week-old, UTI. Can you use ceftriaxone?Avoid if hyperbilirubinaemia (displaces bilirubin → kernicterus); use cefotaxime
4. 8-year-old post-tonsillectomy. Codeine prescribed. Action?Contraindicated — CYP2D6 ultra-rapid metabolizers → morphine toxicity → respiratory arrest/death
5. Neonate on chloramphenicol develops grey skin, hypothermia, CV collapse. Diagnosis?Grey Baby Syndrome — ↓ UGT glucuronidation → toxic levels
6. 5kg infant, gentamicin. Standard vs extended interval?Extended interval (Hartford) preferred: 4–5 mg/kg q24–48h; TDM trough <1 at 18h
7. Prescription: "Morphine 0.1mg/kg 4-hourly". Child weighs 20kg. What volume of 10mg/mL?0.1 × 20 = 2mg/dose → 0.2 mL of 10mg/mL
8. What is the Mosteller formula for BSA?√(Height cm × Weight kg / 3600)
9. 12-year-old on high-dose inhaled fluticasone 500mcg BD. Monitoring?Growth velocity 6-monthly; bone density if >5y; cataract/glaucoma screen
10. Parent asks: "Is this medicine licensed for my child?" You prescribed omeprazole liquid 5mg to 6-month-old. Answer?Off-label (licensed >1 year); explain no licensed alternative, evidence supports use, document consent

14. 14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
mg/kg vs mg/m²mg/kg = most drugs; mg/m² = chemo, some abx (gentamicin sometimes), high TI drugs
Ideal vs actual weightActual weight for most; ideal body weight for lipophilic drugs in obesity (gentamicin, digoxin, midazolam)
Ceftriaxone in neonatesAvoid <28 days corrected if jaundiced (bilirubin displacement); cefotaxime preferred
Codeine vs morphineCodeine prodrug → morphine via CYP2D6; UM = toxic morphine levels
Off-label vs unlicensedOff-label = licensed drug outside licence; Unlicensed = no UK MA (specials, imports)

Mnemonics:

  • GREY BABY = Grey baby syndrome = Chloramphenicol in neonates
  • REYE'S = Reason: Aspirin + Viral = Encephalopathy + Syndrome
  • KERN = Kernicterus = Sulfonamides + Neonates
  • NO ASPIRIN <16, NO TETRACYCLINE <12, NO QUINOLONE <18, NO CODEINE <12
  • LEAD ZERO, NO TRAIL ZERO = 0.5 mg ✓ ; .5 mg ✗ ; 5 mg ✓ ; 5.0 mg ✗

15. 15. Mind Map

Paediatric Prescribing
Dose Calculation
mg/kg (weight-based)
mg/m2 (BSA - Mosteller)
Neonatal: PMA, corrected age
Obesity: IBW for lipophilic
PK Changes by Age
Neonate: ↑TBW, ↓fat, ↓CYP/UGT, ↓GFR, ↑BBB
Infant: CYP overshoot (2-5x)
Child: Adult-like
Contraindicated
Aspirin <16 (Reye's)
Tetracycline <12 (enamel)
Fluoroquinolone <18 (arthropathy)
Codeine <12 (CYP2D6 UM)
Chloramphenicol neonate (Grey Baby)
Sulfonamide <2mo (Kernicterus)
Formulations
Neonate: IV, liquid PF, rectal
Infant: liquid, dispersible
Child: chewable, dispersible
Avoid: excipients (propylene glycol, benzyl alcohol, ethanol)
Safety
Tenfold errors
Lead zero (0.5)
No trail zero (5)
mcg not µg
Weight in kg only
ADRs
Grey Baby, Reye's, Kernicterus
Growth suppression (ICS)
Adrenal suppression (steroids)
HPS (macrolides neonate)
Off-Label
Most neonates, PPIs <1y, psych <18y
GMC: no alternative, evidence, consent, document

16. 16. Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScore (0–5)Next
Day 11d
Day 33d
Day 71w
Day 142w
Day 301m
Day 903m

17. 17. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore%
Dose calculations (mg/kg, BSA)10
Age-related PK changes8
Contraindicated drugs table12
Formulation selection6
Safety checks (tenfold)8
Common dosing reference10
Paediatric ADRs8
Off-label principles4
Viva answers10
Total76

Target: ≥61/76 (80%)

18. 18. Exam Answer Modes

1. Calculation Viva (2 min): "15kg child, ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IV. Vial 1g. Reconstitute to 100mg/mL. Volume?"

  1. Dose = 15 × 50 = 750 mg
  2. Concentration = 100 mg/mL
  3. Volume = 750 / 100 = 7.5 mL

2. Short Question (5 marks): "Codeine in children — contraindication and mechanism"

  • Contraindicated <12 years (UK); <18 post-tonsillectomy
  • Prodrug → morphine via CYP2D6
  • Ultra-rapid metabolizers → toxic morphine levels → respiratory depression, death

3. Ward Round (30 sec): "2-month-old, ceftriaxone for meningitis. Any issue?"

  • Avoid if jaundiced (bilirubin displacement → kernicterus); use cefotaxime. If not jaundiced, ceftriaxone OK.

4. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Paracetamol 15mg/kg q4–6h (max 60mg/kg/d)
  • Ibuprofen 10mg/kg q6–8h (>3m, >5kg)
  • Aspirin <16 = Reye's
  • Tetracycline <12 = enamel
  • Quinolone <18 = arthropathy
  • Codeine <12 = CYP2D6 UM death
  • Chloramphenicol neonate = Grey Baby
  • Gentamicin extended interval = Hartford 4–5mg/kg q24h
  • Ceftriaxone neonate + jaundice = NO (cefotaxime)
  • Mosteller BSA = √(Ht×Wt/3600)
  • 0.5mg ✓ ; .5mg ✗ ; 5mg ✓ ; 5.0mg ✗

19. 19. Summary Card

PAEDIATRIC PRESCRIBING SAFETY TRIAD:
1. WEIGHT in kg — every prescription, every time
2. LEAD ZERO, NO TRAIL ZERO — 0.5 mg not .5 mg; 5 mg not 5.0 mg
3. FORMULATION SPECIFIED — liquid concentration, dispersible, age-appropriate
BIG 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- Aspirin <16 (Reye's)
- Tetracycline <12 (enamel)
- Fluoroquinolone <18 (arthropathy)
- Codeine <12 (CYP2D6 UM → death)

20. 20. MCQs (15)

#QuestionAnswer
115kg child, ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IV. Vial 1g reconstituted to 100mg/mL. Volume?B. 7.5 mL
2Drug contraindicated <16 years due to Reye's syndrome?C. Aspirin
33-week-old UTI. Avoid which cephalosporin if jaundiced?B. Ceftriaxone
4Codeine contraindicated <12 years because:B. CYP2D6 UM produce toxic morphine
5Grey Baby Syndrome caused by:B. Chloramphenicol
6Mosteller BSA formula:B. √(Ht × Wt / 3600)
76mo infant 7.5kg, paracetamol 120mg/5mL. Dose & volume?B. 15mg/kg = 112.5mg → 4.7 mL q4–6h
8Correct paediatric formulation statement?B. Oral liquids state concentration (mg/mL)
9Tetracyclines avoided <12 years due to:B. Enamel dysplasia & bone growth inhibition
1010yo on high-dose fluticasone 500mcg BD x2y. Monitor?B. Growth velocity 6-monthly
11Correctly written prescription?B. Morphine 0.5 mg IV q4h
12Gentamicin extended interval (Hartford) neonate dose?C. 4–5 mg/kg q24h
13Drug causing hypertrophic pyloric stenosis in neonates <2w?A. Erythromycin
14Ideal body weight used for dosing in obese child for:C. Lipophilic drugs (gentamicin, digoxin, midazolam)
15Off-label prescribing GMC requirement NOT needed?D. Hospital formulary approval

21. 21. SBAs (8)

  1. 4kg term neonate (7 days old), suspected sepsis. Gentamicin dosing per Hartford nomogram?

A. 2.5 mg/kg q12h

B. 4 mg/kg q24h

C. 4–5 mg/kg q24h ✓

D. 7.5 mg/kg q24h

E. 5 mg/kg q36h

Explanation: Extended-interval (Hartford) dosing for neonates: 4–5 mg/kg q24h (q36–48h if preterm/renal impairment). TDM trough <1 mg/L at 18h post-dose.

  1. 3-year-old (15kg) with acute otitis media. Amoxicillin 40mg/kg/day TDS. Suspension 250mg/5mL. Volume per dose?

A. 2 mL

B. 3 mL

C. 4 mL ✓

D. 5 mL

E. 6 mL

Explanation: 15kg × 40 = 600mg/day ÷ 3 = 200mg/dose. 250mg/5mL = 50mg/mL. 200/50 = 4mL.

  1. 6-year-old post-tonsillectomy. Codeine 1mg/kg prescribed. Most appropriate action?

A. Give as prescribed; monitor respiratory rate

B. Reduce dose to 0.5mg/kg

C. Stop codeine; prescribe morphine or paracetamol+ibuprofen ✓

D. Switch to tramadol

E. Check CYP2D6 genotype first

Explanation: Codeine contraindicated <12y (UK) and <18y post-tonsillectomy. CYP2D6 ultra-rapid metabolizers → fatal morphine toxicity.

  1. 2-month-old with UTI, mild jaundice (bilirubin 80 µmol/L). IV antibiotic choice?

A. Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg OD

B. Cefotaxime 50mg/kg q8h ✓

C. Cefuroxime 30mg/kg q8h

D. Gentamicin 5mg/kg OD

E. Amoxicillin 50mg/kg q8h

Explanation: Ceftriaxone displaces bilirubin → kernicterus risk. Cefotaxime preferred in jaundiced infants <28 days corrected.

  1. 12-year-old (40kg) on high-dose inhaled fluticasone 500mcg BD for 3 years. Growth velocity slowed from 5 to 3.5 cm/year. Next step?

A. Stop fluticasone immediately

B. Step down to lowest effective dose; spacer; rinse; monitor growth 6-monthly ✓

C. Switch to oral prednisolone

D. Add growth hormone

E. Continue unchanged; growth catch-up expected

Explanation: ICS growth suppression is dose-related and reversible. Step down, ensure technique (spacer), monitor 6-monthly. Catch-up growth occurs after dose reduction.

  1. 10-year-old with severe asthma exacerbation. Prednisolone dose?

A. 0.5 mg/kg OD

B. 1–2 mg/kg OD (max 60mg) × 3–5 days ✓

C. 2 mg/kg BD × 7 days

D. 0.15 mg/kg OD

E. 5 mg/kg OD

Explanation: Prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg/day (max 60mg) for 3–5 days. No taper if <14 days. Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose alternative for croup.

  1. Neonate (28 weeks corrected, 1.2kg) with apnoea of prematurity. Caffeine citrate loading dose?

A. 5 mg/kg IV

B. 10 mg/kg IV

C. 20 mg/kg IV ✓

D. 30 mg/kg IV

E. 50 mg/kg IV

Explanation: Caffeine citrate load 20 mg/kg (equivalent to 10 mg/kg caffeine base) → maintenance 5–10 mg/kg/day OD. TDM not routine.

  1. 8-year-old (25kg) needs IV morphine for severe pain. Prescription: "Morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV q4h PRN". Vial 10mg/mL. Volume per dose?

A. 0.1 mL

B. 0.25 mL ✓

C. 0.5 mL

D. 2.5 mL

E. 25 mL

Explanation: 0.1 × 25 = 2.5mg. 10mg/mL → 2.5/10 = 0.25mL. Safety: lead zero (0.25 not .25), max dose documented.

22. 22. Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
Paracetamol paediatric dose15mg/kg q4–6h PO/PR (max 60mg/kg/day)
Ibuprofen paediatric dose10mg/kg q6–8h PO (>3m, >5kg)
Aspirin contraindication age<16 years (Reye's syndrome)
Tetracycline contraindication age<12 years (enamel dysplasia, bone growth inhibition)
Fluoroquinolone contraindication age<18 years (arthropathy, cartilage damage)
Codeine contraindication age<12 years (CYP2D6 UM → morphine toxicity death)
Chloramphenicol neonate ADRGrey Baby Syndrome (↓ UGT → CV collapse)
Sulfonamide neonate ADRKernicterus (bilirubin displacement)
Gentamicin neonate dosingExtended interval: 4–5mg/kg q24h (Hartford); trough <1 at 18h
Ceftriaxone neonate + jaundiceAVOID (bilirubin displacement → kernicterus); use cefotaxime
Mosteller BSA formula√(Height cm × Weight kg / 3600)
Lead zero / trail zero rule0.5mg ✓ ; .5mg ✗ ; 5mg ✓ ; 5.0mg ✗
kg weight documentationMandatory on every paediatric prescription
Off-label vs unlicensedOff-label = licensed drug outside MA; Unlicensed = no UK MA
GMC off-label requirementsNo alternative, evidence, consent, document, monitor
Growth velocity monitoring ICS6-monthly if high dose/long-term
Macroide neonate <2w ADRHypertrophic pyloric stenosis (↑ risk 7-10x)
Ideal body weight forLipophilic drugs in obesity: gentamicin, digoxin, midazolam

23. 23. Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — 15×50=750mg; 750/100=7.5mL
  2. C — Aspirin + viral = Reye's
  3. B — Ceftriaxone displaces bilirubin
  4. B — CYP2D6 UM → toxic morphine
  5. B — Chloramphenicol ↓ UGT in neonates
  6. B — Mosteller = √(Ht×Wt/3600)
  7. B — 7.5×15=112.5mg; 112.5/24=4.69mL
  8. B — Concentration on Rx mandatory
  9. B — Calcium chelation in teeth/bones
  10. B — Growth velocity 6-monthly
  11. B — Lead zero required; no trail zero
  12. C — Hartford 4–5mg/kg q24h
  13. A — Erythromycin/azithromycin <2w
  14. C — Lipophilic drugs in obesity
  15. D — GMC doesn't require formulary approval

2. SBAs

  1. C — Hartford 4–5mg/kg q24h (extend if preterm/renal impairment)
  2. C — 200mg/dose ÷ 50mg/mL = 4mL
  3. C — Codeine contraindicated <12y; CYP2D6 UM risk death
  4. B — Cefotaxime preferred in jaundiced neonate
  5. B — Step down, spacer, rinse, monitor 6-monthly
  6. B — Prednisolone 1–2mg/kg OD max 60mg × 3–5d
  7. C — Caffeine citrate load 20mg/kg (10mg/kg base)
  8. B — 0.1×25=2.5mg; 2.5/10=0.25mL

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.4.05 Pregnancy And Lactation

Parent Topic: Prescribing in Special Populations → Clinical Therapeutics Overview

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (FCPS/MRCP — FDA pregnancy categories, teratogens, lactation safety, physiological changes)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 2; BNF Pregnancy & Breastfeeding; UKTIS (UK Teratology Information Service); FDA/EMA Guidance; LactMed (NIH); Briggs Drugs in Pregnancy & Lactation; RCOG Green-top Guidelines

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Understand physiological PK/PD changes in pregnancy affecting drug disposition
  • [ ] Apply FDA Pregnancy Categories (old) & new PLLR labelling (Pregnancy, Lactation, Females/Males of Reproductive Potential)
  • [ ] Identify major teratogens (FDA X / contraindicated) and critical periods (organogenesis: 3–8 weeks)
  • [ ] Apply safe prescribing principles: avoid if possible, use lowest effective dose, prefer drugs with long safety record
  • [ ] Know lactation safety: infant dose via milk (RID), L1–L5 categories, drugs to avoid
  • [ ] Apply specific disease management in pregnancy: epilepsy, hypertension, diabetes, asthma, depression, thrombosis, infections
  • [ ] Answer viva: "Which antihypertensives are safe in pregnancy?" and "Teratogens to avoid"

2. 2. 🧠 Core Concept: PK/PD Changes in Pregnancy

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Pregnancy Physiological Changes
↓
PK Changes
PD Changes
↓
↑ Plasma volume 40–50% ↓ Albumin → ↑ Vd (hydrophilic) ↑ Free fraction (protein-bound)
↑ GFR 50% → ↑ Renal clearance ↑ Creatinine clearance ↓ Drug levels: penicillins, atenolol, digoxin, lithium, gabapentin
↑ Hepatic blood flow ↑ CYP3A4, CYP2D6, CYP2C9 ↑ UGT (glucuronidation) ↑ Clearance: lamotrigine, phenytoin, carbamazepine, methadone, buprenorphine
↓ Gastric motility / ↑ pH ↓ Absorption (some drugs) ↑ Bioavailability (others)
↑ Fat mass → ↑ Vd (lipophilic) ↑ Progesterone → ↓ GI transit
↓ Sensitivity to warfarin (↑ clotting factors)
↑ Insulin resistance → ↑ insulin requirement
↓ BP (vasodilation) → ↓ antihypertensive requirement 1st/2nd tri

1. Critical Periods for Teratogenicity

PeriodWeeksSensitivityExamples
Pre-implantation0–2 (fertilisation to implantation)All-or-nothing (embryo dies or survives unharmed)High dose radiation, chemotherapy
Organogenesis3–8 weeks (from LMP: 5–10 weeks)HIGHEST — structural malformationsThalidomide (limb), Isotretinoin (CNS/heart), Valproate (NTD), Warfarin (chondrodysplasia), ACEi (renal), Methotrexate (multiple)
Fetal period9 weeks – termGrowth restriction, functional deficits, neonatal toxicityNSAIDs (premature ductus closure), ACEi (renal dysplasia), SSRIs (PPHN), Benzos (floppy infant), Opioids (NAS)
Viva Key: Organogenesis 3–8 weeks post-conception = 5–10 weeks LMP. This is when structural teratogens act. After 8 weeks = functional/growth effects.

3. 3. ️⃣ FDA Pregnancy Categories (Historical) & PLLR (Current)

1. Old FDA Categories (Still Widely Referenced in Exams)

CategoryDefinitionExamples
AAdequate human studies show no riskLevothyroxine, Folic acid, Pyridoxine, Insulin (human)
BAnimal studies no risk, no human studies OR animal risk but human studies no riskAmoxicillin, Cephalosporins, Metformin, Paracetamol, Metoprolol, Heparin, LMWH
CAnimal studies show risk, no human studies OR no animal/human dataFluoxetine, Sertraline, Amlodipine, Furosemide, Prednisolone, Codeine, Tramadol
DHuman evidence of risk, but benefits may outweigh (life-threatening)Valproate, Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Warfarin, ACEi, ARB, DOACs, Statins, Tetracyclines, Sulfonamides (3rd tri), Lithium
XContraindicated — risk > benefitIsotretinoin, Thalidomide, Misoprostol, Methotrexate, Ribavirin, Statins (some), Estradiol (high dose), Finasteride
Exam Note: FCPS/MRCP still use old FDA categories. Know D vs X: D = may use if life-threatening (e.g., warfarin for mechanical valve); X = never use (e.g., isotretinoin, thalidomide).

2. New FDA PLLR (Pregnancy and Lactation Labelling Rule) — 2015+

SectionContent
PregnancyRisk summary, clinical considerations, data (human/animal)
LactationDrug in milk, infant effects, milk production effects
Females/Males of Reproductive PotentialContraception, infertility, pregnancy testing

No letter categories — narrative-based. But exam questions still use A/B/C/D/X.

4. 4. ️⃣ Major Teratogens (Contraindicated / Avoid) — HIGH YIELD

1. Absolute Contraindications (FDA X / Category D with No Alternative)

DrugTeratogenic EffectCritical Period
IsotretinoinCNS (hydrocephalus, microcephaly), cardiac, thymic, craniofacial3–8 weeks
ThalidomidePhocomelia (limb reduction), ear/eye/heart defects4–7 weeks (limb bud)
MethotrexateMultiple: craniofacial, limb, CNS, cardiac (methotrexate syndrome)6–8 weeks
RibavirinMultiple congenital anomaliesThroughout
MycophenolateEar, eye, limb, heart, oesophageal atresiaThroughout
Finasteride / DutasterideHypospadias, ambiguous genitalia (male fetus)8–14 weeks (genital differentiation)

2. Major Teratogens (Category D — Use Only If No Alternative)

DrugTeratogenic EffectManagement in Pregnancy
ValproateNeural tube defects (1–2%), cardiac, craniofacial, developmental delay, autism (↑ dose)AVOID; switch pre-conception to lamotrigine/levetiracetam; if pregnant: folic acid 5mg, lowest effective dose, monitor levels
CarbamazepineNeural tube defects (0.5–1%), craniofacial, cardiac, hypospadiasFolic acid 5mg; lowest dose; TDM; consider switch to lamotrigine/levetiracetam
PhenytoinFetal hydantoin syndrome: craniofacial, limb, cardiac, NTD, growth restrictionFolic acid 5mg; TDM (FREE level); avoid if possible
WarfarinWarfarin embryopathy (6–12%): chondrodysplasia punctata (stippled epiphyses), nasal hypoplasia, CNS, eye; Fetal intracranial haemorrhageSwitch to LMWH pre-conception or ASAP; if mechanical mitral valve: LMWH 1st tri, warfarin 2nd/3rd tri with close INR, switch back LMWH 36w
ACEi / ARBFetal renal dysplasia, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia, skull hypoplasia (2nd/3rd tri)STOP immediately if pregnancy confirmed; switch to methyldopa/labetalol/nifedipine
DOACsLimited data; animal fetal bleedingContraindicated; switch to LMWH
StatinsTheoretical (cholesterol needed for fetal development); limited human dataContraindicated; stop pre-conception
LithiumEbstein's anomaly (0.1–0.2%) — tricuspid valve dysplasia; neonatal toxicity (floppy, hypothyroidism, diabetes insipidus)Avoid 1st tri if possible; if essential: lowest dose, folic acid, fetal echo 18–20w, monitor maternal levels
TetracyclinesTooth discoloration, enamel hypoplasia, bone growth inhibition (2nd/3rd tri)Avoid after 12 weeks; doxycycline short course <1st tri if essential
Sulfonamides / TMP-SMXKernicterus (displace bilirubin) — 3rd trimester; NTD risk (folate antagonism) 1st triAvoid 1st tri (folate antagonism) and 3rd tri (kernicterus)
FluoroquinolonesCartilage damage (animal) — arthropathyAvoid; use alternatives
AminoglycosidesOtotoxicity (vestibular > auditory) — 8th cranial nerveAvoid if possible; if essential: monitor hearing, shortest course

5. 5. ️⃣ Safe Drugs in Pregnancy (Common Indications)

1. Hypertension

PreferredAvoid
Methyldopa (1st line, most evidence)ACEi / ARB (D)
Labetalol (1st line, β+α)Thiazides (C — reduce placental perfusion)
Nifedipine MR (CCB, 2nd line)Spironolactone (anti-androgen)
Hydralazine (acute severe)Atenolol (↓ fetal growth — avoid 1st line)
Metoprolol (if β-blocker needed)
Viva Key: Methyldopa & Labetalol = 1st line. Nifedipine = 2nd line. ACEi/ARB = STOP immediately (renal dysplasia). Thiazides = reduce placental perfusion.

2. Anticoagulation (AF, VTE, Mechanical Valve)

IndicationRegimen
VTE treatment / prophylaxisLMWH (enoxaparin/dalteparin) throughout — preferred (no placental transfer, no fetal bleeding)
AF (CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2)LMWH throughout; or warfarin 2nd/3rd tri if high risk
Mechanical mitral valveLMWH 1st trimester, Warfarin 2nd/3rd (if INR control good), LMWH last 4 weeks
Mechanical aortic valveLMWH throughout (or warfarin 2nd/3rd if high thromboembolic risk)
Warfarin dose >5mg/dayConsider LMWH throughout (↑ embryopathy risk)

3. Epilepsy

PreferredAvoid / Caution
Lamotrigine (↑ clearance in pregnancy → TDM dose ↑)Valproate (D — NTD, neurodevelopmental)
Levetiracetam (↑ clearance → TDM dose ↑)Carbamazepine (D — NTD, enzyme induction → vit K deficiency)
Topiramate (C — cleft lip/palate, growth restriction)Phenytoin (D — fetal hydantoin syndrome)
Folic acid 5mg daily pre-conception & 1st tri for ALLPhenobarbital (D — similar to phenytoin)
Vitamin K 10mg IM at birth (if on enzyme inducers)

4. Diabetes

PreferredAvoid
Insulin (human, aspart, lispro, detemir, glargine) — Gold standardMetformin (B — but crosses placenta; used in PCOS/GDM but insulin preferred for T1DM/T2DM)
Metformin (B — increasingly used in GDM, PCOS)Sulfonylureas (C/D — glibenclamide crosses placenta; gliclazide less)
SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, DPP-4, Pioglitazone — insufficient data (avoid)

5. Asthma

PreferredAvoid
SABA (salbutamol) — relieverNone — uncontrolled asthma > drug risk
ICS (budesonide > beclomethasone > fluticasone) — controller
LABA (salmeterol/formoterol) — add-on
LTRA (montelukast) — add-on
Theophylline — narrow TI, monitor
Oral steroids — if severe (prednisolone)

6. Depression / Anxiety

PreferredAvoid / Caution
Sertraline (SSRI, most data)Paroxetine (D — cardiac malformations)
Fluoxetine (SSRI, long half-life)Venlafaxine (C — hypertension, neonatal withdrawal)
Citalopram/Escitalopram (C — QT)TCAs (C — anticholinergic, cardiac)
Mirtazapine (C — limited data)MAOIs — avoid
CBT / psychological therapy — 1st line mild-moderateBenzodiazepines (D — floppy infant, withdrawal, cleft palate?)
Neonatal Adaptation Syndrome (SSRI 3rd tri): Jitteriness, irritability, feeding difficulty, respiratory distress, hypoglycaemia — self-limiting 2–14 days. Not neonatal withdrawal.

7. Infections — Antibiotics

Safe (B)Caution (C)Avoid (D/X)
Penicillins (amoxicillin, flucloxacillin, co-amoxiclav)Macrolides (erythromycin, clarithromycin) — QT, hepatotoxicityTetracyclines (D)
Cephalosporins (cefalexin, cefuroxime, ceftriaxone)Metronidazole (C — avoid 1st tri if possible; short course OK)Fluoroquinolones (C/D)
Carbapenems (meropenem)Nitrofurantoin (C — avoid near term: haemolytic anaemia G6PD)Aminoglycosides (D — ototoxicity)
Azithromycin (B — preferred macrolide)Sulfonamides/TMP-SMX (D 1st/3rd tri)
Clindamycin (B)Rifampicin (C — hepatotoxicity, enzyme induction)
Vancomycin (B)

6. 6. ️⃣ Lactation Prescribing

1. Lactation Risk Categories (Hale's Medication & Mothers' Milk)

CategoryDefinitionExamples
L1 (Safest)No risk in controlled studiesParacetamol, Ibuprofen, Penicillins, Cephalosporins, Insulin, Heparin, LMWH, Levothyroxine, Folic acid
L2 (Safer)Limited studies, no adverse effectsMetformin, Sertraline, Labetalol, Nifedipine, Atenolol, Metoprolol, Azithromycin, Clarithromycin
L3 (Moderate Risk)No controlled studies, potential riskFluoxetine, Citalopram, Venlafaxine, Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Valproate (monitor infant), Warfarin, Prednisolone, Codeine
L4 (Possibly Hazardous)Evidence of riskDiazepam (sedation), Lithium (↑ infant levels), Amiodarone (iodine), Gold, Methotrexate
L5 (Contraindicated)Significant riskCocaine, Heroin, Radioactives, Cancer chemo, Ergotamine, Bromocriptine (suppresses lactation)

2. Infant Dose via Milk — Relative Infant Dose (RID)

RID (%) = (Infant dose via milk / Maternal weight-adjusted dose) × 100
  • RID <10% = Generally safe (most drugs)
  • RID >10% = Caution (lithium ~50%, diazepam ~10%, amiodarone high iodine)
  • Premature/neonate — lower threshold (immature metabolism)

3. Drugs to AVOID in Breastfeeding

DrugReason
LithiumExcreted in milk (RID ~50%); infant toxicity (hypotonia, hypothyroidism, renal)
AmiodaroneHigh iodine in milk → infant thyroid dysfunction
Diazepam / Long-acting benzosSedation, poor feeding, weight loss (accumulation)
CodeineUltra-rapid metabolisers (CYP2D6) → morphine → infant respiratory depression
AspirinReye's syndrome risk (viral illness)
Radioisotopes / ChemoDirect toxicity
Ergotamine / Bromocriptine / CabergolineSuppress lactation
High-dose iodineInfant thyroid suppression

4. Preferred Alternatives for Breastfeeding

ConditionAvoidPreferred
AnalgesiaCodeine, AspirinParacetamol, Ibuprofen (L1)
DepressionFluoxetine (long half-life)Sertraline, Paroxetine (low milk transfer)
AnticoagulationWarfarin (monitor infant INR)LMWH, Heparin (no milk transfer)
HypertensionAtenolol (high milk transfer)Labetalol, Nifedipine, Methyldopa
EpilepsyValproate (monitor infant LFTs/blood)Lamotrigine, Levetiracetam (monitor infant levels)
ContraceptionCombined OCP (↓ milk supply)Progesterone-only (POP, implant, IUS), Copper IUD

7. 7. ️⃣ Pre-Conception Prescribing

1. For All Women of Childbearing Potential

ActionDetails
Medication reviewApply STOPP/START; identify teratogens (D/X)
Folic acid 400μg daily5mg if: epilepsy (AEDs), diabetes, BMI >30, previous NTD, family history, on folate antagonists (methotrexate, sulfonamides, TMP-SMX)
Switch teratogens pre-conceptionValproate → lamotrigine/levetiracetam; Warfarin → LMWH; ACEi/ARB → methyldopa/labetalol; Statins → stop; Isotretinoin → stop 1 month before
Rubella/Varicella immunityVaccinate if non-immune (live vaccines — avoid pregnancy 1 month)
ThyroidTSH <2.5 mU/L pre-conception; levothyroxine dose ↑ 25–30% in pregnancy
DiabetesHbA1c <48 mmol/mol (6.5%) pre-conception; insulin optimisation
EpilepsyMonotherapy if possible; lowest effective dose; folic acid 5mg; TDM baseline

8. 8. ️⃣ Specific High-Yield Clinical Scenarios

1. Scenario 1: Woman on Valproate for Epilepsy Becomes Pregnant

  1. Do NOT stop abruptly — seizure risk > teratogen risk
  2. Urgent neurology review — consider switch to lamotrigine/levetiracetam (slow cross-taper)
  3. Folic acid 5mg daily immediately
  4. TDM — valproate levels (↑ clearance in pregnancy)
  5. Fetal anomaly scan (18–20w) + fetal echo
  6. Vitamin K 10mg IM at birth (enzyme induction → vit K deficiency)

2. Scenario 2: Woman on Warfarin for Mechanical Mitral Valve Plans Pregnancy

  1. Pre-conception: Switch to therapeutic LMWH (enoxaparin 1mg/kg BD)
  2. If pregnant on warfarin: Switch to LMWH immediately (1st tri = highest embryopathy risk)
  3. 2nd/3rd trimester: Can continue LMWH OR switch to warfarin (if INR stable, dose ≤5mg) with close INR monitoring
  4. 36 weeks: Switch back to LMWH (reduce intracranial haemorrhage risk at delivery)
  5. Postpartum: Restart warfarin + LMWH bridging

3. Scenario 3: Hypertensive Woman Becomes Pregnant on Ramipril

  1. STOP ACEi immediately (renal dysplasia risk 2nd/3rd tri)
  2. Switch to methyldopa or labetalol (1st line)
  3. Monitor BP weekly; target <140/90 (or <135/85 if end-organ damage)
  4. Aspirin 75–150mg OD from 12 weeks (pre-eclampsia prophylaxis if risk factors)

4. Scenario 4: Breastfeeding Mother Needs Antidepressant

  1. Sertraline — lowest milk transfer (RID <1%), most safety data
  2. Paroxetine — low milk transfer, short half-life
  3. Avoid fluoxetine — long half-life, active metabolite, higher infant levels
  4. Monitor infant: drowsiness, feeding, weight gain

9. 9. ⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Critical teratogenic periodOrganogenesis: 3–8 weeks post-conception (5–10 weeks LMP)
FDA Categories (exam)A=safe, B=animal safe/no human, C=animal risk, D=human risk (may use if benefit>risk), X=contraindicated
Absolute teratogens (X)Isotretinoin, Thalidomide, Methotrexate, Ribavirin, Mycophenolate, Finasteride
Major teratogens (D)Valproate (NTD, neurodevelopmental), Carbamazepine/Phenytoin (NTD, syndromes), Warfarin (chondrodysplasia, ICH), ACEi/ARB (renal dysplasia), DOACs, Statins, Lithium (Ebstein's), Tetracyclines (teeth/bone), Sulfonamides (kernicterus 3rd tri, NTD 1st tri), Fluoroquinolones (cartilage), Aminoglycosides (ototoxicity)
HTN in pregnancy1st line: Methyldopa, Labetalol; 2nd line: Nifedipine MR; Avoid: ACEi/ARB, Thiazides (↓ placental perfusion), Atenolol (↓ growth)
AnticoagulationLMWH throughout for VTE/AF; Mechanical valve: LMWH 1st tri, Warfarin 2nd/3rd (if dose ≤5mg), LMWH last 4w
EpilepsyLamotrigine, Levetiracetam preferred; Valproate AVOID; Folic acid 5mg; TDM (↑ clearance); Vit K at birth if enzyme inducers
DiabetesInsulin gold standard; Metformin B (used in GDM); Avoid SGLT2i/GLP-1/DPP-4/Pioglitazone
AsthmaUncontrolled asthma > drug risk; Budesonide > beclomethasone > fluticasone for ICS
DepressionSertraline preferred; Avoid paroxetine (cardiac); Neonatal adaptation syndrome (self-limiting)
AntibioticsSafe: Penicillins, Cephalosporins, Carbapenems, Azithromycin, Clindamycin, Vancomycin; Avoid: Tetracyclines, Fluoroquinolones, Aminoglycosides, Sulfonamides (1st/3rd tri)
LactationL1/L2 preferred (paracetamol, ibuprofen, penicillins, sertraline, labetalol, LMWH); Avoid L4/L5: Lithium, Amiodarone, Diazepam, Codeine, Aspirin, Combined OCP (↓ supply)
Pre-conceptionFolic acid 400μg (5mg if high risk); Switch teratogens; Rubella/varicella immunity; TSH <2.5; HbA1c <48

10. 10. 🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1What is the critical period for structural teratogenicity?Organogenesis: 3–8 weeks post-conception (5–10 weeks LMP). Pre-implantation = all-or-nothing. Fetal period = growth/functional effects.
2Difference between FDA Category D and X?D: Human evidence of risk, but may use if benefit > risk (e.g., warfarin for mechanical valve). X: Contraindicated — risk always > benefit (e.g., isotretinoin, thalidomide, methotrexate).
3List 5 drugs contraindicated in pregnancy (Category X).Isotretinoin, Thalidomide, Methotrexate, Ribavirin, Mycophenolate, Finasteride.
4Woman on valproate for epilepsy becomes pregnant. Management?Do not stop abruptly. Urgent neuro review; switch to lamotrigine/levetiracetam; folic acid 5mg; TDM; anomaly scan 18–20w; Vit K at birth.
5First-line antihypertensives in pregnancy?Methyldopa (most evidence) and Labetalol. Nifedipine MR 2nd line. Avoid ACEi/ARB, thiazides, atenolol.
6Anticoagulation for mechanical mitral valve in pregnancy?LMWH 1st trimester, Warfarin 2nd/3rd (if dose ≤5mg, good INR control), LMWH from 36 weeks. Or LMWH throughout.
7Why avoid ACE inhibitors in 2nd/3rd trimester?Fetal renal dysplasia → oligohydramnios → pulmonary hypoplasia, skull hypoplasia, neonatal renal failure.
8Which SSRI is preferred in pregnancy?Sertraline (most safety data). Avoid paroxetine (Category D — cardiac malformations).
9Lithium in pregnancy — risks and management if essential?Ebstein's anomaly (0.1–0.2%) 1st tri; neonatal toxicity (floppy, hypothyroidism, DI). If essential: lowest dose, folic acid 5mg, fetal echo 18–20w, monitor maternal levels, avoid breastfeeding.
10Breastfeeding: which drugs to avoid?Lithium (high milk transfer, infant toxicity), Amiodarone (iodine), Diazepam (sedation), Codeine (respiratory depression in ultra-rapid metabolisers), Aspirin (Reye's), Combined OCP (↓ milk supply).
11Folic acid dose in epilepsy/on AEDs?5mg daily (not 400μg) — pre-conception through 1st trimester.
12Warfarin embryopathy features?Chondrodysplasia punctata (stippled epiphyses), nasal hypoplasia, CNS abnormalities, eye defects. Fetal intracranial haemorrhage.
13Phenytoin monitoring in pregnancy?FREE phenytoin level (↑ clearance, ↓ albumin). Total level misleading.
14Metformin in pregnancy — category and use?Category B. Increasingly used in GDM and PCOS; crosses placenta but no teratogenicity. Insulin remains gold standard for T1DM/T2DM.
15Neonatal adaptation syndrome — which drugs, when?SSRIs (especially 3rd trimester) — jitteriness, irritability, feeding difficulty, respiratory distress, hypoglycaemia. Self-limiting 2–14 days. Not withdrawal.

11. 11. 🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"Category B = safe"B = animal studies no risk, no adequate human studies OR animal risk but human studies no risk. "Safe" based on indirect evidence.
"Category C = unsafe"C = animal risk, no human data OR no data. Many C drugs used if benefit > risk (e.g., fluoxetine, amlodipine).
"All statins Category X"Some statins Category X (simvastatin, atorvastatin), some Category D. All contraindicated in pregnancy — cholesterol needed for fetal development.
"Warfarin safe in 2nd/3rd trimester"Warfarin = CNS defects (microcephaly, Dandy-Walker), fetal ICH throughout. 1st tri = embryopathy. 2nd/3rd = CNS/bleeding. Only use if mechanical mitral valve + dose ≤5mg + good INR control.
"Metformin contraindicated in pregnancy"Category B — NOT contraindicated. Used in GDM, PCOS. Crosses placenta but no teratogenicity. Insulin preferred for T1DM/T2DM.
"Breastfeeding safe for all drugs"No — lithium, amiodarone, diazepam, codeine, aspirin, chemo, radioisotopes contraindicated. Check Hale's L1–L5.
"Combined OCP OK in breastfeeding"Oestrogen ↓ milk supply. Use progesterone-only (POP, implant, IUS) or copper IUD.
"Tetracyclines OK in 1st trimester"Avoid ALL trimesters — tooth/bone effects from 2nd tri, but also hepatotoxicity maternal. Doxycycline short course <1st tri if essential (e.g., rickettsial).
"Vaccines contraindicated in pregnancy"Inactivated vaccines SAFE & RECOMMENDED (flu, pertussis/Tdap, COVID). Live vaccines contraindicated (MMR, varicella, yellow fever, BCG, LAIV).
Mnemonic: PREGNANT PRESCRIBING
Period critical: Organogenesis 3–8 weeks (5–10 wks LMP)
Review meds pre-conception: Switch D/X teratogens
Epilepsy: Valproate AVOID → Lamotrigine/Levetiracetam; Folic acid 5mg
Gestational HTN: Methyldopa/Labetalol 1st line; Nifedipine 2nd; ACEi/ARB STOP
No NSAIDs 3rd tri (ductus closure); No tetracyclines (teeth); No fluoroquinolones (cartilage)
Anticoagulation: LMWH throughout for VTE/AF; Mech valve: LMWH 1st, Warfarin 2nd/3rd, LMWH 36w
Neonatal adaptation: SSRIs 3rd tri → jitteriness, self-limiting (not withdrawal)
Teratogens X: Isotretinoin, Thalidomide, Methotrexate, Ribavirin, Mycophenolate, Finasteride
Teratogens D: Valproate, CBZ, PHT, Warfarin, ACEi/ARB, DOACs, Statins, Lithium, Tetracyclines, Sulfonamides, Aminoglycosides
Lactation: L1/L2 safe (paracetamol, ibuprofen, penicillins, sertraline, labetalol, LMWH)
Avoid L4/L5: Lithium, Amiodarone, Diazepam, Codeine, Aspirin, Combined OCP
Conception: Folic acid 400μg (5mg if AED/DM/BMI>30/NTD hx); Rubella/varicella immunity; TSH<2.5; HbA1c<48
TDM in pregnancy: Lamotrigine/Levetiracetam/Valproate/CBZ/PHT ↑ clearance → TDM dose ↑
Insulin: Gold standard DM; Metformin B (GDM/PCOS); Avoid SGLT2i/GLP-1/DPP-4/Pio
Neonatal Vit K: 10mg IM if mother on enzyme inducers (CBZ, PHT, PB, Rifampicin)
Guidelines: BNF, UKTIS, LactMed, RCOG, FDA PLLR

12. 12. 🗺️ Mind Map

root((Pregnancy &
Lactation))
Critical Periods
Pre-implantation (0-2w): All-or-nothing
Organogenesis (3-8w): HIGHEST teratogen risk
Fetal period (9w-term): Growth/functional
FDA Categories (Exam)
A: Safe (levothyroxine, folic acid, insulin)
B: Animal safe (penicillins, metformin, paracetamol, LMWH)
C: Animal risk (fluoxetine, amlodipine, furosemide)
D: Human risk (valproate, warfarin, ACEi, lithium, statins)
X: Contraindicated (isotretinoin, thalidomide, methotrexate)
Major Teratogens
X: Isotretinoin, Thalidomide, MTX, Ribavirin, MMF, Finasteride
D: Valproate (NTD, neuro), CBZ/PHT (NTD, syndromes), Warfarin (chondrodysplasia), ACEi/ARB (renal), DOACs, Statins, Lithium (Ebstein), Tetracyclines, Sulfonamides, FQ, Aminoglycosides
Disease Management
HTN: Methyldopa/Labetalol 1st; Nifedipine 2nd
Anticoag: LMWH throughout; Mech valve LMWH 1st/Warfarin 2-3rd/LMWH 36w
Epilepsy: LTG/LEV preferred; VPA avoid; Folate 5mg; TDM; Vit K birth
DM: Insulin gold; Metformin B (GDM); Avoid others
Asthma: Uncontrolled > drugs; Budesonide ICS
Depression: Sertraline preferred; Paroxetine avoid; Neonatal adaptation
Antibiotics: Pen/Ceph/Carb/Azi/Clinda/Van safe; Avoid Tetra/FQ/AG/Sulfa
Lactation
L1/L2: Paracetamol, Ibuprofen, Penicillins, Sertraline, Labetalol, LMWH
L4/L5 AVOID: Lithium, Amiodarone, Diazepam, Codeine, Aspirin, Combined OCP
Pre-conception
Folate 400μg (5mg high risk); Switch teratogens; Immunity; TSH<2.5; HbA1c<48

13. 13. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScore (0–5)Notes
Day 1
Day 3
Day 7
Day 14
Day 30
Day 90

14. 14. 📝 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore%
Critical periods / FDA categories3
Top 10 teratogens (X + D)4
HTN / Anticoagulation in pregnancy3
Epilepsy / Diabetes / Depression3
Antibiotics safe/avoid2
Lactation (L1–L5, avoid drugs)2

Total20

15. 15. 💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Discuss prescribing in pregnancy with focus on teratogenicity and disease management."Critical periods, FDA categories, major teratogens (X/D), HTN/anticoag/epilepsy/DM/asthma/depression management, antibiotics, lactation, pre-conception
Short Note"Warfarin in pregnancy."Embryopathy 1st tri (chondrodysplasia), fetal ICH 2nd/3rd, mechanical valve: LMWH 1st, warfarin 2nd/3rd if dose≤5mg, LMWH 36w
Viva"28F on valproate 1000mg for epilepsy plans pregnancy. Counsel."Valproate NTD 1–2%, neurodevelopmental delay. Switch pre-conception to LTG/LEV. Folate 5mg. If pregnant: don't stop, urgent neuro, TDM, anomaly scan.
Ward Round"Pregnant woman at 32 weeks on ramipril 10mg for HTN."STOP ramipril immediately (renal dysplasia, oligohydramnios). Switch to methyldopa/labetalol/nifedipine. Monitor BP.
Last-Night"Teratogens: 3 X, 5 D. HTN: 2 drugs. Anticoag: mech valve regimen. Lactation: 3 avoid."X: isotretinoin, thalidomide, methotrexate. D: valproate, warfarin, ACEi, lithium, carbamazepine. HTN: methyldopa, labetalol. Mech valve: LMWH 1st, warfarin 2-3rd, LMWH 36w. Lactation avoid: lithium, amiodarone, diazepam.

16. 16. 📌 Summary

  • Critical period: Organogenesis 3–8 weeks (5–10 weeks LMP) = highest structural teratogen risk
  • FDA Categories: A (safe), B (animal safe), C (animal risk), D (human risk, may use if benefit>risk), X (contraindicated)
  • Category X (avoid always): Isotretinoin, Thalidomide, Methotrexate, Ribavirin, Mycophenolate, Finasteride
  • Category D (major teratogens): Valproate (NTD, neuro), Carbamazepine/Phenytoin (NTD, syndromes), Warfarin (chondrodysplasia, ICH), ACEi/ARB (renal dysplasia), DOACs, Statins, Lithium (Ebstein's), Tetracyclines (teeth/bone), Sulfonamides (kernicterus 3rd tri), Fluoroquinolones (cartilage), Aminoglycosides (ototoxicity)
  • HTN: Methyldopa/Labetalol 1st line; Nifedipine 2nd; ACEi/ARB/Thiazides/Atenolol avoid
  • Anticoagulation: LMWH throughout for VTE/AF; Mechanical valve: LMWH 1st tri, Warfarin 2nd/3rd (if ≤5mg), LMWH 36w
  • Epilepsy: Lamotrigine/Levetiracetam preferred; Valproate avoid; Folic acid 5mg; TDM (↑ clearance); Vit K at birth if enzyme inducers
  • Diabetes: Insulin gold standard; Metformin B (GDM); Others avoid
  • Depression: Sertraline preferred; Paroxetine avoid (cardiac); Neonatal adaptation syndrome (self-limiting)
  • Antibiotics: Safe — Penicillins, Cephalosporins, Carbapenems, Azithromycin, Clindamycin, Vancomycin; Avoid — Tetracyclines, Fluoroquinolones, Aminoglycosides, Sulfonamides
  • Lactation: L1/L2 safe (paracetamol, ibuprofen, penicillins, sertraline, labetalol, LMWH); Avoid L4/L5: Lithium, Amiodarone, Diazepam, Codeine, Aspirin, Combined OCP
  • Pre-conception: Folic acid 400μg (5mg high risk); Switch teratogens; Immunity; TSH<2.5; HbA1c<48

17. 17. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Critical period for structural teratogenicity:

A. 0–2 weeks B. 3–8 weeks C. 9–12 weeks D. 2nd trimester

Answer: B. Organogenesis 3–8 weeks post-conception (5–10 weeks LMP).

  1. FDA Category X drug (contraindicated in pregnancy):

A. Warfarin B. Valproate C. Isotretinoin D. Lithium

Answer: C. Isotretinoin = Category X. Warfarin/Valproate/Lithium = Category D.

  1. First-line antihypertensive in pregnancy:

A. Ramipril B. Methyldopa C. Atenolol D. Hydrochlorothiazide

Answer: B. Methyldopa (most evidence) and Labetalol are 1st line. ACEi/ARB contraindicated.

  1. Anticoagulation for mechanical mitral valve in pregnancy:

A. Warfarin throughout B. DOAC throughout C. LMWH 1st tri, Warfarin 2nd/3rd (if dose≤5mg), LMWH 36w D. Aspirin only

Answer: C. Standard regimen. Or LMWH throughout.

  1. Preferred antiepileptic in pregnancy:

A. Valproate B. Carbamazepine C. Lamotrigine D. Phenytoin

Answer: C. Lamotrigine (and Levetiracetam) preferred. Valproate/CBZ/PHT = Category D teratogens.

  1. Folic acid dose for woman on antiepileptics planning pregnancy:

A. 400μg B. 5mg C. 1mg D. 10mg

Answer: B. 5mg daily for high-risk (AEDs, diabetes, BMI>30, previous NTD, family history, folate antagonists).

  1. SSRI with most safety data in pregnancy:

A. Paroxetine B. Fluoxetine C. Sertraline D. Citalopram

Answer: C. Sertraline preferred. Paroxetine = Category D (cardiac malformations).

  1. Antibiotic contraindicated in pregnancy (Category D):

A. Amoxicillin B. Cephalexin C. Doxycycline D. Azithromycin

Answer: C. Tetracyclines = Category D (tooth discoloration, bone growth inhibition).

  1. Drug to AVOID in breastfeeding (Hale L4/L5):

A. Paracetamol B. Ibuprofen C. Lithium D. Sertraline

Answer: C. Lithium = L4 (high milk transfer, infant toxicity). Paracetamol/Ibuprofen/Sertraline = L1/L2.

  1. Neonatal adaptation syndrome caused by:

A. Benzodiazepines B. SSRIs (3rd trimester) C. Opioids D. Beta-blockers

Answer: B. SSRIs in 3rd trimester → jitteriness, irritability, feeding difficulty, respiratory distress, hypoglycaemia. Self-limiting 2–14 days.

18. 18. 📋 SBAs (10)

  1. 32F with mechanical mitral valve (ON-X) on warfarin 4mg/day plans pregnancy. Best management?

A. Continue warfarin throughout B. Switch to therapeutic LMWH pre-conception C. Switch to apixaban D. Stop anticoagulation

Answer: B. Pre-conception switch to LMWH. Warfarin embryopathy risk 6–12% in 1st trimester.

  1. 26F on valproate 1000mg for juvenile myoclonic epilepsy discovers she's 6 weeks pregnant. Immediate action?

A. Stop valproate immediately B. Continue valproate; urgent neurology referral; start folic acid 5mg C. Switch to carbamazepine D. Reduce valproate by 50%

Answer: B. Do NOT stop abruptly (seizure risk). Urgent specialist review for cross-taper to LTG/LEV. Folic acid 5mg immediately.

  1. Pregnant woman at 28 weeks with UTI. E. coli sensitive to all. Best antibiotic?

A. Ciprofloxacin B. Cefalexin C. Nitrofurantoin D. TMP-SMX

Answer: B. Cephalosporins (cefalexin) = Category B, safe. Ciprofloxacin = avoid (cartilage). Nitrofurantoin = avoid near term (haemolysis). TMP-SMX = avoid 3rd tri (kernicterus).

  1. Breastfeeding mother needs analgesia for mastitis. Safe choice?

A. Codeine B. Aspirin C. Ibuprofen D. Diazepam

Answer: C. Ibuprofen = L1 (safe). Codeine = risk respiratory depression. Aspirin = Reye's. Diazepam = sedation.

  1. Woman on lithium 600mg for bipolar disorder becomes pregnant. Fetal risk?

A. Neural tube defects B. Ebstein's anomaly (tricuspid dysplasia) C. Limb reduction D. Renal dysplasia

Answer: B. Lithium = Ebstein's anomaly (0.1–0.2%). Valproate/CBZ/PHT = NTD. Warfarin = chondrodysplasia. ACEi = renal dysplasia.

  1. Folic acid 5mg indicated in all EXCEPT:

A. Epilepsy on carbamazepine B. BMI 32 C. Previous uncomplicated pregnancy D. Diabetes

Answer: C. High-risk indications: AEDs, diabetes, BMI>30, previous NTD, family history, folate antagonists. Previous uncomplicated pregnancy = standard 400μg.

  1. ACE inhibitor fetopathy features:

A. Chondrodysplasia punctata B. Renal dysplasia, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia C. Ebstein's anomaly D. Neural tube defects

Answer: B. ACEi/ARB 2nd/3rd tri → fetal renal dysplasia → oligohydramnios → pulmonary hypoplasia, skull hypoplasia.

  1. Lamotrigine in pregnancy — dose adjustment?

A. No change B. Increase dose (↑ clearance 2–3x); TDM guided C. Decrease dose D. Stop

Answer: B. Lamotrigine clearance ↑ 2–3x in pregnancy (↑ UGT). TDM essential; dose often doubled.

  1. Combined oral contraceptive in breastfeeding — effect?

A. Increases milk supply B. Decreases milk supply (oestrogen) C. No effect D. Causes infant thrombosis

Answer: B. Oestrogen suppresses prolactin → ↓ milk supply. Use progesterone-only methods.

  1. Warfarin embryopathy — characteristic feature:

A. Chondrodysplasia punctata (stippled epiphyses) B. Ebstein's anomaly C. Neural tube defects D. Renal dysplasia

Answer: A. Warfarin embryopathy = chondrodysplasia punctata, nasal hypoplasia, CNS/eye defects. Ebstein's = lithium. NTD = valproate/CBZ/PHT. Renal dysplasia = ACEi.

19. 19. 🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-C, 3-B, 4-C, 5-C, 6-B, 7-C, 8-C, 9-C, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-C, 5-B, 6-C, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-A

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.4.06 Prescribing In Special Populations
[!info]
Heading Hub for Davidson Chapter 2: Clinical therapeutics and good prescribing.
Davidson alignment: Section on Prescribing in Special Populations (SRJ Maxwell).

1. 1. Scope

Pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic alterations in renal impairment, hepatic impairment, elderly, pregnancy/lactation, and paediatrics; dose adjustment principles and contraindicated drugs.

2. 2. Sub-Topics (Topic-Groups)

1. [[Special Populations/Renal impairment|Renal Impairment]]

  • [[Special Populations/eGFR and Cockcroft-Gault|eGFR & Cockcroft-Gault Estimation]]

  • [[Special Populations/Renal dosing common drugs|Common Drug Dose Adjustments in CKD]]
  • [[Special Populations/Dialysis dosing|Drug Dosing in Dialysis]] (HD, PD, CRRT)
  • [[Special Populations/Renal contraindicated|Drugs Contraindicated in Renal Impairment]]

2. [[Special Populations/Hepatic impairment|Hepatic Impairment]]

  • [[Special Populations/Child-Pugh classification|Child-Pugh Classification for Dosing]]
  • [[Special Populations/Hepatic drug metabolism|Hepatic Drug Metabolism Principles]]
  • [[Special Populations/Hepatic dosing common drugs|Common Drug Dose Adjustments in Liver Disease]]
  • [[Special Populations/Hepatic contraindicated|Drugs Contraindicated in Hepatic Impairment]]

3. [[Special Populations/Elderly|Elderly Prescribing]]

  • [[Special Populations/Age-related PK PD changes|Age-Related PK/PD Changes]]
  • [[Special Populations/Frailty|Frailty & Prescribing Decisions]]
  • [[Special Populations/High-risk drugs elderly|High-Risk Drugs in Elderly]] (Beers, STOPP)
  • [[Special Populations/Deintensification|Deintensification & Deprescribing in Elderly]]

4. [[Special Populations/Pregnancy and lactation|Pregnancy & Lactation]]

  • [[Special Populations/Pregnancy categories|Pregnancy Categories]] (FDA A/B/C/D/X, UK/EU alternatives)
  • [[Special Populations/Teratogenicity|Teratogenicity Principles & Critical Periods]]
  • [[Special Populations/Safe drugs pregnancy|Safe Drugs in Pregnancy]]
  • [[Special Populations/Contraindicated pregnancy|Drugs Contraindicated in Pregnancy]]
