▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.5.03 Digoxin

Parent Topic: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring → Clinical Therapeutics Overview

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (FCPS/MRCP — narrow TI, renal clearance, target 0.5–0.9/1.0–1.5, toxicity signs, Fab fragments)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 2; BNF; NICE; ESC Guidelines; Sanford Guide; Digoxin TDM literature

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Understand digoxin PK: narrow therapeutic index, renal elimination, Vd, tissue binding
  • [ ] Apply therapeutic ranges: AF (0.5–0.9 μg/L) vs HF (0.8–1.5 μg/L) — modern lower targets
  • [ ] Calculate loading dose (rapid digitalisation) vs maintenance dose (renal-adjusted)
  • [ ] Time sampling correctly: ≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h) — avoid distribution phase
  • [ ] Recognise toxicity: GI, CNS (visual), cardiac (arrhythmias), biochemical (K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺)
  • [ ] Know drug interactions: P-gp inhibitors (amiodarone, verapamil, quinidine, clarithromycin)
  • [ ] Manage overdose: Digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Digibind/DigiFab)
  • [ ] Answer viva: "When to check digoxin level?" and "Digoxin toxicity management"

🧠 Core Concept: Digoxin Pharmacokinetics

Key PK Properties

PropertyValueClinical Implication
Therapeutic indexNarrow (toxic >2 μg/L)TDM essential
Absorption (oral)70–80% bioavailabilityIV = 100%; switch IV→PO = same dose
DistributionVd 5–8 L/kg (large; tissue binding)Loading dose needed; not dialysable
Protein binding20–25%Minimal displacement interactions
EliminationRenal (60–80% unchanged)CrCl = primary determinant of clearance
Half-life36–48h (normal renal); 4–6 days (anuria)Steady state: 5–7 days (normal); 2–3 weeks (ESRD)
Clearance~7 L/h/70kg (parallels CrCl)Maintenance dose ∝ CrCl
Key Principle: Digoxin is a P-glycoprotein (P-gp) substrate. P-gp inhibitors → ↑ absorption + ↓ renal/biliary clearance → ↑ levels.

1️⃣ Therapeutic Ranges — Modern Lower Targets

IndicationTarget Range (μg/L)Rationale
Atrial Fibrillation (rate control)0.5–0.9 (some guidelines 0.5–1.0)Lower levels effective for rate control; ↓ toxicity
Heart Failure (HFrEF, sinus rhythm)0.5–0.9 (historically 0.8–1.5; now lower)DIG trial: no mortality benefit >0.9; ↑ toxicity
Acute HF / Severe symptoms0.8–1.2 (short-term)Higher levels for inotropic effect temporarily
Viva Key: Modern target for BOTH AF and HF is 0.5–0.9 μg/L (some use 0.5–1.0). Historical HF target 0.8–1.5 is outdated — DIG trial showed no benefit and more toxicity above 0.9.

When to Check Level

SituationTiming
Steady state≥5 half-lives = 5–7 days (normal renal); 2–3 weeks (ESRD)
After dose changeWait 5 half-lives from change
Suspected toxicityAny time (interpret with clinical picture)
Drug interaction started3–5 days after interacting drug
Renal function changeWhen new steady state reached

Sampling Time — CRITICAL

Dose TimingWhen to Sample
Oral≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h, or pre-next dose)
IV≥6h post-infusion
Why wait?Distribution phase (first 6h): levels falsely high (tissue not equilibrated)
Never check level <6h post-dose — falsely elevated (distribution phase). If drawn early, repeat at correct time.

2️⃣ Dosing — Loading & Maintenance

Loading Dose (Rapid Digitalisation) — AF with Rapid Ventricular Rate

RouteRegimen
IV0.5–0.75 mg stat, then 0.25 mg q6h × 2–3 doses (total 1–1.5 mg over 24h)
Oral0.5–0.75 mg stat, then 0.25 mg q6h × 2–3 doses (total 1–1.5 mg over 24h)
Max total load1.5 mg (rarely >2 mg)
MonitorECG, HR, BP, K⁺ during loading
Contraindicated for loading: WPW + AF, significant AV block, recent MI, severe electrolyte imbalance

Maintenance Dose — Renal Function Based

CrCl (mL/min)Typical Daily Dose (μg)Regimen
>60125–250125–250 μg daily
40–60125125 μg daily
30–40125 q48h or 62.5 dailyAlternate days or half-dose
20–3062.5–125 q48h62.5–125 μg q48h
10–2062.5 q48–72h62.5 μg q48–72h
<10 / HD62.5 q72h or weeklyPost-HD if on HD

Calculation Formula (Jelliffe / Koda-Kimble)

Maintenance Dose (μg/day) = (Target Css × Cl) / F
Cl (L/h) ≈ 0.8 × CrCl (mL/min) + 20 (non-renal)
F (bioavailability) = 0.7–0.8 (oral); 1.0 (IV)
Target Css = 0.7–0.9 μg/L (mid-range)

Simplified: Daily dose (μg) ≈ Target × CrCl × 0.8 (rough approximation)

Obesity & Elderly

  • Obesity: Use ideal body weight for Vd calculation (digoxin distributes in lean tissue)
  • Elderly: ↓ Vd, ↓ CrCl → lower doses (often 62.5–125 μg daily)

3️⃣ Drug Interactions — P-gp Inhibitors (Major)

DrugEffect on DigoxinMagnitudeManagement
Amiodarone↑ Level 2–3x (↓ renal & non-renal clearance, ↑ Vd)Major (most common serious interaction)Halve digoxin dose; monitor level at 3–7 days
Verapamil↑ Level 1.5–2xMajorReduce digoxin dose 50%; monitor
Quinidine↑ Level 2–3xMajorAvoid combination; if essential → halve dose
Clarithromycin / Erythromycin↑ Level 1.5–2x (gut P-gp inhibition)ModerateMonitor level; reduce dose if needed
AzithromycinMinimal effectMinorPreferred macrolide
Dronedarone↑ Level 2xMajorHalve dose
Spironolactone / Eplerenone↑ Level mild (P-gp + renal)ModerateMonitor K⁺ and digoxin level
P-gp inducers (Rifampicin, St John's Wort)↓ LevelModerateMay need dose increase
Viva Key: Amiodarone + digoxin = classic interaction. Halve digoxin dose when starting amiodarone. Check level at 1 week.

4️⃣ Toxicity — Recognition & Management

Clinical Features

SystemFeatures
GI (most common, early)Anorexia, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain
CNS (characteristic)Visual disturbances: yellow-green halos (xanthopsia), blurred vision, photophobia; Confusion, delirium, headache
Cardiac (life-threatening)Arrhythmias: PAT with block (paroxysmal atrial tachycardia with AV block) — pathognomonic; Bradycardia, AV block, VF, VT, bigeminy, bidirectional VT
BiochemicalHyperkalaemia (acute toxicity → K⁺ release from cells); Hypokalaemia (diuretics → ↑ toxicity); Hypomagnesaemia

Toxicity Risk Factors

FactorMechanism
Renal impairment↓ Clearance
Hypokalaemia (diuretics)↓ K⁺ → ↑ Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase binding → ↑ toxicity
HypomagnesaemiaSimilar to hypokalaemia
Hypercalcaemia↑ Toxicity (Ca²⁺ potentiates)
Hypothyroidism↓ Clearance, ↑ sensitivity
Elderly↓ Vd, ↓ CrCl, polypharmacy
P-gp inhibitors↑ Levels (see above)

5️⃣ Management of Toxicity / Overdose

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Digoxin Toxicity
↓
Life-threatening?
↓
Digoxin-Specific Antibody Fragments Digibind / DigiFab
Hold Digoxin Correct Electrolytes Monitor ECG
↓
Calculate Dose See below
Level >2 or Significant arrhythmia?
↓
Recheck level in 24-48h Restart at lower dose if needed

Digoxin-Specific Antibody Fragments (Digibind® / DigiFab®)

ProductBinding CapacityDosing
Digibind (ovine Fab)1 vial binds 0.5 mg digoxinEmpirical: 10–20 vials (acute overdose)
DigiFab (ovine Fab, newer)1 vial binds 0.4 mg digoxinCalculated: see below

Calculated Dosing Formulas

1. Based on Steady-State Level (preferred for chronic toxicity):

Number of vials = (Serum digoxin μg/L × Vd L/kg × Weight kg) / 0.4 mg per vial (DigiFab)
                  = (Level × 6 × Weight) / 0.4  (Vd ≈ 6 L/kg)

Example: 80kg, level 4 μg/L → (4 × 6 × 80) / 0.4 = 480 vials → impractical → use empirical

2. Based on Amount Ingested (acute overdose):

Number of vials = Amount ingested (mg) / 0.4 mg per vial (DigiFab)

Example: 30 tablets × 250μg = 7.5mg → 19 vials

3. Empirical (if unknown):

  • Chronic toxicity: 10–15 vials DigiFab (or 20 vials Digibind)
  • Acute massive overdose: 20–30 vials DigiFab
  • Paediatric: Same calculation

Administration

  • IV infusion over 30 min (can be 15 min if critical)
  • Effect: Free digoxin ↓ within minutes; total digoxin ↑ (bound to Fab)
  • Monitoring: K⁺ (falls as digoxin redistributes), ECG, renal function
  • Lab interference: Total digoxin assay falsely elevated post-Fab; use free digoxin if available

6️⃣ Special Populations

Renal Impairment

CrClAction
30–60Standard dose, monitor level
15–30Halve dose (62.5–125 μg q48h)
<15 / HDMinimal dose (62.5 μg q72h or weekly); not dialysable (large Vd, tissue binding)
CRRTSimilar to CrCl 15–30; monitor levels

Pregnancy

  • Category C (crosses placenta)
  • Vd ↑, Cl ↑ in pregnancy → may need higher dose
  • Monitor levels (target same)
  • Safe in breastfeeding (minimal milk transfer)

Paediatrics

  • Higher Vd (neonates 10–15 L/kg) → higher loading dose per kg
  • Renal function immature → longer half-life in neonates
  • Therapeutic range same (0.5–0.9 μg/L AF; 0.8–1.5 HF — but modern lower)

7️⃣ Practical Monitoring Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Digoxin Prescribed
↓
Indication?
↓
& D
Target 0.5-0.9 μg/L
↓
Check Baseline: U&E, Mg, Ca, TFT, ECG, CrCl
↓
Initiate Dose by CrCl Load if AF with RVR
↓
Check Level at Steady State ≥6h post-dose (ideally 8-12h)
↓
Level in Target?
↓
Continue; Monitor Cr q3-6mo Level if dose change/interaction
Increase dose; Re-level steady state
Reduce/hold dose; Check K+/Mg/Ca Re-level 2-3 days
↓
Toxicity symptoms?
↓
Hold digoxin; Correct electrolytes; Digibind/DigiFab if life-threatening
Re-level at steady state

⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Therapeutic RangeAF: 0.5–0.9 μg/L; HF: 0.5–0.9 μg/L (modern, DIG trial); Historical HF 0.8–1.5 outdated
Sampling Time≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h/pre-dose); <6h = falsely high (distribution phase)
Steady State5–7 days (normal renal); 2–3 weeks (ESRD)
Loading Dose0.5–0.75mg then 0.25mg q6h × 2–3 doses (total 1–1.5mg) for AF with RVR
MaintenanceRenal-adjusted: >60=125–250μg/d; 40–60=125μg/d; 30–40=125μg q48h; <15=62.5μg q72h
Key InteractionAmiodarone → 2–3x level ↑ → HALVE digoxin dose; Verapamil, quinidine, clarithromycin similar
ToxicityGI (nausea), CNS (yellow halos/xanthopsia), Cardiac (PAT with block = pathognomonic), Hyperkalaemia
Risk FactorsRenal impairment, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypercalcaemia, hypothyroidism, elderly, P-gp inhibitors
OverdoseDigibind/DigiFab (Fab fragments); Dose by amount ingested or empirical 10–20 vials
PregnancyCategory C; Vd↑, Cl↑; monitor levels; safe in breastfeeding

🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1What is the modern therapeutic range for digoxin in AF and HF?Both 0.5–0.9 μg/L (some use 0.5–1.0). DIG trial: no mortality benefit >0.9 in HF; more toxicity. Historical HF 0.8–1.5 is outdated.
2When should digoxin level be checked?≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h or pre-dose). NOT <6h — distribution phase gives falsely high levels.
3Steady state for digoxin in normal renal function?5–7 days (5 half-lives; t½ 36–48h). In ESRD: 2–3 weeks.
4Amiodarone started on digoxin — action?Halve digoxin dose immediately. Amiodarone ↑ digoxin 2–3x (P-gp inhibition + ↓ clearance). Check level at 1 week.
5Pathognomonic arrhythmia of digoxin toxicity?Paroxysmal Atrial Tachycardia with AV Block (PAT with block). Also: bidirectional VT, AV block, VF.
6Visual disturbance characteristic of digoxin toxicity?Xanthopsia — yellow-green halos, blurred vision, photophobia.
7Electrolyte abnormalities increasing digoxin toxicity?Hypokalaemia (diuretics), Hypomagnesaemia, Hypercalcaemia. Hypokalaemia = most common.
8Digoxin overdose management?Digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Digibind/DigiFab). Empirical 10–20 vials or calculated by amount ingested. Total digoxin assay falsely elevated post-Fab.
9Digoxin in end-stage renal disease on HD — dosing?62.5 μg q72h or weekly (post-HD). Not dialysable (large Vd, tissue binding). Monitor levels.
10Patient on digoxin 125μg daily, started clarithromycin for pneumonia. Action?Clarithromycin ↑ digoxin 1.5–2x (P-gp inhibition). Reduce digoxin to 62.5μg daily; monitor level at 3–5 days.

🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"Digoxin level at 2h post-dose is fine"NO. <6h = distribution phase. Level falsely HIGH. Must wait ≥6h (ideally 8–12h).
"HF target is 0.8–1.5 μg/L"OUTDATED. DIG trial (1997): no mortality benefit >0.9; more toxicity. Modern target 0.5–0.9 for both AF and HF.
"Digoxin cleared by dialysis"NO. Large Vd (6 L/kg), tissue binding → not dialysable. Dose post-HD weekly.
"Any visual change = digoxin toxicity"Xanthopsia (yellow halos) is characteristic. But check level + electrolytes + clinical context.
"Amiodarone + digoxin = just monitor"NO. Interaction is major, predictable, 2–3x level increase. Halve dose at amiodarone initiation.
"Hyperkalaemia = always present in toxicity"Acute massive overdose → hyperkalaemia (K⁺ release from cells). Chronic toxicity → often normal or low K⁺ (diuretics).
Mnemonic: DIGOXIN TDM
Digoxin target: 0.5-0.9 μg/L (both AF & HF modern)
Interactions: Amiodarone #1 (halve dose); Verapamil, Quinidine, Clarithromycin, Dronedarone (P-gp inhibitors)
GI/CNS/Cardiac toxicity: Nausea, Xanthopsia (yellow halos), PAT with block (pathognomonic)
Overdose: Digibind/DigiFab (Fab fragments); empirical 10-20 vials or by ingested amount
X (Sampling time): ≥6h post-dose (8-12h ideal); <6h = falsely high (distribution)
Index of toxicity risk: HypoK, HypoMg, HyperCa, Hypothyroid, Renal failure, Elderly, P-gp inhibitors
Nephron: Renal clearance = main route; Dose by CrCl: >60=125-250μg/d, <15=62.5μg q72h/weekly
Therapeutic range: Historical HF 0.8-1.5 = OLD; DIG trial → 0.5-0.9 for both
Maintenance: No loading for HF; Load only AF with RVR (0.5-0.75mg then 0.25mg q6h×2-3)
Monitor: Level at steady state (5-7d normal; 2-3w ESRD); K+/Mg/Ca/TFT baseline
Uricaria/dermatitis: rare hypersensitivity
Steady state: 5 half-lives; t½ 36-48h normal; 4-6 days ESRD
Pregnancy: Category C; crosses placenta; Vd↑, Cl↑; safe in breastfeeding
Elderly: ↓ Vd, ↓ CrCl → lower doses (62.5-125μg); polypharmacy risk

🗺️ Mind Map

Digoxin TDM
Target
AF: 0.5-0.9
HF: 0.5-0.9 (modern)
Old HF 0.8-1.5 outdated
Sampling
≥6h post-dose (8-12h ideal)
NOT <6h (distribution phase)
Steady state: 5-7d normal
Dosing
Load: AF RVR only (1-1.5mg/24h)
Maint: by CrCl
>60: 125-250μg/d
<15: 62.5μg q72h
Interactions
Amiodarone: 2-3x ↑ → halve dose
Verapamil, Quinidine, Clarithro
P-gp inhibitors
Toxicity
GI: Nausea
CNS: Xanthopsia (yellow halos)
Cardiac: PAT with block
K+: Hyper (acute), Hypo (chronic)
Overdose
Digibind/DigiFab
Empirical 10-20 vials
Total assay falsely high

📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScore (0–5)Notes
Day 1
Day 3
Day 7
Day 14
Day 30
Day 90

📝 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore%
Therapeutic Range2
Sampling Time2
Dosing (Load/Maint)3
Interactions (P-gp)3
Toxicity Features3
Overdose Management2
Special Populations2
Monitoring Algorithm3
Total20

💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe digoxin therapeutic drug monitoring including dosing, sampling, toxicity and interactions."Target 0.5-0.9, sampling ≥6h, renal dosing, loading for AF, P-gp interactions (amiodarone), toxicity (GI, xanthopsia, PAT with block), Fab fragments
Short Note"Digoxin toxicity management."Hold digoxin, correct K+/Mg/Ca++, Digibind/DigiFab for life-threatening, monitor ECG, total assay falsely elevated post-Fab
Viva"Patient on digoxin 125μg daily, starts amiodarone for AF. Action?"Halve digoxin to 62.5μg daily immediately. Amiodarone ↑ level 2-3x. Check level at 1 week. Monitor electrolytes.
Ward Round"Digoxin level drawn 3h post-dose = 2.2 μg/L. Patient asymptomatic. Action?"Ignore level — drawn in distribution phase (<6h). Repeat at 8–12h post-dose. If still >1.5 → reduce dose.
Last-Night"Target 0.5-0.9. Sample ≥6h (8-12h). Load AF only. Maint by CrCl. Amiodarone halve dose. Toxicity: nausea, yellow halos, PAT+block, hyperK. Fab for overdose."Target range. Sampling. Loading. Renal dosing. Key interaction. Toxicity triad. Antidote.

📌 Summary

  • Therapeutic range: 0.5–0.9 μg/L for both AF and HF (modern, DIG trial)
  • Sampling: ≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h/pre-dose); <6h = falsely high (distribution phase)
  • Steady state: 5–7 days (normal renal); 2–3 weeks (ESRD)
  • Dosing: Loading only for AF with RVR (1–1.5mg/24h); Maintenance by CrCl: >60=125–250μg/d, <15=62.5μg q72h
  • Key interaction: Amiodarone → 2–3x level ↑ → HALVE dose; Verapamil, quinidine, clarithromycin similar (P-gp)
  • Toxicity: GI (nausea), CNS (xanthopsia = yellow halos), Cardiac (PAT with block = pathognomonic), Hyperkalaemia (acute)
  • Risk factors: Renal impairment, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypercalcaemia, hypothyroidism, elderly, P-gp inhibitors
  • Overdose: Digoxin-specific Fab fragments (Digibind/DigiFab); empirical 10–20 vials or calculated by ingested amount
  • ESRD/HD: Not dialysable; minimal dose weekly post-HD; monitor levels

❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Modern digoxin therapeutic range for heart failure:

A. 0.8–1.5 μg/L B. 1.0–2.0 μg/L C. 0.5–0.9 μg/L D. 1.5–2.5 μg/L

Answer: C. DIG trial: no benefit >0.9; more toxicity. Target 0.5–0.9 for both AF and HF.

  1. Correct sampling time for digoxin level:

A. 1h post-dose B. 3h post-dose C. ≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h) D. Any time

Answer: C. <6h = distribution phase → falsely elevated. Wait ≥6h (8–12h ideal).

  1. Drug interaction requiring digoxin dose halving:

A. Furosemide B. Amiodarone C. Aspirin D. Atorvastatin

Answer: B. Amiodarone ↑ digoxin 2–3x (P-gp inhibition). Halve dose at initiation.

  1. Pathognomonic arrhythmia of digoxin toxicity:

A. Atrial fibrillation B. PAT with AV block C. SVT D. Ventricular fibrillation

Answer: B. Paroxysmal Atrial Tachycardia with AV Block = pathognomonic.

  1. Visual disturbance in digoxin toxicity:

A. Blue vision B. Yellow-green halos (xanthopsia) C. Double vision D. Tunnel vision

Answer: B. Xanthopsia — yellow-green halos, blurred vision, photophobia.

  1. Digoxin overdose antidote:

A. N-acetylcysteine B. Flumazenil C. Digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Digibind/DigiFab) D. Physostigmine

Answer: C. Digibind/DigiFab = ovine Fab fragments binding digoxin.

  1. Digoxin in ESRD on haemodialysis — dosing:

A. 125μg daily B. 125μg post-HD C. 62.5μg q72h or weekly post-HD D. 250μg q48h

Answer: C. Not dialysable (large Vd). Minimal dose 62.5μg q72h or weekly post-HD.

  1. Electrolyte abnormality MOST increasing digoxin toxicity:

A. Hyperkalaemia B. Hypokalaemia C. Hypernatraemia D. Hypophosphataemia

Answer: B. Hypokalaemia (from diuretics) → ↑ Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase binding → ↑ toxicity.

  1. Steady state for digoxin in normal renal function:

A. 24h B. 48h C. 5–7 days D. 2 weeks

Answer: C. t½ 36–48h → 5 half-lives = 5–7 days.

  1. Digoxin level drawn 3h post-dose = 2.2 μg/L. Interpreation?

A. Toxic — stop digoxin B. Falsely high — ignore, repeat at 8–12h C. Therapeutic D. Subtherapeutic

Answer: B. <6h = distribution phase. Level not at steady state. Repeat at correct time.

📋 SBAs (10)

  1. 75M, AF, digoxin 125μg daily, CrCl 45. Starts amiodarone. Best action?

A. Continue same dose, monitor level B. Reduce digoxin to 62.5μg daily C. Stop digoxin D. Increase digoxin

Answer: B. Amiodarone ↑ digoxin 2–3x. Halve dose immediately. Check level at 1 week.

  1. Patient on digoxin, presents with nausea, yellow halos, HR 40, irregular. ECG: atrial tachycardia with 2:1 block. K+ 5.2. Diagnosis?

A. Beta-blocker toxicity B. Digoxin toxicity C. Calcium channel blocker toxicity D. Myocardial infarction

Answer: B. Triad: GI (nausea), CNS (xanthopsia), Cardiac (PAT with block). Hyperkalaemia in acute toxicity.

  1. 80M, digoxin 250μg daily for 10 years, CrCl 20. Presents confused. Level 3.5 μg/L. Management?

A. Hold digoxin; correct electrolytes; consider Digibind if arrhythmia B. Reduce dose to 125μg C. Continue, monitor D. Switch to beta-blocker

Answer: A. Level >2 = toxicity. Hold digoxin. Correct K+/Mg. Digibind if life-threatening arrhythmia.

  1. Digoxin overdose: 50 tablets of 250μg ingested 2h ago. DigiFab dose?

A. 5 vials B. 20 vials C. 10 vials D. 30 vials

Answer: B. 50 × 0.25mg = 12.5mg. DigiFab 1 vial = 0.4mg. 12.5/0.4 = 31 vials. Empirical 20–30 vials for acute overdose.

  1. Digoxin level timing — which is CORRECT?

A. 2h post-IV = therapeutic B. Pre-dose oral = peak C. ≥6h post-dose oral = valid D. Any time if steady state

Answer: C. Must wait ≥6h post-dose (8–12h ideal) for valid trough level.

🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-C, 2-C, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B, 6-C, 7-C, 8-B, 9-C, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-A, 4-B, 5-C

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.5.04 Immunosuppressants

Parent Topic: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring → Clinical Therapeutics Overview

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (FCPS/MRCP — narrow TI, CYP3A4/P-gp interactions, trough vs C2 monitoring, rejection vs toxicity)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 2; BNF; KDIGO Guidelines; BST Guidelines; Maudsley; Sanford Guide; Transplant TDM literature

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Understand CNI (Calcineurin Inhibitor) PK: narrow TI, CYP3A4/P-gp metabolism, intra-patient variability
  • [ ] Apply therapeutic ranges: Tacrolimus vs Cyclosporine (trough vs C2), Induction vs Maintenance
  • [ ] Time sampling correctly: Trough (C0) standard; C2 for Cyclosporine (better AUC correlation)
  • [ ] Adjust for drug interactions: CYP3A4 inhibitors (↑ levels), inducers (↓ levels)
  • [ ] Recognise CNI toxicity: Nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity (tremor, seizures, PRES), diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidaemia
  • [ ] Manage Sirolimus/Everolimus: mTOR inhibitors, different monitoring (trough), wound healing, pneumonitis
  • [ ] Apply conversion between CNIs
  • [ ] Answer viva: "Tacrolimus vs Cyclosporine monitoring" and "CNI interactions with clarithromycin/rifampicin"

🧠 Core Concept: Immunosuppressant Pharmacokinetics

PropertyTacrolimusCyclosporineSirolimus / Everolimus
ClassCalcineurin Inhibitor (CNI)Calcineurin Inhibitor (CNI)mTOR Inhibitor
MechanismFKBP12 → Calcineurin inhibition → ↓ IL-2Cyclophilin → Calcineurin inhibition → ↓ IL-2FKBP12 → mTOR inhibition → ↓ T-cell proliferation
MetabolismCYP3A4/5 (gut + liver)CYP3A4/5 (gut + liver)CYP3A4 (gut + liver)
TransporterP-glycoprotein (P-gp)P-glycoprotein (P-gp)P-gp
Protein binding99% (albumin, α1-acid glycoprotein)90% (lipoproteins)92%
Blood:Plasma ratio~15–25 (high in RBCs)~2 (variable)~30–40
Half-life12–24h6–12hSirolimus: 60h; Everolimus: 30h
Therapeutic indexVery narrowNarrowNarrow
Key toxicityNephro, Neuro, Diabetes, HTNNephro, HTN, Hyperlipidaemia, HirsutismThrombocytopenia, Pneumonitis, Wound healing, Hyperlipidaemia
Key Principle: All three are CYP3A4 + P-gp substrates. Inhibitors ↑ levels (toxicity), Inducers ↓ levels (rejection). Whole blood monitoring (not plasma/serum) due to RBC partitioning.

1️⃣ Tacrolimus (FK506 / Prograf® / Advagraf® / Envarsus®)

Therapeutic Ranges (Whole Blood Trough, C0) — KDIGO/BST

PhaseTarget Range (μg/L = ng/mL)
Induction (0–3 months)10–15 (kidney), 15–20 (liver/heart/lung)
Maintenance (3–12 months)5–10 (kidney), 10–15 (liver/heart/lung)
Long-term (>1 year)3–8 (kidney), 5–10 (liver/heart/lung)
Minimisation (with MMF/mTOR)3–7
Viva Key: Tacrolimus trough higher in induction, lower in maintenance, lowest in minimisation. Liver/heart/lung targets higher than kidney.

Formulations & Dosing

FormulationDosingConversion
Prograf® (IR)BD (0.1–0.2 mg/kg/day)Reference
Advagraf® (PR)OD (same total daily dose)1:1 total daily dose
Envarsus® (ER)OD (lower dose)Envarsus 0.7mg ≈ Advagraf 1mg ≈ Prograf 1mg

Sampling Time

  • Trough (C0): Pre-dose (12h post-BD, 24h post-OD)
  • Consistent timing critical (food, other drugs)
  • Steady state: 3–5 days (5 half-lives)

2️⃣ Cyclosporine (Neoral® / Sandimmune®)

Therapeutic Ranges (Whole Blood)

MonitoringTarget Range (μg/L)
Trough (C0)Induction: 200–300; Maintenance: 100–200; Long-term: 75–150
C2 (2h post-dose)Induction: 800–1200; Maintenance: 400–800; Long-term: 300–600
C2 vs C0: C2 (2h post-dose) better correlates with AUC and clinical outcomes. Preferred for Cyclosporine (Neoral®). C0 acceptable if C2 not feasible.

Formulations

FormulationBioavailabilityNotes
Neoral® (Microemulsion)~30–50% (more consistent)Standard; BD dosing
Sandimmune® (Oil-based)~20–30% (variable)Avoid interchange; not bioequivalent

Sampling Time

  • C0: Pre-dose (12h post-BD)
  • C2: Exactly 2h post-dose (critical timing)
  • Steady state: 3–5 days

3️⃣ Sirolimus (Rapamune®) / Everolimus (Certican® / Afinitor®)

Therapeutic Ranges (Whole Blood Trough)

DrugInductionMaintenanceMinimisation (with CNI reduction)
Sirolimus12–205–124–10 (with low-dose CNI)
Everolimus6–103–83–6 (with reduced CNI)
Key: mTOR inhibitors used with reduced-dose CNI (minimisation) or CNI-free (if nephrotoxicity). Higher targets with CNI-free.

Key Characteristics

FeatureSirolimusEverolimus
Half-life~60h~30h
DosingOD (loading 6–15mg, then 2–5mg)BD (0.75–1.5mg)
Steady state5–7 days3–4 days
MonitoringTrough (C0)Trough (C0)

Specific Toxicities

ToxicitySirolimusEverolimus
Wound healing impairmentHigh (hold peri-op)Moderate
Pneumonitis~10–15% (interstitial)~5–10%
ThrombocytopeniaCommonCommon
HyperlipidaemiaSevere (↑ TG, cholesterol)Moderate
ProteinuriaCan worsenCan worsen
Oral ulcersCommonCommon

4️⃣ Major Drug Interactions — CYP3A4 / P-gp

CYP3A4 / P-gp Inhibitors (↑ Levels → TOXICITY)

DrugEffect on CNI/mTORMagnitudeManagement
Clarithromycin / Erythromycin↑↑ Tacrolimus, Cyclosporine, Sirolimus5–10xAvoid; use azithromycin (no CYP3A4 inhibition)
Azole antifungals (Fluconazole, Itraconazole, Voriconazole, Posaconazole)↑↑ All3–10xReduce dose 50–75%; monitor levels q2–3d
Protease inhibitors (Ritonavir, Atazanavir)↑↑ All5–20xAvoid / specialist only
Diltiazem / Verapamil↑ Tacrolimus, Cyclosporine2–4xReduce dose 50%; monitor
Grapefruit juice↑ All2–3xAvoid
Amiodarone↑ Tacrolimus2–3xReduce dose; monitor
Rifampicin↓↓ All (inducer)90% ↓Avoid; if essential → increase dose 3–5x + close monitoring
Carbamazepine / Phenytoin / Phenobarbital↓↓ All50–80% ↓Avoid; use alternative anticonvulsant (levetiracetam)
St John's Wort↓ All30–50% ↓Avoid
Viva Key: Macrolides (clarithromycin) + Tacrolimus = classic interaction → acute nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity. Use azithromycin instead. Azoles → reduce CNI dose 50–75%. Rifampicin → avoid (rejector risk).

Other Interactions

InteractionEffect
ACEi/ARB + CNI↑ Nephrotoxicity risk (additive)
NSAIDs + CNI↓ GFR + ↑ Nephrotoxicity
Statins + CNI/mTOR↑ Myopathy risk (esp. simvastatin/atorvastatin with sirolimus)
Potassium-sparing diuretics + Tacrolimus↑ Hyperkalaemia risk
IVIG + Tacrolimus↑ Neurotoxicity (PRES)

5️⃣ CNI Toxicity — Recognition & Management

Tacrolimus vs Cyclosporine Toxicity Profile

ToxicityTacrolimusCyclosporine
NephrotoxicityAcute (vasoconstriction) + Chronic (arteriolar hyalinosis)Similar (more chronic)
NeurotoxicityTremor, headache, insomnia, PRES, seizures (more common)Less common
Diabetes (NODAT)High risk (↑ 10–20% kidney Tx)Lower risk
HypertensionCommonMore common/severe
HyperlipidaemiaMildMarked (↑ TG, cholesterol)
Hirsutism / Gingival hyperplasiaRareCommon
ElectrolytesHyperkalaemia, HypomagnesaemiaHyperkalaemia

Nephrotoxicity — Acute vs Chronic

TypeFeaturesManagement
Acute (Functional)Reversible afferent arteriolar vasoconstriction; ↑ Cr, ↓ UOPReduce dose; ensure euvolaemia; avoid NSAIDs/ACEi; IV fluids
Chronic (Structural)Arteriolar hyalinosis, interstitial fibrosis; irreversibleCNI minimisation/withdrawal; convert to mTOR/belatacept

Neurotoxicity — PRES (Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome)

TriggerHigh tacrolimus level, hypertension, IVIG, sepsis
FeaturesHeadache, visual disturbance, seizures, confusion, cortical blindness
ManagementStop/Reduce tacrolimus; control BP; anticonvulsants; usually reversible

6️⃣ CNI Conversion / Minimisation Strategies

Tacrolimus ↔ Cyclosporine Conversion

DirectionRatioMethod
Tac → CyA1:5 to 1:7 (Tac 1mg ≈ CyA 5–7mg)Switch at same time; monitor C0/C2 at 24h, 48h, 1w
CyA → Tac5:1 to 7:1Same; monitor closely
Principle: Convert total daily dose using ratio. Monitor levels daily × 3–5 days.

CNI Minimisation / Withdrawal (for Nephrotoxicity)

StrategyRegimen
CNI → mTORReduce CNI by 50% → start sirolimus/everolimus → taper CNI over 4–8w
CNI-freeStop CNI → sirolimus/everolimus + MMF + steroids (selected patients)
CNI → BelataceptIV monthly (EBV+ only); CNI-free; better renal function

7️⃣ Monitoring Schedule (KDIGO/BST)

ParameterFrequency
Tacrolimus/Cyclosporine levelDaily × 1w post-Tx/change → 2–3x/wk × 1m → weekly × 3m → monthly × 1y → 3-monthly
Sirolimus/Everolimus levelWeekly × 1m → monthly × 3m → 3-monthly
Renal function (Cr, eGFR)Daily (early) → weekly → monthly → 3-monthly
Fasting lipidsBaseline → 3-monthly (mTOR: monthly × 3m)
Glucose / HbA1cBaseline → 3-monthly (Tac: monthly × 3m)
BPEach visit
Mg²⁺, K⁺Monthly × 3m → 3-monthly
LFTMonthly × 3m → 3-monthly
FBCMonthly × 3m → 3-monthly (mTOR: thrombocytopenia)

8️⃣ Special Populations

Pregnancy (Transplant Recipients)

DrugSafetyMonitoring
TacrolimusPreferred CNI (more data); ↑ clearance in pregnancyLevels q2w; dose ↑ 20–50%
CyclosporineAlternative; ↑ HTN riskLevels q2w
Sirolimus/EverolimusContraindicated (teratogenic in animals)Stop pre-conception
MMF/MycophenolateContraindicated (teratogenic)Switch to Azathioprine pre-conception

Paediatrics

  • Higher clearance → higher mg/kg doses
  • Tacrolimus: 0.1–0.3 mg/kg/day BD
  • Growth monitoring (steroids, CNI)

Hepatic Impairment

  • Tacrolimus: ↓ Clearance → reduce dose; monitor levels daily
  • Cyclosporine: ↓ Clearance → reduce dose
  • Sirolimus: ↑↑ Levels → avoid severe hepatic impairment

9️⃣ Practical Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Transplant Recipient on CNI/mTOR
↓
Routine Level Check
↓
Level in Target?
↓
Continue; Monitor Renal/Metabolic
& F
Check Adherence Check Interactions (Rifampicin?)\nIncrease dose 25-50%\nRe-level 24-48h
↓
Toxicity Signs?
↓
Reduce CNI; Euvolaemia; Avoid NSAIDs
Reduce Tacrolimus; Consider CyA switch
Reduce Tac; Add/Adjust OHAs/Insulin
Statin; Consider mTOR reduction
Hold mTOR peri-op

⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DrugTarget Trough (μg/L)SamplingKey ToxicityKey Interactions
TacrolimusInduction 10–15; Maint 5–10; Long 3–8C0 pre-doseNephro, Neuro (tremor/PRES), NODAT, HTN, HyperKClarithromycin ↑↑ (avoid); Azoles ↓ dose 50-75%; Rifampicin ↓↓ (avoid)
CyclosporineC0: Ind 200–300, Maint 100–200; C2 preferred: Ind 800–1200, Maint 400–800C0 or C2 (2h post)Nephro, HTN, Hyperlipidaemia, Hirsutism, Gingival hyperplasiaSame CYP3A4/P-gp interactions
SirolimusInd 12–20; Maint 5–12; Min 4–10C0 pre-doseWound healing, Pneumonitis, Thrombocytopenia, Hyperlipidaemia, ProteinuriaSame CYP3A4/P-gp; Statin myopathy risk
EverolimusInd 6–10; Maint 3–8; Min 3–6C0 pre-doseSimilar to sirolimus; less pneumonitisSame

🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1Tacrolimus therapeutic trough range for kidney transplant maintenance?5–10 μg/L (ng/mL). Induction 10–15, Long-term 3–8.
2Cyclosporine monitoring — C0 vs C2?C2 (2h post-dose) preferred — better AUC correlation. C0 acceptable if C2 not feasible.
3Patient on tacrolimus starts clarithromycin for pneumonia. Action?Avoid clarithromycin (↑ tacrolimus 5–10x → nephro/neurotoxicity). Use azithromycin (no CYP3A4 inhibition). If clarithromycin essential → reduce tacrolimus 75%, daily levels.
4CNI interaction with azole antifungals?↑↑ Levels 3–10x. Reduce CNI dose 50–75%; monitor levels q2–3d.
5Tacrolimus vs Cyclosporine — diabetes risk?Tacrolimus ↑ NODAT risk (10–20% kidney Tx). Cyclosporine lower risk.
6Sirolimus — when to hold peri-operatively?Hold 1 week before & 2 weeks after major surgery (wound healing impairment).
7Cyclosporine → Tacrolimus conversion ratio?CyA:Tac ≈ 5:1 to 7:1 (CyA 100mg ≈ Tac 15–20mg). Monitor levels daily × 3–5 days.
8PRES (Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome) — trigger in transplant?High tacrolimus level + hypertension + IVIG + sepsis. Features: headache, visual loss, seizures, confusion. Management: stop/reduce tacrolimus, control BP.
9mTOR inhibitor + statin — risk?↑ Myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk (esp. simvastatin/atorvastatin with sirolimus). Use lower statin dose or pravastatin/rosuvastatin.
10Rifampicin + Tacrolimus — effect?Rifampicin induces CYP3A4 → ↓ tacrolimus 90% → rejection risk. Avoid rifampicin; if essential → increase tacrolimus 3–5x + daily levels.

🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"Tacrolimus and Cyclosporine same monitoring"NO. Tacrolimus = trough (C0) only. Cyclosporine = C2 preferred (2h post-dose).
"C2 = 2h post ANY formulation"C2 validated for Neoral® (microemulsion). Sandimmune® not suitable for C2.
"Sirolimus = CNI-sparing = no CNI needed"Usually with REDUCED CNI (minimisation). CNI-free only in selected patients (nephrotoxicity).
"Azithromycin interacts like clarithromycin"NO. Azithromycin = NO CYP3A4 inhibition → safe with CNIs. Preferred macrolide.
"CNI levels = plasma/serum"NO. Whole blood (EDTA) — CNIs partition into RBCs. Plasma/serum levels meaningless.
"Tremor = always tacrolimus toxicity"Tremor common at therapeutic levels. Coarse tremor + hyperreflexia + confusion = toxicity.
"Wound healing only with sirolimus"Everolimus also impairs (less). CNIs also impair but less. Hold mTOR peri-op.
"Cyclosporine gingival hyperplasia = poor hygiene"Cyclosporine-specific (not tacrolimus). Good hygiene helps but not preventable.
Mnemonic: IMMUNOSUPPRESSANT TDM
Immunosuppressants: Tacrolimus, Cyclosporine, Sirolimus, Everolimus
Monitoring: Whole blood (not plasma) — RBC partitioning critical
Metabolism: CYP3A4 + P-gp for ALL — Inhibitors ↑ (toxicity), Inducers ↓ (rejection)
Universal interactions: Clarithromycin (avoid → azithromycin), Azoles (↓ dose 50-75%), Rifampicin (avoid)
Nephrotoxicity: Acute (reversible) vs Chronic (irreversible → CNI minimisation/mTOR switch)
Optimal ranges: Tac C0: Ind 10-15, Maint 5-10; CyA C2: Ind 800-1200, Maint 400-800
Sirolimus: mTOR; Wound healing, Pneumonitis, Thrombocytopenia, Hyperlipidaemia
Unique toxicities: Tac = Neuro (tremor/PRES), NODAT; CyA = HTN, Lipids, Hirsutism
Pregnancy: Tac/CyA OK (levels q2w); Sirolimus/Everolimus/MMF CONTRAINDICATED
PERI-OP: Hold mTOR 1w pre / 2w post (wound healing)
Refraction: CyA ↔ Tac 5:1 to 7:1; daily levels × 3-5d
Everolimus: t½ 30h (vs Siro 60h); BD dosing; lower pneumonitis
Sampling: Tac/CyA/Siro/Evero = Trough (C0) pre-dose; CyA C2 = 2h post (better AUC)
Statin interaction: mTOR + Statin = ↑ Myopathy (simva/atorva worst)
Azoles: Fluconazole/Itraconazole/Voriconazole/Posaconazole all ↑↑ CNI/mTOR
NODAT: Tacrolimus > Cyclosporine (insulinopenia + insulin resistance)
Neuro: Tac = Tremor → PRES → Seizures; CyA = less neuro
Therapeutic drug monitoring: MANDATORY for all — narrow TI, high variability
Dose adjustment: Small changes (25-50%); re-level 24-48h
Minimisation: CNI → mTOR for chronic nephrotoxicity; Belatacept alternative

🗺️ Mind Map

Immunosuppressant TDM
Tacrolimus
Target: Ind 10-15, Maint 5-10
Sampling: C0 pre-dose
Toxicity: Nephro, Neuro, NODAT, HTN
Formulations: IR BD, PR OD, ER OD
Cyclosporine
Target: C2 preferred (Ind 800-1200)
C0: Ind 200-300
Toxicity: HTN, Lipids, Hirsutism
Neoral vs Sandimmune
Sirolimus/Everolimus
mTOR inhibitors
Target: Siro Ind 12-20, Ever Ind 6-10
Toxicity: Wound healing, Pneumonitis, Thrombocytopenia
Hold peri-op
Interactions
CYP3A4/P-gp Inhibitors: Clarithro (avoid), Azoles (↓50-75%)
CYP3A4 Inducers: Rifampicin (avoid), Carbamazepine
Conversion
CyA ↔ Tac 5:1 to 7:1
CNI → mTOR for nephrotoxicity
Monitoring Schedule
Daily → Weekly → Monthly → 3-monthly

📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScore (0–5)Notes
Day 1
Day 3
Day 7
Day 14
Day 30
Day 90

📝 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore%
Tacrolimus (Range, Toxicity, Interactions)5
Cyclosporine (C0 vs C2, Toxicity)4
Sirolimus/Everolimus (mTOR, Toxicity)4
Key CYP3A4/P-gp Interactions4
Conversion/Minimisation3
Total20

💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe therapeutic drug monitoring for calcineurin inhibitors and mTOR inhibitors."Tacrolimus/Cyclosporine/Sirolimus/Everolimus ranges, sampling (C0 vs C2), CYP3A4/P-gp interactions, toxicity profiles, conversion, minimisation, pregnancy
Short Note"Tacrolimus interactions with macrolides and azoles."Clarithromycin/erythromycin ↑ tac 5-10x → avoid (use azithromycin). Azoles ↑ 3-10x → reduce tac 50-75%, monitor q2-3d.
Viva"Kidney transplant on tacrolimus 4mg BD, level 18, tremor, Cr rising. On clarithromycin for chest infection. Action?"Clarithromycin ↑ tacrolimus → toxicity. Stop clarithromycin, switch to azithromycin. Reduce tacrolimus dose (50-75%). Monitor levels daily, renal function.
Ward Round"Liver transplant 6m post-op, cyclosporine level low. On carbamazepine for seizures. Action?"Carbamazepine induces CYP3A4 → ↓ cyclosporine. Switch carbamazepine to levetiracetam (no induction). Increase cyclosporine dose, monitor levels.
Last-Night"Tac: C0 Ind10-15, Maint5-10. CyA: C2 Ind800-1200, Maint400-800. Siro: Ind12-20, Maint5-12. CYP3A4/P-gp: Clarithro avoid, Azoles ↓50-75%, Rifampicin avoid. Tac neuro NODAT; CyA HTN lipids; Siro wound/pneumonia. Peri-op hold mTOR. Preg: Tac/CyA OK, Siro/Evero/MMF contraindicated."Key ranges. Interactions. Toxicity profiles. Peri-op. Pregnancy.

📌 Summary

  • Whole blood monitoring for all (RBC partitioning)
  • Tacrolimus: C0 trough; Induction 10–15, Maintenance 5–10, Long-term 3–8 μg/L
  • Cyclosporine: C2 (2h post) preferred; Induction 800–1200, Maintenance 400–800; C0 Ind 200–300, Maint 100–200
  • Sirolimus/Everolimus: mTOR inhibitors; C0 trough; Siro Ind 12–20, Maint 5–12; Ever Ind 6–10, Maint 3–8
  • CYP3A4/P-gp interactions: Clarithromycin/erythromycin → avoid (use azithromycin); Azoles → reduce dose 50–75%; Rifampicin/carbamazepine/phenytoin → avoid (rejection risk)
  • Toxicity: Tacrolimus = Neuro (tremor, PRES), NODAT; Cyclosporine = HTN, Hyperlipidaemia, Hirsutism; mTOR = Wound healing, Pneumonitis, Thrombocytopenia, Hyperlipidaemia
  • Pregnancy: Tacrolimus/Cyclosporine OK (levels q2w); Sirolimus/Everolimus/MMF contraindicated
  • Peri-op: Hold mTOR 1 week pre / 2 weeks post major surgery
  • Conversion: CyA:Tac ≈ 5:1 to 7:1; CNI minimisation with mTOR for chronic nephrotoxicity

❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Preferred sampling time for Cyclosporine (Neoral®) monitoring:

A. Trough (C0) only B. C2 (2h post-dose) C. Peak (1h) D. Random

Answer: B. C2 better correlates with AUC and outcomes for Cyclosporine microemulsion.

  1. Tacrolimus therapeutic trough for kidney transplant maintenance:

A. 15–20 μg/L B. 5–10 μg/L C. 20–30 μg/L D. 0.5–1.0 μg/L

Answer: B. Maintenance 5–10 μg/L. Induction 10–15, Long-term 3–8.

  1. Macrolide SAFE with tacrolimus (no CYP3A4 inhibition):

A. Clarithromycin B. Erythromycin C. Azithromycin D. Roxithromycin

Answer: C. Azithromycin does not inhibit CYP3A4. Clarithromycin/erythromycin ↑ tacrolimus 5–10x.

  1. Tacrolimus vs Cyclosporine — higher diabetes risk:

A. Cyclosporine B. Tacrolimus C. Equal D. Neither

Answer: B. Tacrolimus ↑ NODAT risk (10–20% kidney Tx). Cyclosporine lower risk.

  1. Sirolimus — peri-operative management:

A. Continue throughout B. Hold 1w pre-op, 2w post-op C. Hold 24h only D. Double dose

Answer: B. mTOR inhibitors impair wound healing → hold 1 week before and 2 weeks after major surgery.

  1. Cyclosporine to Tacrolimus conversion ratio:

A. 1:1 B. 2:1 C. 5:1 to 7:1 D. 10:1

Answer: C. CyA 100mg ≈ Tac 15–20mg (5:1 to 7:1). Monitor levels daily × 3–5 days.

  1. Rifampicin effect on tacrolimus:

A. ↑ Levels B. ↓↓ Levels (90%) C. No effect D. Variable

Answer: B. Rifampicin induces CYP3A4 → ↓ tacrolimus clearance ~90% → rejection risk. Avoid.

  1. mTOR inhibitor + Statin — highest myopathy risk:

A. Pravastatin B. Rosuvastatin C. Simvastatin D. Ezetimibe

Answer: C. Simvastatin/atorvastatin CYP3A4 substrates → ↑ levels with mTOR inhibitors → myopathy risk.

  1. Pregnancy in transplant recipient — contraindicated:

A. Tacrolimus B. Cyclosporine C. Sirolimus/Everolimus D. Prednisolone

Answer: C. Sirolimus/Everolimus teratogenic in animals. MMF also contraindicated.

  1. Patient on tacrolimus, started fluconazole for candidiasis. Level rises from 8 to 22. Action?

A. Increase tacrolimus B. Reduce tacrolimus 50-75%; monitor levels q2-3d C. Stop fluconazole D. No change

Answer: B. Fluconazole inhibits CYP3A4 → ↑ tacrolimus 3–10x. Reduce dose 50–75%, monitor closely.

📋 SBAs (10)

  1. Renal transplant 3m post-op, tacrolimus 3mg BD, level 14 (target 5-10). On fluconazole for oral thrush. Best action?

A. Stop fluconazole B. Reduce tacrolimus to 1.5mg BD; monitor level in 48h C. Increase tacrolimus D. Switch to cyclosporine

Answer: B. Fluconazole ↑ tacrolimus → reduce dose 50–75%, close monitoring.

  1. Heart transplant on cyclosporine (C2 monitoring). C2 = 300 (target 400-800). On rifampicin for TB. Action?

A. Increase cyclosporine B. Stop rifampicin (use alternative regimen); monitor cyclosporine C. Reduce cyclosporine D. Switch to tacrolimus

Answer: B. Rifampicin induces CYP3A4 → ↓ cyclosporine → rejection risk. Avoid rifampicin; use rifabutin if possible (less induction).

  1. Liver transplant 2y post-op, sirolimus 2mg OD for CNI nephrotoxicity. Scheduled for elective hernia repair. Peri-op sirolimus?

A. Continue B. Hold 1w pre-op, restart 2w post-op if healing C. Hold 24h D. Switch to tacrolimus

Answer: B. mTOR inhibitors impair wound healing → hold 1 week before, 2 weeks after major surgery.

  1. Tacrolimus neurotoxicity — PRES risk factors:

A. Low tacrolimus level B. High tacrolimus level + hypertension + IVIG C. Hypokalaemia D. Hyperglycaemia

Answer: B. PRES triggered by high tacrolimus level, hypertension, IVIG, sepsis.

  1. Kidney transplant on tacrolimus, develops NODAT (HbA1c 52 mmol/mol). Management?

A. Stop tacrolimus B. Reduce tacrolimus; add metformin/insulin; consider switch to cyclosporine C. Increase tacrolimus D. Add sirolimus

Answer: B. Tacrolimus ↑ NODAT risk. Reduce dose, treat diabetes. Consider conversion to cyclosporine if refractory.

🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-B, 3-C, 4-B, 5-B, 6-C, 7-B, 8-C, 9-C, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.5.05 Lithium

Parent Topic: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring → Clinical Therapeutics Overview

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (FCPS/MRCP — narrow TI, renal clearance, target 0.4–0.8/0.6–0.8, toxicity, pregnancy, drug interactions)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 2; BNF; NICE Bipolar Disorder Guidelines; Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines; Lithium TDM literature

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Understand lithium PK: narrow therapeutic index, 100% renal elimination, no metabolism
  • [ ] Apply therapeutic ranges: Acute mania (0.6–0.8/0.8–1.0), Maintenance (0.4–0.8), Prophylaxis (0.4–0.6)
  • [ ] Time sampling correctly: 12h post-dose (standard trough) — critical for interpretation
  • [ ] Calculate dose adjustment for renal impairment and elderly
  • [ ] Recognise toxicity: GI, neurologic, renal (DI, CKD), endocrine (hypothyroidism)
  • [ ] Manage drug interactions: Diuretics, ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs — ↑ levels → toxicity
  • [ ] Monitor long-term: Renal, thyroid, parathyroid, calcium
  • [ ] Answer viva: "Lithium monitoring schedule" and "Lithium + diuretic interaction"

🧠 Core Concept: Lithium Pharmacokinetics

Key PK Properties

PropertyValueClinical Implication
Therapeutic indexVery narrow (toxic >1.0–1.2)TDM mandatory
Absorption~100% (oral); slow-release = smoother levelsSR preferred for adherence/less peaks
DistributionVd ~0.6–0.9 L/kg (total body water)↑ in dehydration, elderly; not dialysable efficiently
Protein bindingNoneNo displacement interactions
MetabolismNone (excreted unchanged)No CYP interactions
Elimination100% renal (glomerular filtration + proximal reabsorption)CrCl = clearance; Na⁺/volume status affects reabsorption
Half-life18–36h (normal renal); 40–60h (elderly/CKD)Steady state: 4–7 days
Clearance~20–30% of CrCl (proximal reabsorption ~70–80%)Parallels renal function
Key Principle: Lithium is handled like sodium — proximal tubular reabsorption. Volume depletion → ↑ reabsorption → ↑ levels. NSAIDs/ACEi/ARB/diuretics → ↓ renal perfusion → ↑ reabsorption → toxicity.

1️⃣ Therapeutic Ranges (Serum Lithium)

IndicationTarget Range (mmol/L)Notes
Acute Mania0.6–0.8 (some guidelines 0.8–1.0)Higher end for acute control
Maintenance / Prophylaxis0.4–0.6 (some 0.4–0.8)Lower = less toxicity, still effective
Rapid Cycling / Refractory0.6–0.8May need higher
Elderly / Renal Impairment0.4–0.6 (lower end)↑ Sensitivity, ↓ clearance
Viva Key: Modern targets are lower than historical. Maintenance 0.4–0.6 mmol/L is standard. Acute mania 0.6–0.8 (not routinely >1.0). Toxic >1.0–1.2.

2️⃣ Sampling Time — CRITICAL

PreparationSampling Time
Standard release (Camcolit, Priadel liquid)12h post-dose (trough)
Slow-release (Priadel SR, Liskonum)12h post-dose (trough) — same
Why 12h?Lithium t½ ~24h → 12h = consistent trough; avoids peak variability
Never check random level. Must be 12h post-dose (±1h). If timing wrong → repeat. Document exact dose time and sample time.

3️⃣ Dosing & Renal Adjustment

Initiation (Lithium-Naive)

StepDetails
BaselineU&E, Cr, eGFR, TFT, Ca²⁺, TSH, ECG (>50 or cardiac hx), BMI, pregnancy test
Starting dose200–400mg daily (SR preferred) or 200mg BD (standard)
TitrationIncrease by 200–400mg weekly based on level
Target levelCheck at 1 week (steady state ~5 days), then weekly until stable

Renal Impairment — Dose Adjustment

eGFR / CrCl (mL/min)Dose AdjustmentMonitoring
>60Standard dosingLevel 12h post-dose weekly × 4, then 3-monthly
45–60Reduce dose ~25%Level weekly × 4, then 2-monthly; U&E 3-monthly
30–45Reduce dose ~50%Level 2-weekly × 4, then monthly; U&E 2-monthly
15–30Reduce dose ~75% (100–200mg daily)Level weekly; U&E monthly; consider specialist
<15Avoid if possible; if essential → minimal dose (100mg daily/alternate)Level weekly; U&E 2-weekly; nephrology input
Rule of Thumb: Lithium clearance ≈ 20–30% of CrCl. Dose ∝ CrCl. Halve dose if CrCl halved.

Obesity

  • Vd ↑ (total body water) → loading may need higher
  • But clearance ∝ CrCl (not weight) → maintenance by renal function
  • Use ideal body weight for dose calculation if obese

4️⃣ Drug Interactions — Major (↑ Lithium Levels → Toxicity)

Drug ClassMechanismMagnitudeManagement
Thiazide diuretics↓ Na⁺ → ↑ proximal Li⁺ reabsorption↑ Level 25–50% (most common serious interaction)Avoid if possible; if essential → halve Li⁺ dose; monitor level weekly × 4
Loop diureticsLess effect (distal) but volume depletion↑ Level 10–20%Monitor; reduce dose if needed
ACE inhibitors↓ GFR + ↑ proximal reabsorption↑ Level 20–50%Avoid if possible; if essential → halve Li⁺ dose; monitor
ARBsSimilar to ACEi↑ Level 20–40%Same as ACEi
NSAIDs↓ Prostaglandins → ↓ GFR + ↑ reabsorption↑ Level 20–60%Avoid chronic use; if essential → monitor level
COX-2 inhibitorsSimilar to NSAIDsModerateMonitor
Potassium-sparing diuretics (amiloride, triamterene)Minimal effect on Li⁺LowPreferred over thiazides if diuretic needed
TetracyclinesMay ↑ levelsMildMonitor
MetronidazolePossible ↑ levelsMildMonitor
Viva Key: Thiazide + lithium = classic interaction. Halve lithium dose and monitor level weekly. ACEi/ARB + lithium = similar. NSAIDs + lithium = avoid chronic.

Interactions Decreasing Levels (Rare)

DrugMechanism
Theophylline↑ Renal clearance (mild)
Caffeine↑ Renal clearance (mild)
Sodium bicarbonate↑ Urinary pH → ↑ excretion

5️⃣ Toxicity — Acute vs Chronic

Acute Toxicity (Overdose / Rapid Accumulation)

LevelFeatures
1.5–2.0GI (nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea), tremor, confusion, ataxia
2.0–2.5Neurologic: coarse tremor, hyperreflexia, clonus, seizures, encephalopathy, coma
>2.5–3.0Life-threatening: seizures, status epilepticus, respiratory failure, permanent neuro damage

Chronic Toxicity (Therapeutic Levels but Accumulation / Sensitivity)

FeatureDetails
NeurologicFine tremor (most common), cognitive slowing, memory impairment
RenalNephrogenic Diabetes Insipidus (NDI) — polyuria, polydipsia (↑ 20–40% patients); Chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis → CKD (↓ eGFR over years)
EndocrineHypothyroidism (goitre, ↑ TSH) — 10–20% long-term; Hyperparathyroidism (↑ Ca²⁺) — 5–10%
CardiacBenign T-wave flattening/inversion (not ischaemic); Sick sinus syndrome (rare)
DermatologicAcne, psoriasis exacerbation, alopecia

Risk Factors for Toxicity

FactorWhy
Renal impairment↓ Clearance
Dehydration (vomiting, diarrhoea, sweating)↑ Reabsorption
Hyponatraemia / Low salt diet↑ Reabsorption
Elderly↓ CrCl, ↓ Vd, polypharmacy
Drug interactions (diuretics, ACEi, NSAIDs)↑ Levels
Febrile illness↓ Intake, ↑ losses

6️⃣ Management of Toxicity

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Lithium Toxicity Suspected
↓
Level & Clinical
↓
& D & E
HOLD lithium IV saline 1-2L (if euvolaemic) Monitor ECG, neuro, renal Level q12-24h
ICU admission IV saline + consider **haemodialysis** Dialysis if: level >4, severe neuro, renal failure, <60yo
↓
Restart at lower dose when level <0.8 & asymptomatic Review interactions

Haemodialysis Indications

IndicationThreshold
Severe neurotoxicitySeizures, encephalopathy, coma
Level >4.0 mmol/L(or >2.5 with severe symptoms)
Renal failure (unable to excrete)AKI on CKD
Elderly / comorbidLower threshold (e.g., >2.5 with symptoms)
Note: Lithium is dialysable (small, water-soluble, low protein binding) but rebound occurs (redistribution from tissues). Prolonged/intermittent HD often needed.

7️⃣ Long-Term Monitoring Schedule (NICE / Maudsley)

TestFrequency
Lithium level (12h post-dose)Weekly until stable × 2; then 3-monthly (stable); 2-monthly if elderly/CKD/interactions
U&E, eGFRBaseline; 3-monthly (stable); 2-monthly if elderly/CKD/diuretics
Thyroid function (TSH, FT4)Baseline; 6-monthly (stable); annually if stable long-term
Calcium (corrected)Baseline; annually (screen for hyperparathyroidism)
Weight / BMIEach review
ECGBaseline if >50 or cardiac hx; repeat if clinically indicated

Monitoring Action Thresholds

ParameterAction
Lithium >1.0Reduce dose; check adherence; review interactions; repeat level 1 week
eGFR ↓ >20% from baseline or <45Review dose; nephrology referral if <30; consider alternative
TSH >10 or symptomatic hypothyroidismStart levothyroxine; continue lithium if psychiatric benefit
Ca²⁺ >2.65 (corrected)Check PTH; endocrinology referral if hyperparathyroidism
Polyuria >3L/dayWater deprivation test for NDI; consider amiloride (reduces polyuria)

8️⃣ Special Populations

Pregnancy

AspectDetails
CategoryD (human risk) — Ebstein's anomaly (0.1–0.2%)
1st TrimesterAvoid if possible; if essential → lowest dose; level monitoring weekly; fetal echo 18–20w
2nd/3rd TrimesterClearance ↑ (↑ GFR) → dose may need ↑; monitor level weekly
PeripartumStop 24–48h before delivery (neonatal toxicity: floppy, hypotonia, hypothyroidism)
BreastfeedingContraindicated (high milk levels, infant toxicity)

Elderly

  • Target 0.4–0.6 mmol/L (lower)
  • Start low (100–200mg daily); titrate slowly
  • Monitor renal/thyroid/calcium more frequently
  • Review interactions (diuretics, ACEi, NSAIDs common)

Paediatrics / Adolescents

  • Off-label but used in bipolar
  • Weight-based dosing (20–30mg/kg/day)
  • Monitor growth, thyroid, renal closely

9️⃣ Switching Preparations

SwitchAction
Standard → Slow-release (SR)Same total daily dose; SR gives smoother levels, less peak toxicity
SR → StandardSame total dose; may need BD dosing
Liquid → TabletSame dose (bioequivalent)
NeverChange preparation without checking level at steady state (1 week)
Priadel SR 200mg = Camcolit 200mg = Li-Liquid 5mL (200mg/5mL)

⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Therapeutic RangeAcute mania: 0.6–0.8; Maintenance: 0.4–0.6 (modern lower targets)
Sampling Time12h post-dose (±1h) — trough; standard + SR same timing
Steady State4–7 days (5 half-lives; t½ 18–36h)
Renal Clearance100% renal; Li⁺ handled like Na⁺; volume depletion → ↑ reabsorption → toxicity
Key InteractionsThiazides (↑ 25–50%), ACEi/ARB (↑ 20–50%), NSAIDs (↑ 20–60%) — halve Li⁺ dose
ToxicityAcute: >1.5 GI/neuro; >2.5 seizures/coma. Chronic: tremor, NDI (polyuria), hypothyroidism, CKD, hyperparathyroidism
ManagementMild: reduce/hold, hydrate. Severe: HD (level >4 or severe neuro). Rebound post-HD.
MonitoringLevel 3-monthly (stable); U&E 3-monthly; TSH 6-monthly; Ca²⁺ annually
PregnancyD (Ebstein's 0.1–0.2%); avoid 1st tri; weekly levels; stop before delivery; breastfeeding contraindicated
ElderlyTarget 0.4–0.6; start low; frequent monitoring

🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1What is the therapeutic range for lithium maintenance?0.4–0.6 mmol/L (some guidelines 0.4–0.8). Acute mania 0.6–0.8.
2When should lithium level be taken?12 hours post-dose (±1h). Standard and slow-release same timing.
3Thiazide diuretic started on lithium — action?Halve lithium dose; monitor level weekly × 4. Thiazides ↓ Na⁺ → ↑ proximal Li⁺ reabsorption → ↑ levels 25–50%.
4ACE inhibitor + lithium — management?Avoid if possible; if essential → halve lithium dose; monitor level weekly; monitor renal function.
5Chronic lithium toxicity features?Fine tremor (most common), NDI (polyuria/polydipsia), hypothyroidism, CKD, hyperparathyroidism (↑ Ca²⁺).
6Lithium level 2.2 mmol/L with confusion and tremor. Management?HOLD lithium; IV saline 1–2L; monitor level q12–24h; correct electrolytes; restart when level <0.8.
7When is haemodialysis indicated for lithium toxicity?Level >4.0, OR severe neurotoxicity (seizures, encephalopathy, coma), OR renal failure unable to excrete.
8Long-term monitoring for lithium?Level 3-monthly (stable); U&E 3-monthly; TSH 6-monthly; Ca²⁺ annually; weight each visit.
9Lithium in pregnancy — category and key risk?Category D — Ebstein's anomaly (tricuspid dysplasia) 0.1–0.2%. Avoid 1st trimester if possible.
10Lithium + NSAID interaction — mechanism and management?NSAIDs ↓ prostaglandins → ↓ GFR + ↑ proximal reabsorption → ↑ lithium 20–60%. Avoid chronic NSAIDs; if essential → monitor level, reduce dose.

🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"Lithium level anytime is fine"NO. Must be 12h post-dose (trough). Random level uninterpretable.
"Therapeutic range is 0.6–1.2"OUTDATED. Maintenance 0.4–0.6; Acute mania 0.6–0.8. Toxic >1.0–1.2.
"Lithium is metabolised by liver"NO. 100% renal excretion unchanged. No CYP metabolism.
"Thiazide + lithium = just monitor"NO. Halve lithium dose when starting thiazide. Interaction is predictable, major (25–50% ↑).
"Lithium not dialysable"YES it is dialysable (small, water-soluble, no protein binding). But rebound occurs — need prolonged/intermittent HD.
"Lithium safe in breastfeeding"NO. Contraindicated — high milk levels (RID ~50%), infant toxicity (floppy, hypothyroidism, renal).
"NSAIDs only affect lithium if renal impairment"NO. NSAIDs affect lithium at any renal function (prostaglandin inhibition). Avoid chronic use.
"Tremor = always toxicity"Fine tremor common at therapeutic levels. Coarse tremor + hyperreflexia + confusion = toxicity.
Mnemonic: LITHIUM TDM
Lithium range: Maintenance 0.4-0.6; Acute mania 0.6-0.8 (modern lower)
Interactions: Thiazides #1 (halve dose); ACEi/ARB (halve); NSAIDs (avoid chronic)
Timing: 12h post-dose (trough); standard & SR same; document exact times
Hydration: Volume depletion ↑ reabsorption → toxicity; Na⁺/water balance crucial
Intake: Low salt diet ↑ toxicity; consistent Na⁺ intake
Urine: NDI (polyuria) — 20-40%; amiloride helps; water deprivation test
Monitoring: Level 3-monthly; U&E 3-monthly; TSH 6-monthly; Ca²⁺ annually
Toxicity: Acute >1.5 GI/neuro; >2.5 seizures. Chronic: Tremor, NDI, Hypothyroid, CKD, HyperCa
Dialysis: Dialysable (small, water-soluble); Rebound — prolonged HD; level>4 or severe neuro
Management: Mild reduce/hold + hydrate; Severe ICU + HD; Restart when <0.8
Pregnancy: D (Ebstein's 0.1-0.2%); avoid 1st tri; weekly levels; stop pre-delivery; BF contraindicated
Elderly: Target 0.4-0.6; start low; frequent monitoring; review interactions
Renal: 100% renal clearance; dose ∝ CrCl; halve if CrCl halved; avoid if eGFR<15
NSB: Format SR vs standard — same total dose; check level 1 week after switch

ReviewDateScore (0–5)Notes

SectionMaxScore%

💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe lithium monitoring including therapeutic ranges, interactions, toxicity and long-term monitoring."Range 0.4-0.6/0.6-0.8; 12h sampling; renal handling; thiazide/ACEi/NSAID interactions; acute/chronic toxicity; HD; 3-monthly level, U&E, 6-monthly TSH, annual Ca
Short Note"Lithium + thiazide interaction."Thiazide ↓ Na⁺ → ↑ proximal Li⁺ reabsorption → ↑ level 25–50%. Halve Li⁺ dose. Monitor level weekly × 4.
Viva"Patient on lithium 600mg daily, starts hydrochlorothiazide for HTN. Level rises from 0.5 to 0.9. Action?"Halve lithium to 300mg daily. Thiazide interaction predictable. Monitor level weekly until stable. Consider amiloride instead of thiazide.
Ward Round"Lithium level 1.8 mmol/L, patient confused, coarse tremor. Management?"HOLD lithium. IV saline 1–2L. Monitor level q12–24h. Correct K⁺/Mg²⁺. If seizures/coma → HD. Restart when <0.8.
Last-Night"Range 0.4-0.6 maint, 0.6-0.8 acute. Sample 12h. Thiazide/ACEi/NSAID halve dose. Toxicity: tremor, NDI, hypothyroid, CKD. HD level>4 or severe neuro. Monitor 3-m level, 6-m TSH."Range. Sampling. Key interactions (halve). Chronic toxicity triad. HD indications. Monitoring schedule.

📌 Summary

  • Therapeutic range: Maintenance 0.4–0.6 mmol/L; Acute mania 0.6–0.8 (modern lower targets)
  • Sampling: 12h post-dose (trough) — standard, SR, liquid all same timing
  • PK: 100% renal excretion; handled like Na⁺; volume depletion → ↑ reabsorption → toxicity
  • Key interactions: Thiazides (↑ 25–50% → HALVE DOSE), ACEi/ARB (↑ 20–50% → HALVE DOSE), NSAIDs (↑ 20–60% → AVOID CHRONIC)
  • Toxicity: Acute (>1.5 GI/neuro, >2.5 seizures/coma); Chronic (fine tremor, NDI/polyuria, hypothyroidism, CKD, hyperparathyroidism)
  • Management: Mild — hold, hydrate; Severe — ICU, HD if level >4 or severe neuro/renal failure
  • Monitoring: Level 3-monthly (stable); U&E 3-monthly; TSH 6-monthly; Ca²⁺ annually
  • Pregnancy: Category D (Ebstein's 0.1–0.2%); avoid 1st tri; weekly levels; stop pre-delivery; breastfeeding contraindicated
  • Elderly: Target 0.4–0.6; start low; frequent monitoring

❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Modern lithium maintenance therapeutic range:

A. 0.6–1.2 mmol/L B. 0.8–1.0 mmol/L C. 0.4–0.6 mmol/L D. 0.6–0.8 mmol/L

Answer: C. Modern target 0.4–0.6 for maintenance. 0.6–0.8 for acute mania.

  1. Correct lithium sampling time:

A. 2h post-dose B. 6h post-dose C. 12h post-dose (±1h) D. Random

Answer: C. 12h post-dose trough for all preparations (standard, SR, liquid).

  1. Thiazide diuretic started on lithium — dose adjustment:

A. No change B. Halve lithium dose C. Double lithium dose D. Stop lithium

Answer: B. Thiazides ↓ Na⁺ → ↑ proximal Li⁺ reabsorption → ↑ level 25–50%. Halve dose.

  1. Chronic lithium toxicity feature (most common):

A. Coarse tremor B. Fine tremor C. Seizures D. Coma

Answer: B. Fine tremor is most common chronic toxicity. Coarse tremor = acute toxicity.

  1. Lithium-induced nephropathy — characteristic feature:

A. Nephrotic syndrome B. Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (polyuria) C. Rapidly progressive GN D. Obstructive uropathy

Answer: B. NDI (polyuria/polydipsia) in 20–40% long-term. Chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis → CKD.

  1. Haemodialysis indication for lithium toxicity:

A. Level >1.5 B. Level >2.5 C. Level >4.0 OR severe neurotoxicity D. Level >3.0

Answer: C. HD if level >4.0, OR severe neuro (seizures, encephalopathy, coma), OR renal failure.

  1. Lithium + ACE inhibitor — recommended action:

A. No change B. Halve lithium dose; monitor weekly C. Stop ACEi D. Increase lithium

Answer: B. ACEi ↓ GFR + ↑ proximal reabsorption → ↑ lithium 20–50%. Halve dose if combination essential.

  1. Lithium in pregnancy — category and risk:

A. B (safe) B. C (caution) C. D (Ebstein's anomaly 0.1–0.2%) D. X (contraindicated)

Answer: C. Category D — Ebstein's anomaly (tricuspid valve dysplasia) 0.1–0.2%.

  1. Lithium half-life in normal renal function:

A. 6–12h B. 18–36h C. 48–72h D. >72h

Answer: B. t½ 18–36h (normal); 40–60h (elderly/CKD). Steady state 4–7 days.

  1. NSAID + lithium interaction — mechanism:

A. CYP inhibition B. Prostaglandin inhibition → ↓ GFR + ↑ proximal reabsorption C. Protein displacement D. Altered absorption

Answer: B. NSAIDs ↓ prostaglandins → ↓ renal perfusion + ↑ proximal Li⁺ reabsorption → ↑ levels 20–60%.

📋 SBAs (10)

  1. 65M on lithium 800mg daily (level 0.55), starts bendroflumethiazide 2.5mg. 2 weeks later level 0.95, mild tremor. Action?

A. Continue, monitor B. Reduce lithium to 400mg daily C. Stop thiazide D. Increase thiazide

Answer: B. Thiazide interaction → halve lithium dose. Monitor level weekly.

  1. 30F on lithium 600mg daily, pregnant 6 weeks. Level 0.5. Best management?

A. Stop lithium immediately B. Continue lithium; weekly levels; fetal echo 18-20w; folic acid 5mg C. Switch to valproate D. Reduce dose by 50%

Answer: B. Do not stop abruptly. Fetal risk (Ebstein's) but relapse risk high. Weekly levels, fetal echo, folate 5mg.

  1. Patient on lithium presents with polyuria 4L/day, polydipsia, normal glucose. Lithium level 0.5. Diagnosis?

A. Diabetes mellitus B. Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus C. SIADH D. Psychogenic polydipsia

Answer: B. NDI from chronic lithium — collecting duct resistance to ADH. Level can be therapeutic.

  1. Lithium toxicity level 3.2 mmol/L, seizures, GCS 8. Immediate management?

A. Oral hydration B. IV saline + haemodialysis C. Activated charcoal D. Flumazenil

Answer: B. Level >2.5 with severe neuro (seizures, coma) → ICU + haemodialysis.

  1. Lithium monitoring — which is CORRECT schedule for stable patient?

A. Level monthly, U&E monthly, TSH monthly B. Level 3-monthly, U&E 3-monthly, TSH 6-monthly, Ca²⁺ annually C. Level 6-monthly, U&E annually D. Level weekly, U&E weekly

Answer: B. NICE/Maudsley stable monitoring schedule.

🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-C, 2-C, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B, 6-C, 7-B, 8-C, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.5.06 Theophylline

Parent Topic: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring → Clinical Therapeutics Overview

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐ HIGH (FCPS/MRCP — narrow TI for asthma/COPD, CYP1A2 metabolism, smoking/age/drug interactions, toxicity: seizures/arrhythmias)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 2; BNF; NICE Asthma/COPD Guidelines; GINA/GOLD; Sanford Guide; Theophylline TDM literature

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Understand theophylline PK: narrow TI, CYP1A2 metabolism, low protein binding
  • [ ] Apply therapeutic range: 10–20 mg/L (5–10 mg/L for COPD maintenance)
  • [ ] Time sampling correctly: trough (pre-dose) at steady state
  • [ ] Adjust dose for clearance factors: smoking, age, heart failure, liver disease, drug interactions
  • [ ] Recognise toxicity: GI, CNS (tremor, seizures), cardiac (arrhythmias), metabolic (hypokalaemia, hyperglycaemia)
  • [ ] Manage key interactions: CYP1A2 inhibitors (↑ levels), inducers (↓ levels)
  • [ ] Answer viva: "Theophylline therapeutic range" and "Interactions with ciprofloxacin/fluvoxamine"

🧠 Core Concept: Theophylline Pharmacokinetics

Key PK Properties

PropertyValueClinical Implication
Therapeutic indexNarrow (toxic >20 mg/L)TDM essential
Absorption (oral)100% (IR); SR variable (food effects)Take on empty stomach (IR); SR with/without food per brand
DistributionVd 0.45–0.5 L/kg (TBW)Dose by ideal/actual BW
Protein bindingLow (40–60%)Minimal displacement interactions
MetabolismHepatic: CYP1A2 (major), CYP2E1, CYP3A4Major interaction target; saturable at high doses
EliminationRenal ~10% unchanged; hepatic ~90%Renal impairment → minor dose reduction
Half-lifeHighly variable: 3–9h (adult non-smoker); 2–3h (smoker); 24h+ (HF/liver disease)Clearance = primary determinant
ClearanceClearance-based dosing (mL/kg/h)Not dose-proportional at high levels
Key Principle: Theophylline clearance is CYP1A2-dependent and highly variable. Smoking induces (↓ t½ to 2–3h). Heart failure, liver disease, elderly, CYP1A2 inhibitors ↓ clearance (↑ t½ to 24h+).

1️⃣ Therapeutic Ranges

IndicationTarget Range (mg/L = μg/mL)Notes
Acute Asthma10–20Higher for severe exacerbation
Chronic Asthma10–20Symptom control
COPD (stable)10–20 (some guidelines 5–10)Lower for maintenance; ↑ toxicity risk in elderly
Apnoea of Prematurity6–13Neonatal dosing different
Toxic Threshold>20Adverse effects increase sharply
Viva Key: Standard therapeutic range 10–20 mg/L. COPD maintenance often 5–10 (lower efficacy needed, ↑ toxicity risk in elderly). Toxic >20.

2️⃣ Clearance-Based Dosing —(Key to Theophylline)

Clearance Estimates by Population

PopulationClearance (mL/kg/h)Typical Dose (mg/kg/day)Half-life
Adult non-smoker0.70.7 × 15 = ~10.5 mg/kg/day6–9h
Adult smoker1.51.5 × 15 = ~22.5 mg/kg/day2–3h
Elderly (>65)0.4–0.50.5 × 15 = ~7.5 mg/kg/day8–12h
Heart Failure (NYHA III/IV)0.3–0.40.35 × 15 = ~5 mg/kg/day12–24h
Liver Disease (cirrhosis)0.3–0.40.35 × 15 = ~5 mg/kg/day12–24h
Viral illness (children)0.3–0.5ReducedProlonged
Neonate (preterm)0.05–0.10.08 × 15 = ~1.2 mg/kg/day20–30h

Dosing Formula (Steady-State Maintenance)

Dose (mg/day) = Target Css (mg/L) × Clearance (mL/kg/h) × Weight (kg) × 24 / 1000

Simplified: For target 15 mg/L:

Dose (mg/kg/day) ≈ Clearance (mL/kg/h) × 15 × 24 / 1000 ≈ Clearance × 0.36

Practical Starting Doses (mg/kg/day) — IR vs SR

PopulationAminophylline IV (loading)Oral IR (BD)Oral SR (OD/BD)
Adult non-smoker5 mg/kg over 20 min300 mg BD400 mg BD
Adult smoker5 mg/kg over 20 min400 mg BD600 mg BD
Elderly / HF / Liver disease2.5 mg/kg over 30 min200 mg BD200–300 mg BD
Paediatric >1yr5 mg/kg12–16 mg/kg/day12–16 mg/kg/day
Loading dose (IV aminophylline): 5 mg/kg (non-smoker) / 2.5 mg/kg (elderly/HF/liver) over 20–30 min. No loading if on oral theophylline (risk of toxicity).

3️⃣ Sampling Time & Steady State

ParameterValue
Steady state5 half-lives = 24–48h (adult); 3–5 days (elderly/HF)
Sampling timeTrough (pre-dose) for BD dosing; pre-dose for OD SR
When to checkAfter 3 days at stable dose (or 24h post-IV load)
Repeat after dose change3–5 days (5 half-lives at new clearance)
Do NOT check random level. Trough = pre-dose (just before next dose).

4️⃣ Major Drug Interactions — CYP1A2 Focus

CYP1A2 Inhibitors (↑ Theophylline Levels → Toxicity)

DrugEffect on ClearanceDose Adjustment
Fluvoxamine↓ 80–90% (strongest)Avoid; if essential → ↓ theophylline 75–90%
Ciprofloxacin↓ 30–50%Halve dose; monitor level
Enoxacin / Norfloxacin↓ 30–50%Similar to ciprofloxacin
Macrolides (clarithromycin, erythromycin)↓ 20–30%Monitor level; consider alternative
Cimetidine↓ 20–30%Monitor; ranitidine/famotidine preferred
Allopurinol↓ 20–30%Monitor
Oral contraceptives↓ 20–30%Monitor
Ticlopidine↓ 20–30%Monitor
Interferon-α↓ 30–50%Halve dose

CYP1A2 Inducers (↓ Theophylline Levels → Loss of Control)

DrugEffect on ClearanceDose Adjustment
Smoking (cigarettes)↑ 50–100% (2x)Double dose; monitor level
Marijuana↑ Similar to tobaccoMonitor
Rifampicin↑ 50–100%Double dose; monitor
Carbamazepine / Phenytoin / Phenobarbital↑ 50–100%Double dose; monitor
St John's Wort↑ 30–50%Increase; monitor
Charcoal-broiled food (chronic)↑ MildMonitor

Other Interactions

DrugInteraction
AdenosineTheophylline antagonises adenosine (↑ dose needed for SVT)
Beta-blockersNon-selective ↑ theophylline clearance slightly; theophylline ↓ beta-blocker effect
Loop diuretics↑ Hypokalaemia risk (theophylline ↓ K⁺)
CorticosteroidsAdditive hypokalaemia; theophylline may ↑ steroid clearance

5️⃣ Toxicity — Concentration-Dependent

Level (mg/L)Clinical Features
10–20 (Therapeutic)Usually asymptomatic; mild GI upset, insomnia, tachycardia
20–30 (Mild Toxic)Nausea, vomiting, tremor, agitation, tachycardia, hypokalaemia, hyperglycaemia
30–40 (Moderate Toxic)Seizures (tonic-clonic), ventricular arrhythmias, severe hypokalaemia, metabolic acidosis
>40 (Severe Toxic)Status epilepticus, VF, hypotension, rhabdomyolysis, death

Chronic Toxicity (Levels 15–25 in elderly/sensitive)

  • Insomnia, personality change, GI reflux, tremor, weight loss

Key Toxicity Risk Factors

FactorWhy
Elderly↓ Clearance, ↑ sensitivity (CNS, cardiac)
Heart Failure / Liver Disease↓ Clearance dramatically
Viral Illness (esp. children)↓ Clearance
CYP1A2 InhibitorsDrug interactions (see above)
Smoking CessationClearance ↓ 50% over 1 week → toxicity if dose not reduced
Viva Key: Theophylline toxicity = GI (nausea), CNS (tremor, seizures), Cardiac (arrhythmias), Metabolic (hypokalaemia, hyperglycaemia). Seizures at >30 mg/L often refractory to benzodiazepines (adenosine antagonism).

6️⃣ Management of Toxicity

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Theophylline Toxicity Suspected
↓
Level & Severity
↓
Hold dose Hydrate Correct K⁺/Mg²⁺ Repeat level 24h
**STOP theophylline** **Activated charcoal** (if recent, MDAC) IV fluids + KCl (hypokalaemia) **Seizures: Benzodiazepines** (may be refractory) **Arrhythmias: Standard ACLS** **NO adenosine** (antagonised) **NO beta-blockers** (worsen bronchospasm) Consider **haemodialysis/haemoperfusion** if >40 or severe
ICU **Haemodialysis/haemoperfusion** (theophylline dialysable: low protein binding, small Vd) Supportive care

Haemodialysis Indications

  • Level >40 mg/L (acute)
  • Level >30 mg/L with seizures/arrhythmias
  • Severe symptoms unresponsive to supportive care
  • Theophylline is dialysable (Vd 0.5 L/kg, 40% protein binding, low MW) — HD effective

7️⃣ Special Populations

Elderly (>65)

  • Clearance ↓ 30–50% (0.4–0.5 mL/kg/h)
  • Target 5–10 mg/L for COPD maintenance
  • Start low: 200 mg BD SR (or 300 mg OD SR)
  • Monitor: Level at 3 days, then 3-monthly; renal function; drug interactions

Heart Failure (NYHA III/IV)

  • Clearance ↓ 50–70% (0.3–0.4 mL/kg/h) — ↓ hepatic perfusion
  • IV loading dose: 2.5 mg/kg (not 5 mg/kg)
  • Maintenance: 200–300 mg/day max
  • Monitor closely: Daily levels if acute; renal function

Liver Disease (Cirrhosis)

  • Clearance ↓ 50–70% (0.3–0.4 mL/kg/h)
  • Avoid if possible; if essential → low dose, frequent levels

Pregnancy

  • Clearance ↑ (especially 3rd trimester) — may need ↑ dose
  • Category C (crosses placenta; neonatal irritability, tachycardia at high levels)
  • Monitor levels monthly; adjust dose

Smoking

  • Current smoker: Clearance ~1.5 mL/kg/h (2x non-smoker)
  • Ex-smoker (>1 week): Clearance returns to non-smoker over 1 week
  • Dose adjustment essential on starting/stopping smoking

Paediatrics

  • Children 1–9yr: Higher clearance (1–1.5 mL/kg/h) → higher mg/kg doses
  • Viral illness: Clearance ↓ dramatically → toxicity risk
  • Apnoea of prematurity: Different dosing (see neonatal formularies)

8️⃣ Practical Monitoring Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Theophylline Indicated
↓
Population?
↓
& D & E
Start 400mg BD IR or 600mg BD SR
Start 200mg BD IR or 200-300mg BD SR
↓
Check Trough at 3 days (steady state)
↓
Level
↓
& I & J
Continue
Decrease dose 25%; check K+
STOP; manage toxicity
↓
Re-check at 3 days
↓
Stable → Check level 3-monthly Or if: dose change, interaction, illness, smoking change

⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Therapeutic Range10–20 mg/L (asthma/acute); 5–10 mg/L (COPD maintenance, elderly)
SamplingTrough (pre-dose) at steady state (3 days adult; 5 days elderly/HF)
Clearance-Based DosingCl (mL/kg/h) × 15 × 24 / 1000 = mg/kg/day; Non-smoker 0.7, Smoker 1.5, Elderly/HF 0.35
Key InteractionsCYP1A2 inhibitors: Fluvoxamine (avoid), Ciprofloxacin (halve dose), macrolides, cimetidine, OCPs. Inducers: Smoking (2x dose), rifampicin, anticonvulsants.
ToxicityGI (nausea), CNS (tremor, seizures), Cardiac (SVT, VT, VF), Metabolic (hypokalaemia, hyperglycaemia)
SeizuresOften refractory to benzos (adenosine antagonism); consider propofol/barbiturates
AdenosineTheophylline antagonises adenosine — higher doses needed for SVT
Smoking CessationClearance ↓ 50% over 1 week → reduce theophylline dose by 50%
Elderly/HF/LiverClearance ↓ 50–70% → low dose, target 5–10, frequent monitoring
HaemodialysisEffective (low protein binding, small Vd) — indicated >40 or severe toxicity

🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1What is the therapeutic range for theophylline?10–20 mg/L for asthma; 5–10 mg/L for COPD maintenance/elderly.
2When should theophylline level be checked?Trough (pre-dose) at steady state (3–5 days).
3How does smoking affect theophylline clearance?Induces CYP1A2 → clearance doubles (1.5 vs 0.7 mL/kg/h) → dose needs to double.
4Patient on theophylline starts ciprofloxacin. Action?Halve theophylline dose; monitor level in 2–3 days. Ciprofloxacin ↓ clearance 30–50%.
5Patient on theophylline stops smoking. Action?Reduce theophylline dose by 50% over 1 week as clearance normalises. Monitor level.
6Theophylline toxicity — characteristic features?Nausea, tremor, tachycardia, hypokalaemia, hyperglycaemia, seizures (>30), arrhythmias.
7Why is adenosine less effective in theophylline toxicity?Theophylline is an adenosine receptor antagonist — antagonises adenosine effect.
8Theophylline in heart failure — dosing adjustment?Clearance ↓ 50–70% (0.3–0.4 mL/kg/h). Loading dose 2.5 mg/kg (not 5). Maintenance 200–300 mg/day.
9Haemodialysis for theophylline — when indicated?Level >40 mg/L, or >30 with seizures/arrhythmias, or severe symptoms unresponsive. Theophylline is dialysable.
10Elderly COPD patient — target theophylline level?5–10 mg/L (lower target for maintenance, ↓ toxicity risk).

🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"Theophylline has wide therapeutic index"NO. Narrow — toxic >20 mg/L. TDM mandatory.
"Peak level is best for monitoring"NO. Trough (pre-dose) at steady state. Peak varies with absorption.
"Smoking only affects dose slightly"NO. Smoking DOUBLES clearance → dose must double. Smoking cessation → 50% dose reduction.
"Ciprofloxacin safe with theophylline"NO. Ciprofloxacin ↓ clearance 30–50% → halve theophylline dose. Fluvoxamine ↓ 80–90% (avoid).
"Theophylline seizures respond to diazepam"Often REFRACTORY (adenosine antagonism). May need propofol, phenobarbital, anaesthesia.
"Beta-blockers safe with theophylline"Non-selective beta-blockers ↑ theophylline clearance slightly; theophylline ↓ beta-blocker effect. Avoid non-selective in asthma anyway.
"Theophylline not dialysable"YES dialysable — low protein binding (40%), small Vd (0.5 L/kg), low MW. HD effective.
Mnemonic: THEOPHYLLINE TDM
Therapeutic: 10-20 mg/L (asthma); 5-10 (COPD/elderly)
Half-life: Variable — 3-9h (adult), 2-3h (smoker), 24h+ (HF/liver/elderly)
Enzyme: CYP1A2 (major) — Inhibitors ↑ levels, Inducers ↓ levels
Oral: IR BD, SR BD; IV = aminophylline (loading 5mg/kg, 2.5mg/kg HF/elderly)
Peak NOT used — Trough (pre-dose) at steady state (3-5 days)
Hypokalaemia + Hyperglycaemia = metabolic toxicity
Young children: viral illness ↓ clearance → toxicity risk
Loading dose: No load if on oral; IV load 5mg/kg (2.5mg/kg HF/elderly)
Liver/HF/elderly: Clearance 0.35 mL/kg/h → dose ~5mg/kg/day; target 5-10
Interactions: Ciprofloxacin (halve dose), Fluvoxamine (avoid), Smoking (double dose)
Neuro: Tremor → Seizures (>30) — refractory to benzos (adenosine antagonism)
Ex-smoker: Clearance drops 50% in 1 week → reduce dose 50%
No adenosine for SVT on theophylline (antagonised)
Dialysis: Effective (low protein binding, small Vd) — >40 or severe neuro/cardiac
Monitoring: Level 3-monthly stable; after any interaction/illness/smoking change
IV aminophylline: Monitor HR, ECG, K+; extravasation → necrosis
Narrow TI — TDM mandatory for all indications
Elderly/COPD: Lower target (5-10) — less efficacy needed, more toxicity risk

🗺️ Mind Map

Theophylline TDM
Target
Asthma/Acute: 10-20
COPD/Elderly: 5-10
Sampling
Trough (pre-dose)
Steady state: 3-5 days
Clearance
Non-smoker: 0.7
Smoker: 1.5 (2x)
Elderly/HF/Liver: 0.35
Dosing
Clearance × 15 × 24 / 1000
IR BD / SR BD
Interactions
CYP1A2 Inhibitors: Fluvoxamine (avoid), Cipro (halve), Macrolides
CYP1A2 Inducers: Smoking (2x), Rifampicin, Anticonvulsants
Toxicity
GI: Nausea
CNS: Tremor → Seizures (>30)
Cardiac: Arrhythmias
Metabolic: HypoK, Hyperglycaemia
Seizures: Refractory to benzos
Special
Smoking cessation: halve dose
Pregnancy: clearance ↑
HF/Liver: low dose, target 5-10
Dialysis: effective

ReviewDateScore (0–5)Notes
Day 1
Day 3
